###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DRAFT COBOL revision

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2009-10-02T13:25:28-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ha5lhv02dfd@news1.newsguy.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<4AC23F47.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`
- **References:** `<obZrm.19835$Xg3.19307@en-nntp-04.dc1.easynews.com> <h8r72l41d46@news2.newsguy.com> <83d15920-1599-4bda-8992-1167f12fb904@h30g2000vbr.googlegroups.com> <_z9um.40309$Hw.10121@en-nntp-08.dc1.easynews.com> <psbib55bn48gfkvmkl3qte4t3sgloqv537@4ax.com> <407560d7-1998-44ae-9a5f-eb810cdd483c@w36g2000yqm.googlegroups.com> <4AC23F47.6F0F.0085.0@efirstbank.com>`

```
Frank Swarbrick wrote:
> That is certainly interesting.  What you are looking for is local variables
> that are local not only to a "program" (or a method), but are actually local
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> you came up with?

They are if you use OO methods, even if your program is all one big
static object, and you never create any other objects.

In MF COBOL, using the abbreviated .Net syntax, for example:

-----
method-id. "processOrders".
1 myOrder type "ClientOrder".
procedure division.
   perform until false
      set myOrder to new "Order" as type "ClientOrder"
      if myOrder::"Total" > 0
         ...
      else
         exit perform
      end-if
   end-perform
end method "processOrders".
-----

That's not quite what Emerson was looking for - the smallest variable
scope is the method, not the perform statement - but since the method
needn't contain anything other than the perform statement, that's not
such a difference.

Personally, I wouldn't write this method this way. I don't like hidden
loop invariants, and the if-else is structural bloat caused by the
hidden invariant. And even though I'm primarily a C programmer, I'm
just as happy that the ternary operator isn't available in COBOL,
particularly not when it's being abused the way Emerson did in his
example, ie just to avoid writing an if statement.

But the abbreviated OO syntax does make it pretty convenient to use
tightly-scoped variables.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DRAFT COBOL revision

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-10-01T10:34:19+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ii16jF31gdr9U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<obZrm.19835$Xg3.19307@en-nntp-04.dc1.easynews.com> <h8r72l41d46@news2.newsguy.com> <83d15920-1599-4bda-8992-1167f12fb904@h30g2000vbr.googlegroups.com> <_z9um.40309$Hw.10121@en-nntp-08.dc1.easynews.com> <psbib55bn48gfkvmkl3qte4t3sgloqv537@4ax.com> <407560d7-1998-44ae-9a5f-eb810cdd483c@w36g2000yqm.googlegroups.com> <a059aba9-692e-44eb-a66e-162bf8c1a48e@g22g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <30b5dadb-61fa-4ab5-994f-3c70cf5bbc82@c28g2000yqd.googlegroups.com>`

```
Emerson wrote:
> To code Cobol in Perl-style is almost a criminal offense :) I just
> want to create smaller applications in Cobol, that�s all.
…[135 more quoted lines elided]…
> You gain productivity! Is this so wrong?

I read your posts with interest, Emerson.

You say this would gain productivity, but I'm not so sure. What gain have 
you made? If your original was so clumsy and verbose, what makes you think 
that improving it by 40% now makes it acceptable? It simply makes it 40% 
less clumsy and verbose... The same solution in C# or Java would still be 
less clumsy and verbose than the new "improved" version.

Apart from not really having achieved much, for the majority of COBOL 
people, this would be an even steeper learning curve than a move to OO COBOL 
as it stands at the moment. (And most of them have not made THAT move, so it 
is unlikely you will get them to make the move you are proposing...)

You ask: "Is this so wrong?"

I think what Richard was saying (and I agree with him) is not that it's 
"wrong" but rather that it's not necessary. Richard's comment was along the 
lines of: "If you're going to write PERL in COBOL, why not write it in 
PERL?"

It is a fair comment.

The main problem with what you're doing above is that your original is too 
verbose, and your revision ISN'T COBOL. (It is (just...) OO COBOL.

I think you recognised the first problem but the attempt to address it is 
not something that would be acceptable to most of the COBOL community, even 
if you could get a major vendor to implement it, or the COBOL Standards 
people to endorse it. (We can't get this for normal standard COBOL 2002, 
never mind an esoteric version of OO COBOL on steroids... :-))

The majority of the people reading your posts in this forum simply have no 
idea what you're talking about. It is foreign to them and they don't see it 
as "COBOL".

I don't either. (even though I do understand what you're talking about)

As I read your above I found myself automatically "translating" it to C# in 
my head; I didn't see it in terms of COBOL. What I ended up with was even 
fewer lines (and far fewer words) than your revision, so if less verbosity 
is the criterion, then you'd be better off simply using C# in the first 
place.

Pushing COBOL evolution towards PERL, or Java or C# is simply pointless. It 
is like re-inventing the wheel. If you could do it without need for learning 
new concepts, then it might be worthwhile, but if you are going to have to 
learn new ("foreign") concepts anyway, then you might as well learn a 
language which supports them better than COBOL.

I guess the bottom line is that instead of clinging desperately to COBOL and 
complaining it isn't good enough (which it isn't for what you are requiring 
of it), it just makes more sense to let it go and use a language designed 
for where you are going.

Pete.
```

```
Paul wrote:
> Gads - all this seems to be an incredibly complex way to do a simple
> thing. How for instance would you just write a hello world web page
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> Or something like that.

That is (almost) available with existing OO COBOL...

move MY-NAME to "Text" of "MyName" of  textbox.

(Where "textbox" is an object reference.)

There is a shorthand form as well which I have never used but which some 
people may find easier.

I think the major drawback with Open COBOL (certainly as far as my clients 
are concerned) is that it doesn't currently support COM or OO. The PRIMA 
Migration model uses both of these features extensively. (Fujitsu and Micro 
Focus both support them.)

(See http://primacomputing.co.nz/cobol21/mig.aspx )

<snipped nothing new>

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DRAFT COBOL revision

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-10-03T02:48:47+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7imem1F31tfq0U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<obZrm.19835$Xg3.19307@en-nntp-04.dc1.easynews.com> <09164878-53a3-4d19-bf2b-e1cd7308e0dd@2g2000prl.googlegroups.com> <4ac40fc1$0$23744$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <7ikiqaF31e1hcU1@mid.individual.net> <ha4s4p$kkk$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article <7ikiqaF31e1hcU1@mid.individual.net>,
> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> Is anyone working on an equivalent of MOVE CORR yet?

As I mentioned, this is not the ONLY way to reference object properties in 
COBOL. It is one way.

It isn't quite the same as duplicating datanames then using qualification to 
reference them, or using move corresponding, as you noted.

The difference is that here the qualification is to a single object 
reference, not to duplicated fields.

In OO COBOL, qualification is used because it is already inherent in the 
language. <"Text" of "MyName" of textbox> is just a COBOL form of 
<textbox.MyName.Text>.

You can possibly see one reason why COBOL is not preferred for Object 
Oriented programming...?

>
> (note - years on back I was taught to avoid *both* of these styles for
> reasons which might best be reserved for the next thread on Style
> Wars)
>
 I have used them both without problem but it soon becomes tedious, and it 
is much easier to COPY a record definition and change the prefix so each 
field is unique (say for IN-... and OUT-...  ) than to copy the same book 
twice annd use qualification for every field you reference.

There are times when MOVE CORR can be useful (you see it frequently in date 
manipulations, although I've never used it for that myself) and I guess my 
favourite one is for reformatting data arrangements in different templates 
which have similar data, but organised differently. It is much easier to say 
MOVE CORR than to individually move 50 or 60 fields on a form or template, 
for instance.

Pete.
```

```
>twice annd use qualification for every field you reference.

Our experiences in this are similar, Mr Dashwood, but I'd say that mine is 
a bit more pragmatic.  I've used qualified datanames to maintain style in 
code or because He Who Signs My Timesheets says it is to be done; I have 
never designed anything on my own which requires them.

>
>There are times when MOVE CORR can be useful (you see it frequently in date 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>for instance.

On The Other Hand, of course... I've seen programs stop Doing What They're 
Supposed To because a field in the middle of that list of 50 or 60 has 
been renamed or had its PIC changed on one side and not on the other.

DD
```

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On Fri, 02 Oct 2009 14:18:30 -0300, Clark F Morris
> <cfmpublic@ns.sympatico.ca> wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> it, or whether it is just "It's not English enough".

I don't KNOW, but I suspect...

Faced with a need ot qualify a reference, given there is already a mechanism 
for qualifyiong things in the language (even though it was never intended 
for this particular form of qualification, I guess I'd go with what already 
existed, on the assumption that this would be familiar and comfortable to 
COBOL programmers.

Personally, I'd offer the dot notation as an extension.

Pete.
```

```
I tend to use both of those styles, though with some care of where and 
when I use 'em Doc. ;)
I especially love MOVE CORRESPONDING, as it has saved me a lot of time. 
You can get in
trouble with it of course, but then, you can get in trouble with 
anything... well at least *I* can. :)

The object syntax is referring to a specific instance of an object (be 
that a data structure or whatever).
Like using one record format to access many different copies,  by 
changing the address in a pointer variable.

-Paul
```

```
I wish compilers would include in the output listing the implied MOVE 
statements when a MOVE CORRESPONDING is used. These could be output as if 
they were comments - that would be fine. It would be a clue to what moves 
were actually generated without having to resort to examining the MOVE CORR 
... Obviously, this feature would be more useful to the programmer that came 
along 10 years later than the person who just wrote the code.
```

```
On Wed, 30 Sep 2009 22:41:45 -0500, Paul
<paul-nospamatall.raulerson@mac.com> wrote:

>> There will be no "Cobol community" as you know in ten years from now.
>
>Yes, and they said the mainframe would be dead in 1994. A silly assertation,

The variable here is "as you know".   As stable as the "CoBOL
community" has been, it isn't identical to the way it was a decade
ago.

We can accurately say there is no "London Community" as was known a
millennium ago, and certainly I am not the same person I was when I
learned CoBOL in the 1960s.   Things change.    

I haven't heard anybody propose that CoBOL will be dead in a decade
though.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DRAFT COBOL revision

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-10-02T11:07:58+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7iknhvF32b2ltU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<psbib55bn48gfkvmkl3qte4t3sgloqv537@4ax.com> <407560d7-1998-44ae-9a5f-eb810cdd483c@w36g2000yqm.googlegroups.com> <a059aba9-692e-44eb-a66e-162bf8c1a48e@g22g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <30b5dadb-61fa-4ab5-994f-3c70cf5bbc82@c28g2000yqd.googlegroups.com> <09164878-53a3-4d19-bf2b-e1cd7308e0dd@2g2000prl.googlegroups.com> <a3b9b76a-fe3b-43c8-8d34-238a6f99a7d5@g31g2000yqc.googlegroups.com> <4ac424f8$0$23761$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <f5c9c5ta5s9rrfgkpic5v9bbj3ri7k5r96@4ax.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On Wed, 30 Sep 2009 22:41:45 -0500, Paul
> <paul-nospamatall.raulerson@mac.com> wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> though.

It really depends what you mean by "dead", Howard.

Your point on change is well taken. COBOL today is nothing like the COBOL of 
the 1970s and 80's and does not enjoy the supremacy it once did. However, I 
agree that does not mean it is "dead".

I guess "dead" means different things in this context.

It is already "dead" for me inasmuch as I no longer consider it for new 
projects. However, I am spending a large amount of my time (the majority of 
it, in fact) writing C# that generates COBOL so it would be kind of stupid 
to do that for a "dead" language :-)

I do not believe it will be the "development language of choice" on most 
sites where development is still going on, by 2015. Whether that means it is 
"dead" or not (assuming I am right, even...) is arguable.

Pete.
```

```
On Wed, 30 Sep 2009 21:39:36 -0700 (PDT), riplin <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
wrote:

>Then don't use those classes for XML. Actually: don't use XML. Using
>templating means that the actual output could be JSON, HTML, XML or
>anything else without rewriting the program. If you do it right then
>the output can be changed without touching the program at all.

I've always liked the theory of templating.    In the past, I've seen
when it really wasn't worth the cost though.   I've worked where all
of our CoBOL report output went to a separate reporting system in
something very much like templating.    

Right now it seems as though things are changing fast enough to make
templating more cost effective.     But I have my doubts that it is as
effective as I had hoped for.    Templating is great when the format
changes, but the underlying data don't change.    But is it a wise to
design our processes with this assumption?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DRAFT COBOL revision

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Emerson <emersonlopes@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2009-10-01T07:53:24-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c4415de4-d122-470d-a938-e3d07077a4f1@33g2000vbe.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<psbib55bn48gfkvmkl3qte4t3sgloqv537@4ax.com> <407560d7-1998-44ae-9a5f-eb810cdd483c@w36g2000yqm.googlegroups.com> <a059aba9-692e-44eb-a66e-162bf8c1a48e@g22g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <30b5dadb-61fa-4ab5-994f-3c70cf5bbc82@c28g2000yqd.googlegroups.com> <09164878-53a3-4d19-bf2b-e1cd7308e0dd@2g2000prl.googlegroups.com> <a3b9b76a-fe3b-43c8-8d34-238a6f99a7d5@g31g2000yqc.googlegroups.com> <6e2d6c0c-859f-4469-80ce-b4786d6ed267@q40g2000prh.googlegroups.com> <ifc9c5p7fpp5drkqaghqce2g7t3m4qokb2@4ax.com>`

```
I did not say that Cobol will be dead in 10 years...I said that the
Cobol community (PEOPLE) will be different in 10 years, with a lot of
newcomers arriving from everywhere while baby boomers fade way (into
their Miami house or beating their heads against some padded cell in
some asylum...I'll be one of them for sure).

Seriously, I'm not saying that Cobol will disappear any soon from now.
I never said that in my life, au contraire (actually I think that in
many years from now there will no C# (as MS is now marketing D#/F# and
soon G#, H#...and there a plenty of letters until the end of
alphabet), and a lot of Java frameworks will be decommissioned (or
treated as legacy...take a look in Struts 1.x...it is legacy already).

So, having said that, once again, what I want some Cobol vendors
already does in their compilers (maybe not all, but a lot of things).
I just want to see some stuff standardized and improved. Cobol has the
greatest switch statement available out there...why not to have the
best external framework support too? And if it include local
variables, so let it be.

Regards, Emerson

On Oct 1, 9:48 am, Howard Brazee <how...@brazee.net> wrote:
> On Wed, 30 Sep 2009 21:39:36 -0700 (PDT), riplin <rip...@Azonic.co.nz>
> wrote:
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> - James Madison
```

```
> something very much like templating.    

What was the cost ? and how did this compare to the cost of
maintaining the program code for every minor change in layout or
data ?

> Right now it seems as though things are changing fast enough to make
> templating more cost effective.     But I have my doubts that it is as
> effective as I had hoped for.    Templating is great when the format
> changes, but the underlying data don't change.    But is it a wise to
> design our processes with this assumption?

Sure, if the data changes then the programs that deal with that data
need to be changed. In general I put more data to the templating
module interface than the (current) template uses. It can be added in
to the output if required without changing the code.

In web site/web services programs the template name is an optional
input. The default may be an html page, say, a table of orders. At the
bottom of this (in the template) is a link
&lt;a href="-url-?formname=xxorder&...&template=ordercsv"&gt;CSV file
of this%lt;/a&gt; There is no extra code in the program, it just
builds the template data exactly the same and neither knows nor cares
that a different template file is creating a csv that can open OOo
Calc (or Excel) or be saved.

Or it could be XML or JSON or a report for the printer.

I also have a 'Company' code that the user belongs to. If, in the
example above, there is a template file ordercsv.company (where
'company' is the user's company code) then this is used, otherwise it
will use ordercsv.tem. This allows the users to configure their own
templates with no program changes.
```

```
On Thu, 1 Oct 2009 13:52:04 -0700 (PDT), riplin <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
wrote:

>> I've always liked the theory of templating. ï¿½ ï¿½In the past, I've seen
>> when it really wasn't worth the cost though. ï¿½ I've worked where all
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>What was the cost ? 

The cost was basically that we needed more skills.   I'm reminded of
shops that have a bit of Report Writer.   Report Writer is a bit
quicker for those programmers who are familiar with it.    But all
CoBOL programmers can write & maintain output, it's simple even
without Report Writer.   Some aren't familiar with Report Writer,
losing its slight advantage.    Hiding the output behind a template
program just meant it was less obvious to the new programmer exactly
what was happening. 

Not a big cost - but one that needs to be balanced by an advantage,
which we didn't see back then.

>and how did this compare to the cost of
>maintaining the program code for every minor change in layout or
>data ?

I didn't see changes in layout when we did this.   I think the
templates were used more for standardization than for anything else.
Put all of the report data through a standard template and enforce
that everything looks the same.

I don't understand how changes in data impacts this discussion. Maybe
I'm missing something here.    If the program needs to process
different data differently, the program needs to be changed.   If we
produced more data, both the program and the output need to be
changed.
```

```
Then your 'reporting system' is not 'like templating' at all. My Cobol
templating module was written in Cobol (many years ago). The templates
are just the text (html,csv,xml) with tags where the data goes. Some
are built in sections, some use simple loop controls.

>   I'm reminded of
> shops that have a bit of Report Writer.   Report Writer is a bit
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> losing its slight advantage.    

I don't use Report Writer. It is just a way of writing programs. For
reports I usually don't write programs at all. I have my own script
interpreter written in Cobol. Each file has an interface module to get
the data and the report is written with a script that specifies which
file, what controls, sort order, selection, calculations, output line
data. This can write printouts, csv, html, etc or output to screen.

Most reports in a system are just scripts that are written in a few
minutes.

> Hiding the output behind a template
> program just meant it was less obvious to the new programmer exactly
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DRAFT COBOL revision

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-10-03T03:14:12+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7img5mF30uiemU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<obZrm.19835$Xg3.19307@en-nntp-04.dc1.easynews.com> <a3b9b76a-fe3b-43c8-8d34-238a6f99a7d5@g31g2000yqc.googlegroups.com> <6e2d6c0c-859f-4469-80ce-b4786d6ed267@q40g2000prh.googlegroups.com> <7iknsqF321vmiU1@mid.individual.net> <ha4sap$kkk$2@reader1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article <7iknsqF321vmiU1@mid.individual.net>,
> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
My objection here is not based on that.

I object to the idea that if something is not good, having less of it is 
less not good, or may actually be good. That sounds to me like a decidedly 
sticky wicket...

As for Elegance versus Maintainabilty, I prefer smart concise code that is 
elegant.

I can afford this, because I have no problem maintaining other people's 
COBOL, and have never had a COBOL program that I couldn't fix.  (I DID have 
an Assembler one that never did exactly what it should have, despite my best 
efforts... But that was many years ago and before I even learned COBOL in 
1967...)

All too often I've seen the inherent power and strengths of COBOL removed in 
the interests of "maintainability". I've worked on sites where they had 
really stupid numbnuts local standards like "PERFORM VARYING must not be 
used" (too hard to follow...), "ALL IFs must test TRUE and NOT is not 
permitted...", STRING and UNSTRING may be used, but NOT with POINTER...". 
There are many more...

This kind of castration is offensive to a professional programmer  (when I 
was a professional programmer) and it gets very tedious to hear the old 
"junior programmer at 2 in the morning" chestnut being trotted out in 
defence of dumbing down the programming language.

(If you are prepared to let junior programmers sort out production problems 
at 2:00 am, unsupervised, then you deserve everything you get... If you are 
not prepared to invest in properly training your programming staff so they 
are able to use the "advanced" features of the language, then you deserve 
everything you get...and if you have things in production that crash at 2:00 
am then it's time you overhauled the processes whereby code gets promoted to 
production...)

I would stress that convoluted "clever" code is NOT elegant, so the position 
I'm outlining here is not a charter for writing unmaintainable code.

Pete.
```

```
again?

>This kind of castration is offensive to a professional programmer  (when I

>"junior programmer at 2 in the morning" chestnut being trotted out in

There have been times when I've encountered the same thing and trotted out 
my copy of McCracken, saying 'I do not believe that a two-year programmer 
should be unfamiliar with constructs covered in an introductory textbook'.

(The response is, quite frequently, 'I sign your timesheets and this is 
how it is going to be done here.'  I then decide if I want to 'die' on 
that particular hill... and if I do then I find another job.)

>
>(If you are prepared to let junior programmers sort out production problems 
>at 2:00 am, unsupervised, then you deserve everything you get...

For night-time Production Support 'the beeper' (or its modern equivalent) 
gets circulated... and *everyone* was a virgin at one point or another.  
When Prod blows up the initial response I've most often seen is 'Get it 
running, NOW, and we'll deal with this later.'  The newbie gets the call, 
fixes (or think s/he fixes) the code so as to meet the batch window and 
then first thing next morning goes to a more senior programmer and 
details the incident.

>If you are 
>not prepared to invest in properly training your programming staff so they 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>production...)

See above about 'advanced' features covered in an introductory text... and 
we've had this discussion about training before: from what I have seen 
Management knows that training Costs Money and gives the recipient of the 
training the utterly foolish notion that they are worth more money because 
they then know more and can Do More Stuff... which means either an 
increase in salary or someone who moves to another shop, taking the 
training with them.  Training Costs Money, increasing salary Costs Money, 
replacing personnel Costs Money... it is a Managerial loss, all around.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DRAFT COBOL revision

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-10-02T10:58:42+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ikn0jF31valnU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<obZrm.19835$Xg3.19307@en-nntp-04.dc1.easynews.com> <h8r72l41d46@news2.newsguy.com> <83d15920-1599-4bda-8992-1167f12fb904@h30g2000vbr.googlegroups.com> <_z9um.40309$Hw.10121@en-nntp-08.dc1.easynews.com> <psbib55bn48gfkvmkl3qte4t3sgloqv537@4ax.com> <407560d7-1998-44ae-9a5f-eb810cdd483c@w36g2000yqm.googlegroups.com> <a059aba9-692e-44eb-a66e-162bf8c1a48e@g22g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <30b5dadb-61fa-4ab5-994f-3c70cf5bbc82@c28g2000yqd.googlegroups.com> <09164878-53a3-4d19-bf2b-e1cd7308e0dd@2g2000prl.googlegroups.com> <a3b9b76a-fe3b-43c8-8d34-238a6f99a7d5@g31g2000yqc.googlegroups.com>`

```
Emerson wrote:
> Wow, so many twisted mouths and no love at all.
>

You come to Usenet looking for love? I think you may be in the wrong 
forum... :-)

> Pete/Riplin
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> convert legacy code into C#, Java or anything else.

I have a number of clients (hopefully, a growing number... :-)) who 
disagree. The cost of Migration does not have to be prohibitive. My company 
is providing solutions which are well within the reach of the small to 
medium business. In fact our base Toolset costs less than a .NET COBOL 
compiler. To that we add value with advice, experience and guidance which 
results in very happy customers who act as referrals for us.

Long sighted people realise that their existing COBOL base will become 
unmaintainable in the long term. Migration is a case of "when" not "if". For 
some people (Fujitsu PowerCOBOL users for example) there is already a sword 
hanging over their heads which tends to lend some urgency to the Migration 
effort.

>A good amount of
> investment will flow into rehosting projects (Mainframe->whatever,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> stuff):

But only because you are trying to do it in COBOL.

A point you may have missed is that when code can be easily generated, the 
quantity of it really doesn't matter.

I have tools that generate very efficient OO COBOL and nobody has to sit 
down and write it. The resulting objects can run anywhere (Web or desktop) 
and can interact easily with other languages. In the future there will be 
options to generate this code in other languages besides COBOL; in the 
meantime, an object is an object and the code it is written in is immaterial 
to the applications which consume it.

Legacy COBOL is a fact of life.

The need to accommodate it is a fact of life.

To think that the status quo will continue because nobody can afford to deal 
with it is just plain wrong.

As time passes, the NEED to deal with it becomes more pressing. Eventually, 
a point is reached where you either start over from scratch (some companies 
will opt to do this) using a different approach entirely (packages, tools, 
more modern languages perhaps...), or you lever your existing legacy into a 
form where it can co-exist with new development in a new environment. (Some 
companies have a large investment in code that works generally very well. 
They have no desire to abandon this code base, but they realise that future 
maintenance of it is becoming more and more problematic. For these people 
Migration is an attractive option.)

>
> class-id. OOCobol as "OOCobol"  inherits         ClassWebService
…[88 more quoted lines elided]…
> To implement those features in C# or Java or C++ it is not the answer.

It is a BETTER answer than implementing them in COBOL...

> JNI architecture IS a mess, and make maintenance cost go up like a
> rocket.

Still cheaper than maintaining thousands of lines of hand cobbled source 
code. COBOL maintenance is labour intensive and error prone. Encapsulated 
objects are not.


>Import/export of files is a bad joke, message is NOT MADE for
> real time apps, so whenever you mix technologies (Cobol+something),
> and you don't delimit the boundaries correctly projects get
> jeopardized.

Sure, that's fair comment. But it isn't because you mixed COBOL with 
something else. It is because it isn't EASY to mix COBOL with something 
else.
>
> I'm tired to see people who never coded a single line in Cobol (not
> you, of course) saying: "Cobol cannot do this, lets do this using C +
> Java + messaging blah, blah, blah". This is stupid.

Is it stupid, or is it just uninformed?

Not everyone who doesn't use COBOL is stupid...

"To a man with a hammer, everything is a nail."

Systems should not be developed according to the toolset in place; the 
toolset should support the implementation of required functionality. Systems 
should be designed with focus on required functionality (often Methods 
arrived at through use cases) and the data needed to support that 
functionality (often Objects). You can use a paper model for your proposed 
design without ever even considering what technical tools and technology 
will be used.  I managed one project (successfully implemented) where we 
never did any program code for the first 4 months... it was all workshops 
and paper models with the Business, deliberately trying to keep tech jargon 
oput of it. Once you are satisfied you have the required objects and layers, 
THEN you can start looking at what would be best to implement it. (The 
project I referred to was implemented using Java and Ajax, but that was a 
technical choice made after functional design was complete; it did not 
influence the design in any way. If the design is built around objects and 
layers, it is not hard to do this.)

I have recently completed a web based .NET system using new tools and 
generators that support this environment. The whole thing was up and running 
(including a full relational DB) in 12 working days. There is not a single 
line of COBOL or Java in it. I did write some C# code-behinds, and modified 
some generated pages (in an area of the page where generated classes can be 
overridden so that your amendments are not discarded next time you generate 
the page). For me, this is indicative of the way things are going. It is 
silly to have people writing code that computers can write for us.

>I know a lot of
> cases where projects like this failed miserably, and even when things
> works nobody really knows why, just pray to stay working until they
> could move everything into a new environment (NOT).

Does "nobody" in the above sentence actually mean "you"?

> Cobol can and does
> design patterns, Webservices, XML, graphics and lot of nice things.
> Cobol is good for everything? NO!

It isn't what it CAN do, it is whether or not something else can do it 
better...


> But even Java has failed in GUI
> despite all investment made by Sun and its partners(I know this
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> keep your IT environment simple).

You can "keep your IT Environment simple" by using COBOL Screen Sections. 
But why would you? Separate GUI from applications with data buffers (much as 
CICS and IMS/DC do/did on mainframes) and it really doesn't matter whether 
you implement your GUI with Web Forms, Windows forms, WPF, or Ajax... It 
isn't the Tool that makes it simple, it is the design...

>
> I'm suggesting less coding in order to improve productivity, that's
> all.
>

But it is less coding for something that has already been overtaken by 
events and superseded by better tools that don't require less coding.


> There are a lot of things that I do in C#, Java, TCL, and even in
> Perl, but Cobol is my real passion, and I want to see Cobol evolving.

Good luck...   See you at the tar pit.

> This kind of resistence remind me those guys that tried to stop the
> ANS commite to release Cobol-85...This is what really hurts Cobol, not
> new (and soon dead) languages.

You are not the only one here who is passionate about COBOL.

However, it is a programming language,not life and death. Getting 
emotionally attached to computer hardware or software is about as logical as 
falling in love with the toaster or the washing machine.

The "resistence" (sic) you are seeing is not out of intransigence or even 
"anti-COBOL"; it is simply a recognition that the world has changed and the 
very reason that COBOL was invented is not a problem any longer. It isn't 
that there is "something wrong with COBOL" it is more that "there are better 
tools and solutions for today's environment". I too, took some delight in 
showing people on various installations what could be done with OO COBOL and 
it was always nice to hear them say:"I didn't know you could do that with 
COBOL".  But the fact is that that was over a decade ago. Since then it is 
no longer enough to say:"COBOL can do that...", you have to be able to say: 
"COBOL can do that BETTER than your other options..." ... and that just 
isn't the case.

By all means, push your COBOL agenda. But don't get petulant if some of us 
who have been pushing COBOL agendas for decades, have actually encountered 
what is required in terms of Standards and vendor user groups, and realised 
it is going nowhere, and have expended large amounts of time and energy 
(even money) in the "case for COBOL" are no longer waving the banner.

I like whatever works. And I REALLY like what works well and easily. For the 
Brave New World, that isn't COBOL, whether you succeed in getting the 
predefinition of data removed from it or not, whether you succeed in getting 
the code reduced by 40% or not, I'll still prefer C# when developing for 
.NET. It isn't emotion or passion; it is just sound common sense...

I make no apology for this.

Pete.
<remainder - nothing new - snipped>
```

- **References:** `<_z9um.40309$Hw.10121@en-nntp-08.dc1.easynews.com> <psbib55bn48gfkvmkl3qte4t3sgloqv537@4ax.com> <407560d7-1998-44ae-9a5f-eb810cdd483c@w36g2000yqm.googlegroups.com> <a059aba9-692e-44eb-a66e-162bf8c1a48e@g22g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <30b5dadb-61fa-4ab5-994f-3c70cf5bbc82@c28g2000yqd.googlegroups.com> <09164878-53a3-4d19-bf2b-e1cd7308e0dd@2g2000prl.googlegroups.com> <a3b9b76a-fe3b-43c8-8d34-238a6f99a7d5@g31g2000yqc.googlegroups.com> <7ikn0jF31valnU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Fri, 2 Oct 2009 10:58:42 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>Still cheaper than maintaining thousands of lines of hand cobbled source 
>code. COBOL maintenance is labour intensive and error prone. Encapsulated 
>objects are not.

The two big costs in CoBOL are:
1.   Analysis of the problem.  
2.   Extensive user testing of everything that the change touches.

Moving to encapsulated objects, the first cost doesn't change.   The
2nd cost changes considerably, as we pretty much assume it's
impossible, and write our own automated tests to test that we have
coded our assumptions correctly.
```

```
Emerson wrote:
> 
> Regarding local variables, I'm sorry, I cannot see why do I need to
> predefine everything. This make no sense, specially when it comes to
> external types (.Net/Java).

It makes a lot of sense. As Richard pointed out, requiring explicit
declaration prevents accidentally defining another variable when you
wanted to use an existing one, but mistyped it. This is a *big*
problem in languages with implicit declaration. It shows up frequently
in ECMAScript ("Javascript", etc) programming, for example.

The point of redundancy in an information encoding is to reduce the
error rate. Software has a bad enough error rate as it is. Don't wish
away useful redundancy.

> The code size is almost multiplied by 2 in
> such cases (2x ugh) (repository + working-storage).

Source code size. Big deal. Use a copybook or an editor with folding
capabilities if you don't want to read it.

And as more implementations move toward providing abbreviated syntaxes
that don't require the repository, that piece at least is going away.
I've never written a COBOL program that used the repository.
```

```
Michael,

I don't think that a copybook would be a good solution in this case. I
want to be able to understand my code without navigate in a bunch os
files to verify variables types. I do not want to depend on
intellisense or anything like that in order to do a peer-review, for
example.

Someone above said something about mess that local variable
declaration could cause citing ECMAJavascript. Javascript IS NOT A
GOOD EXAMPLE since this is a weak typed language, so a variable, could
be reassigned to any value/type at any time. I do not want this to
Cobol. This is error prone and should not be used as a valid example.

Java has local declaration and I never heard anybody complaining about
that. C# too, so why not OO Cobol? And, please, don't give me second
hand examples. I do programming in Java in a daily basis, and I could
not live without local variable declarations.

Anyways, this is an empty discussion. Micro Focus has already added
syntax to define types on the fly. At least this is what it seems to
be. Soon on later other cobol vendors will add that support too. I'm
just worried that the Cobol committe would be disconnected from
reality by ignoring latest software engineering advances that vendors
are already adding to the language (and we not even start to discuss
Generics, Closures, Dynamic Typing, Metaprogramming etc).

BTW, Pete said that Cobol maintenance is error prone. He is completely
out of his mind. One of the reasons why Cobol survived so many years
is because is very hard to make mistakes. When C appeared, a lot of
people thought that it would replace Cobol, but soon pointers
nightmare start to pop-up, and people moved away, looking for some
programming language more predictable. Interop is fine for quick and
dirt solutions, but I would not recomend this as the main
architecture. COM DLL hell is very alive in my mind (Yes, I did a lot
of VB programming in my life, too). Pete also insinuated that I was
the one with lack of knowledge in order to make a jungle of disparated
technologies to work smoothly together. Well, I cannot stop thinking
that this comment is full of bias as Pete company apparentlty sells
exactly that kind of solution. I don't think it was a fair comment, so
I'll not take in consideration.

Regards, Emerson
```

```
Emerson wrote:
> Michael,
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> not live without local variable declarations.

OO COBOL DOES support local variable declarations. You simply declare them 
in the Working-Storage or Local-Storage section of your Method definition.
>
> Anyways, this is an empty discussion. Micro Focus has already added
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> out of his mind.

Perhaps not completely... :-)

You have never experienced having amendments made to a section of code which 
then affected something in another, totally unrelated area of the code, or, 
even worse, in some downstream process where your subtle change did not 
become immediately apparent?

Lucky you...

COBOL suffers from not being encapsulated. Good programmers can make it so, 
but it is not inherent in the language. (Obviously, it is in OO COBOL...) 
Large monolithic programs comprised of many thousands of lines are certainly 
error prone to maintain, but don't take my lunatic word for it, do some 
homework (Doc may help you here...).

The major part of the cost of running ANY COBOL installation is maintaining 
the code base. On sites where they bother to keep metrics on such things 
(and I have worked on a few) this maintenance cost can be further broken 
down. You could reasonably expect that maintenance due to business 
requirements would be the main reason for doing maintenance, and usually it 
is, but I have seen sites where that was only 60% of the maintenance.  Some 
20% was caused by maintenance needed to correct errors arising out of 
maintenance. (The rest was due to technical changes in the environment and 
any "Other" reason for maintaining code.).

You don't think your site has a percentage of maintenance caused by doing 
maintenance?  It would be extremely unusual if that were so.In fact, I know 
of at least one site where they  issued contracts to get experienced COBOL 
guys to show them why their maintenance was so high and look at ways of 
improving it. (Letting junior programmers maintain production, unsupervised, 
at 2:00 am is a really good contributory factor; managers constantly 
changing their minds, inadequate specifications, unclear requirements, 
unrealistic deadlines, are all other factors that can infuence the 
statistics on maintenance coding and costs. These are problems for any site 
using any language. The fact is that where component and object based 
solutions are in place, implementing encapsulated functionality, the 
granularity is much larger and this means that errors are lesss likely and 
can be more readily spotted when they do occur.

It is fair to draw the conclusion that COBOL maintenance is error prone 
(which is what I stated). I guess what I was saying was that it is MORE 
error prone than encapsulated re-usable object or component  maintenance, 
and that may not be fair. (apples and oranges...)

>One of the reasons why Cobol survived so many years
> is because is very hard to make mistakes.

You haven't been around much, have you, Emerson? The ingenious ways in which 
COBOL can be screwed up are almost infinite... Do some contract programming 
and take short term contracts... You'll be amazed at what you see... :-)

No, the reason COBOL survived so long is because it was the only game in 
town. There simply weren't other realistic options for commercial program 
development. PL/1 and RPG both have good points, but they were never going 
to be a threat to COBOL. COBOL was the only game in town because it was 
ideally suited to the prevalent paradigm (at the time) of a powerful central 
processor with non-distributed sequential processing of tasks. Commercial 
networking was just not the norm and was the province of the "lunatic 
fringe". (Eventually the lunatics took over the asylum and the rest of us 
realized that theirs was "a FINE madness...".)

>When C appeared, a lot of
> people thought that it would replace Cobol, but soon pointers
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> technologies to work smoothly together.

OK, first off, I don't insinuate. I say what I think. There was no 
insinuation or implication that your ability was in question. None of my 
comments were intended to be personal, and, trust me, had they been, you 
would know about it.

I said the problem was that it is not easy to mix COBOL with other 
technologies. It isn't. You can do it, but it isn't easy and seamless or a 
natural flow. OO COBOL running in a .NET environment works, not because of 
the COBOL, but because objects are objects and the source they are written 
in is virtually irrelevant. Procedural COBOL does NOT integrate well with 
code in other languages. That was all I was saying. Why you would feel 
personally under attack from this escapes me; perhaps your paranoia is 
showing?  (And you reckon I'm the crazy one... Sheesh!)


>Well, I cannot stop thinking
> that this comment is full of bias as Pete company apparentlty sells
> exactly that kind of solution.

No, we are not in the business of getting your procedural COBOL to work with 
VB or Java (or anything else). We are certainly interested in helping you 
refactor it for .NET, and we are particularly adept at getting your existing 
data and code migrated to a modern environment where you may continue using 
COBOL or move off it to other languages, or employ a mixture of both, with 
the minimum of pain.


> I don't think it was a fair comment, so
> I'll not take in consideration.

But you DID take it into consideration, and you misunderstood what I said.

Never mind.

I genuinely apologize for any personal offence you may feel over my post; 
none was intended.

Pete.
```

<a id="t-c36da27e1e"></a>
## Thread: COBOL: It's Everywhere — 5 this month / 79 total

_Thread started [2009-09](2009-09.md#t-c36da27e1e)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL: It's Everywhere... but going Nowhere....

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Emerson <emersonlopes@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2009-10-01T11:13:16-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ac80b3cf-f68f-4ccb-b064-a3472b17a7e1@33g2000vbe.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<edeaf1d5-12b1-4bbb-b52d-069c49ef40ea@r33g2000vbp.googlegroups.com> <7h30oeF2pakqpU1@mid.individual.net> <h8lecv$bmk$1@reader1.panix.com> <7h6v5sF4dla6U1@mid.individual.net> <h8llab$6bj$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
Come on, Pete...admit, you miss that time when you had happiness
coding nice things that no one have seen before in Cobol. The very
first time that you created a Window in character mode, the first time
that you added mouse support to your apps, the frustrating windows API
study, being ahead of time, until you saw OO in Cobol and said, this
is great, but I do not want to code thousands of lines to get GUI
(Fujitsu here I come). I know exactly what you feel, I was a beta
tester for VISOC back in middle 90's, after being happy with MF and
realize that VISOC wasn't the answer I went to Fujitsu too, and
finally I left Cobol for ASP, Java, C# and other stuff. I also spent a
lot of time feeling angry about the laziness of Cobol's developers
everywhere (they simply seems like frogs that don't try to escape even
when the water temp raises until boiling point). Now I know that this
community is formed by people that only common attribute is being
target of prejudice everywhere, and to be honest many of them will
never understand OO, nor appreciate the elegance of design patterns,
but so what? Out of 1000 there are 1 or 2 people that understand it,
and these are the people that will make the difference.

Resistance is futile. You will be (re)assimilated, soon or later. If
not, why are you still flying around this "dead body"? ;)

Emerson


On Sep 14, 10:51 am, docdw...@panix.com () wrote:

>
…[95 more quoted lines elided]…
> - Show quoted text -
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL: It's Everywhere... but going Nowhere....

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-10-02T14:22:16+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7il2ubF31u7vaU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<edeaf1d5-12b1-4bbb-b52d-069c49ef40ea@r33g2000vbp.googlegroups.com> <7h30oeF2pakqpU1@mid.individual.net> <h8lecv$bmk$1@reader1.panix.com> <7h6v5sF4dla6U1@mid.individual.net> <h8llab$6bj$1@reader1.panix.com> <ac80b3cf-f68f-4ccb-b064-a3472b17a7e1@33g2000vbe.googlegroups.com>`

```
Emerson wrote:
> Come on, Pete...admit, you miss that time when you had happiness
> coding nice things that no one have seen before in Cobol.

I responded on this very point before I read your post... :-)


>The very
> first time that you created a Window in character mode,

That was under Windows 3.1 using Micro Focus COBOL. I used a Screen section 
and have never used it since... I can't think when it was and  the pentium 3 
laptop with it all on is packed away in storage, but I would estimate late 
eighties. I moved to Panels and Dialog very quickly. Then switched to 
Fujitsu (after the VISOC debacle) and PowerCOBOL (which was infinitely 
better).



> the first time
> that you added mouse support to your apps, the frustrating windows API
> study,

I LOVED winAPI ! (Not that I would get emotional about computer software, 
you understand... :-))  I started programming in 1965 in very low level 
assembler (on various "mini-computers" and mainframes) and spent the first 
couple of years of my career writing low-level code. WinAPI was natural for 
me.

> being ahead of time, until you saw OO in Cobol and said, this
> is great, but I do not want to code thousands of lines to get GUI
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> finally I left Cobol for ASP, Java, C# and other stuff.

I'm sure many people followed a similar path to both of us.


> I also spent a
> lot of time feeling angry about the laziness of Cobol's developers
> everywhere (they simply seems like frogs that don't try to escape even
> when the water temp raises until boiling point).

I got angry with the Standards committee when it took 17 years to produce 
COBOL 2002, and I got annoyed and frustrated with people in this group who 
seemed to pour scorn and ridicule on new ideas, but I soon got over it. I 
came to realize that people who say: "Everything I want to do I can do in 
COBOL..." simply have very limited viewpoints... I also became quite excited 
by stuff I was doing myself using OO COBOL components and started to realize 
the fantastic implications of component based systems.


>Now I know that this
> community is formed by people that only common attribute is being
> target of prejudice everywhere,

I'm not sure that's fair... I found much more interest and courtesy from 
non-COBOL people when I discussed what COBOL could do with them, than I 
found in this COBOL group when I tried to discuss new concepts and ideas. I 
think the prejudice is two way, not one way. It is also fair to say, that 
with the passage of time, we have all grown, and things are much better here 
now as people are realising from experience in their workplaces that "new 
technology" isn't going to go away and the days of COBOL being the "Only 
game in town" are long gone. I'm also more mature and (hopefully...) less 
brash than I once was so I think it all helps... :-) (It took 20 years, but 
I guess I'm a slow learner... :-))


> and to be honest many of them will
> never understand OO, nor appreciate the elegance of design patterns,
> but so what?

I agree, it doesn't matter. But the sad reality is that it doesn't matter 
because COBOL doesn't matter.


>Out of 1000 there are 1 or 2 people that understand it,
> and these are the people that will make the difference.

...or not. :-)

>
> Resistance is futile. You will be (re)assimilated, soon or later. If
> not, why are you still flying around this "dead body"? ;)

You make a good point, but I have worked with COBOL for so long I wouldn't 
just not be interested overnight. COBOL was good to me. It fed me, clothed 
me, and took me all over the planet, where I had some amazing experiences. I 
am not so shallow as to fail to give recognition to a seminal influence in 
making me what I am. For most of us, our work is a very important part of 
our lives (in fact, it takes many years for some people to realize that they 
are NOT defined by their work, and are just as "important" as people, 
whether they are working or not), and it shapes and helps to make us 
whatever we become.

I'm quite sure I would be a completely different person, if I had followed a 
completely different career. Perhaps the underlying traits and attitudes are 
there anyway, but work experience and interactions in the workplace DO have 
effects on most of us.

So, it is natural I would retain an interest in COBOL, even though I don't 
personally write much of it any more.

More importantly (for me at least) is the fact that this is an unmoderated 
Newsgroup. That makes it one of the last bastions of Free Speech on our 
planet. No vested interests (sponsors), no political affiliations, just 
people saying what they think. It never ceases to amaze me and you have no 
idea how much I appreciate this. Sometimes it is tedious and pedantic, 
sometimes it is abusive, sometimes it is just silly, but there are pearls of 
wit and wisdom that come out of comp.lang.cobol, which make me keep coming 
here. I've always said I'd be posting as long as it is fun and it is still 
fun.

Over the years sacred cows like religion and politics have been debated at 
length here; minds may not have been changed but insights have certainly 
been gained. It is a privelege to have a community of generally bright, 
thinking people, giving their views on the world. For that reason I often 
enjoy the OT posts more than the other ones, although I have tried to 
contribute to both.

A small community of people all tied together by a shared interest in COBOL.

If COBOL never did anything else, it could justify its existence by that 
alone. :-)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL: It's Everywhere... but going Nowhere....

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-10-03T03:26:19+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7imgsdF32m6b6U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<edeaf1d5-12b1-4bbb-b52d-069c49ef40ea@r33g2000vbp.googlegroups.com> <7h30oeF2pakqpU1@mid.individual.net> <h8lecv$bmk$1@reader1.panix.com> <7h6v5sF4dla6U1@mid.individual.net> <h8llab$6bj$1@reader1.panix.com> <ac80b3cf-f68f-4ccb-b064-a3472b17a7e1@33g2000vbe.googlegroups.com> <7il2ubF31u7vaU1@mid.individual.net> <7ilo7jF321useU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Robert Doerfler wrote:
>> I LOVED winAPI ! (Not that I would get emotional about computer
>> software, you understand... :-))  I started programming in 1965 in
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> (ibm/370, pdp) is more comfortable than WinAPI.

I learned and wrote Autocoder for IBM 1401s, BAL for 360, PLAN for ICL 1900, 
COMPASS for CDC Cybers, NEAT for NCR 315 (the original NEAT before it became 
NEAT 3 for Century series),  Burroughs B500 Assembler, and both Motorola 
(not much of this) and Intel 8086 Assembler (tons of it before COBOL became 
readily available for PCs). Using Macros in these facilities (when Macros 
became available) was a very good grounding for interfacing to WinAPI.  When 
you've hooked interrupt vectors and written TSRs for PCs, interfacing to 
WinAPI is a doddle. I never said WinAPI was more comfortable than the 
Assemblers, I simply observed that WinAPI was not a problem for me.

YMMV.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: (OT) - Buying advice for new PC (desktop)

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2009-10-02T14:59:56-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ha5li022dfd@news1.newsguy.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<RoqdnQtsg_aohljXnZ2dnUVZ_q-dnZ2d@earthlink.com>`
- **References:** `<6cbum.42512$Xg3.33060@en-nntp-04.dc1.easynews.com> <f9b03410-bc28-4d13-9cae-eeea85374aa4@z34g2000vbl.googlegroups.com> <RoqdnQtsg_aohljXnZ2dnUVZ_q-dnZ2d@earthlink.com>`

```
HeyBub wrote:
> foodman wrote:
>>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>> may have.

Really? I've yet to find anything that will run in 32-bit Vista but
won't run in 64-bit Vista (under WOW32).

> If she sticks with 32-bit anything, the 6 Gigs of RAM is 2 Gigs redundant.

Closer to 3 GB redundant. The 32-bit flat address space covers 4 GB,
but typically at least 1 GB of address space is lost to memory-mapped
devices. It's not unusual for video cards these days to map half a GB.

On the other hand, RAM is cheap. If I were buying a new machine, I'd
get a great whopping pile of it, run a 64-bit hypervisor like VMware
ESXi (which is free), and run whatever 32- or 64-bit VMs I liked under
it. No reason to choose between, say, Vista Ultimate 64-bit and one of
the 64-bit Linux distros; run 'em both. (I'd actually likely be
running VMs for Vista, Server 2008, and OpenSUSE, at least, but that's
just me.)
```

## Thread: Urgent opening for Mainframe Programmer in Sacramento — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Urgent opening for Mainframe Programmer in Sacramento

- **From:** Sam <scsheth@gmail.com>
- **Date:** 2009-10-01T22:07:28-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<24b821cc-d957-4aab-b121-c9e29d6a26f8@g1g2000pra.googlegroups.com>`

```
Please send your resumes to scsheth@gmail.com

PROJECT SCOPE/TASKS

The scope of this project encompasses the following tasks:

Under the direction of the manager, the Contractor will:
•	Analyze service requests and problem reports and prepare impact
analysis and estimates of schedule and effort.
•	Analyze, design, code, test, and install into production approved
modifications to existing applications.
•	Assist as directed in the review of design, code and documentation
of client’ developers.
•	Create or modify development and testing environments.
•	Facilitate JAD sessions and conduct design and code walk-thrus.
•	Prepare documents in accordance with SDLC and client’/IEEE
standards, including but not limited to functional requirements
documents, technical design specifications documents, test plans,
system and program documentation.
•	Prepare written status reports and participate in project meetings
as required.
•	Provide knowledge transfer to technical and program area staff as
required.

PERSONNEL QUALIFICATIONS

Mandatory Qualifications
•	Senior level proficiency in COBOL programming and VSAM.
•	Senior level proficiency in COMPAREX, File Aid, CICS, TSO, JES3,
JCL, ISPF, XPEDITER, FTP and Attachmate Extra/3270 emulation software
and tools on IBM Mainframe with z/OS.
•	Senior level proficiency in project management, SDLC, IEEE, JAD
facilitation, and conduct of design and code walk-thrus.
•	Senior level proficiency in analyzing, designing and implementing
successful system solutions to business problems in either a
development or maintenance environment.
•	Senior level proficiency in working cooperatively with business and
IT professionals, gaining the trust of team members and customers, and
communicating clearly and effectively both verbally and in writing.
•	Consistent record of total customer satisfaction with work products
and work performance.
“Senior” level is defined as advanced proficiency sustained over a
minimum of three years of the last five years of professional work
experience.

Desirable Qualifications
Additional consideration will be given to contract personnel with the
following qualifications:
•	Working Knowledge of, Visio, IBM Utilities, AFP, DMS/FileNet, CA-
View, EJES, Primavera Team Play and Team Player.
•	Working knowledge of VSAM database access routines.
•	Experience with contribution reporting and retirement benefit
systems.
```

## Thread: [OT] JCL/Utility to List Datasets by High-Order Node? — 20 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### [OT] JCL/Utility to List Datasets by High-Order Node?

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2009-10-02T13:28:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ha4v4v$rtp$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```

All righty... I know that certain IBM mainframe ISPF functions can be 
invoked in batch programs; Search For (option 3.14) and Compare (option 
3.12) have a screen option for Online or Batch, the latter generating JCL 
to invoke program ISRSUPC with appropriate parameters and control-cards.

Is anyone aware of where I might find a similar function for the Data Set 
List Utility (option 3.4)?  All I know now is how to do it manually:

Invoke the Utility from the command line.
Type HINODE.* on the Dsname Level line and whack Enter.
Type SAVE on the command line... and the results will be sent to something 
like MYUID.SYSn.SPFm.LIST which I can then use for other manipulations.

There are, most likely, CLIST or REXX solutions to this as well; given the 
level of sophistication in my present environment I believe it would be 
best to use plain JCL.  If that cannot be done then one of these other 
methods might be used but first choice is Use What They're Familiar With.

Thanks much!

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: [OT] JCL/Utility to List Datasets by High-Order Node?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2009-10-02T15:02:37-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d1txm.86916$Wo3.30124@en-nntp-04.dc1.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<ha4v4v$rtp$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
I am not clear from your question if you are looking for a "JCL only - possibly 
with IBM utility" or if you are trying to find a "batch" solution.  If the 
latter, check out the ISPF service documented at;
  http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/ISPZSG80/2.27

You can call that service from COBOL or many other "languages" (including - I 
think REXX).

If you are looking for a "utility only" solution, then I think (but haven't 
checked it) that IDCAMS will support this.
```

```
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>checked it) that IDCAMS will support this.

They have made some improvements to IDCAMS in z/OS 1.10 or 1.11 that
support this in a non-intuitive manner and have problems if the user
catalog isn't known.  Fixes are being worked on.  See the archives of
bit.listserv.ibm-main or go to the ibm-main archives at bama.ua.edu.
You probably will need to register.
```

```
Actually... woke up this morning with some manner of amphibian lodged in 
my larynx and In These Days of Influenza-Transmission I sent off an email 
to a tech-lead (with cc:'s to two corner-office holders) saying 'Y'know... 
if someone else told me that they felt - and sounded - as I do I'd respond 
'Stay home and do the Big Three: bedrest, force fluids and aspirin.'

I then thought 'Wait a minute... I can remote log-in (even though they 
don't like it except for emergencies), I have email and a cellular 
telephone... why am I so unworthy of doing what I'd tell anyone else to do 
under these circumstances?'

So... an extra pot of coffee, a 16oz glass of half orange/half grapefruit 
juice, a curious combination of drugs and - just to be sure - a dollop of 
Nin Jiom Pei Pa Koa to soothe the frazzled throat.

E'en in illness... Life is Good, aye.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: JCL/Utility to List Datasets by High-Order Node?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2009-10-07T17:10:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<haii2i$bbf$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<ha4v4v$rtp$1@reader1.panix.com> <haaovp$qpe$1@reader1.panix.com> <mOOdnSq1pfGry1TXnZ2dnUVZ_qGdnZ2d@earthlink.com> <hacpdg$msu$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```

All righty... here's the bird's-eye low-down on this caper... whatever 
that means (Firesign Theatre).

Mr Klein suggested 
http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/ISPZSG80/2.27 
and this details use of the LMDDISP data set list service.  I coded an 
IKJEFT01 job with ISPEXEC LMDDISP LISTID('MYUID') VIEW(VOLUME) 
CONFIRM(YES) and got a Condition Code of 20 (according to 
<http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/ISPZSG80/2.27.4?SHELF=&DT=20090611002745&CASE=> 
this means 'A severe error occurred while processing the data set list'.

Another suggestion was to use a LISTCAT under IDCAMS... this both worked 
and did not work.  LISTCAT ENTRIES(MYUID.*) ALL gave me all the datasets 
with MYUID as the high-order node and only one lower level node; in other 
words it would return

MYUID.NODE1
MYUID.NODE3

... and *not* return MYUID.NODE2.NODE2.  Changing to MYUID.*.* or MYUID* 
both got me a Condition Code of 12 and error IDC3203I.

Mr Maclean offered 
http://www.tsotimes.com/articles/archive/summerfall98/ispf.html which lead 
to an article on how to invoke the ISPF Edit macro on a dataset which 
contained what I already wanted ('For example you could run a listcat job 
in batch for all datasets with the high level qualifier of "TEST". Have 
the output of this job go to a dataset which is then cataloged.')... but, 
as noted above, I could get LISTCAT to display only the two-noded 
datasets.

So close... and yet so far.  I recall using ADRDSSU in the wild-card 
manner I need... but not to list dataset names, just to delete them.  Call 
me lazy but I don't want to kill the datasets and then have to restore 
them just to get a listing.

Thanks to all for the help, though.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: JCL/Utility to List Datasets by High-Order Node?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2009-10-08T21:51:40-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7f-dnbqsXZmxClPXnZ2dnUVZ_jadnZ2d@earthlink.com>`
- **References:** `<ha4v4v$rtp$1@reader1.panix.com> <haaovp$qpe$1@reader1.panix.com> <mOOdnSq1pfGry1TXnZ2dnUVZ_qGdnZ2d@earthlink.com> <hacpdg$msu$1@reader1.panix.com> <haii2i$bbf$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:haii2i$bbf$1@reader1.panix.com...
>
> All righty... here's the bird's-eye low-down on this caper... whatever
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I think this does what you want:

//JOBNAME JOB (ACCT,'ROOM'),HOTTEL,CLASS=A,MSGCLASS=S
//PROCLIB JCLLIB ORDER=USERID.DVL.PROC
//        SET TITLE1='PFX.LISTCAT'
//OUTPUT INCLUDE MEMBER=DESTCH
//*-------------------------------------------------------------------*
//*        IDCAMS                                                     *
//*-------------------------------------------------------------------*
//STEP1    EXEC PGM=IDCAMS
//SYSPRINT DD SYSOUT=*
//SYSIN    DD *
  LISTCAT  LEVEL(USERID) -
           NAME

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: JCL/Utility to List Datasets by High-Order Node?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Clark F Morris <cfmpublic@ns.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2009-10-09T12:46:22-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rfluc59rc7e7j4mqe59ariaace5bd2a22o@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<ha4v4v$rtp$1@reader1.panix.com> <hacpdg$msu$1@reader1.panix.com> <haii2i$bbf$1@reader1.panix.com> <7f-dnbqsXZmxClPXnZ2dnUVZ_jadnZ2d@earthlink.com> <han1io$d0p$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
On Fri, 9 Oct 2009 09:59:52 +0000 (UTC), docdwarf@panix.com () wrote:

>In article <7f-dnbqsXZmxClPXnZ2dnUVZ_jadnZ2d@earthlink.com>,
>Charles Hottel <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote:
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>but it provides much good use.

If you add the keyword VOL to the statement you will get the Volume
Serial number and device type.  The catalog only has space allocation
for VSAM data set so you would need another utility.  If you are into
Assembler, there are several utilities available at www.cbttape.org.
If your site has FDR/ABR from innovation, it has FDRREPORT which
allows you the ability to get all of the information you want nicely
formatted and only the information you want.  This is an outstanding
utility.  For full information on this google IBM IDCAMS LISTCAT and
go to the first entry, then pick the manual DSSMS Access Method
Services For Catalogs and use LISTCAT.  The URL
http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/infocenter/zos/v1r9/topic/com.ibm.zos.r9.idai200/dgt2i251.htm
probably will get you to the exact entry.  For a system that is
supposedly Closed, you can get a wealth of information online
including every generally available manual (all of the COBOL manuals
and enough information to probably write your own 3270 emulator).  If
your shop has an IBM software product, you can view the manuals for it
anywhere in the world.
>
>Thanks much!
>
>DD
```

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2009-10-09T14:03:58-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dNadnWmJ1r-W5lLXnZ2dnUVZ_g6dnZ2d@earthlink.com>`
- **References:** `<ha4v4v$rtp$1@reader1.panix.com> <hacpdg$msu$1@reader1.panix.com> <haii2i$bbf$1@reader1.panix.com> <7f-dnbqsXZmxClPXnZ2dnUVZ_jadnZ2d@earthlink.com> <han1io$d0p$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:han1io$d0p$1@reader1.panix.com...
> In article <7f-dnbqsXZmxClPXnZ2dnUVZ_jadnZ2d@earthlink.com>,
> Charles Hottel <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote:
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Here is a program, COBTSO, from Gilbert St. Flour that can issue a TSO/E 
command.  You might be able to modify it into what you want.

//JOBNAME  JOB (ACCT,'ROOM'),'HOTTEL COBTSO',MSGCLASS=S,CLASS=K
//PROCLIB JCLLIB ORDER=USERID.DVL.PROC
//        SET TITLE1='PFX.COB00007'
//OUTPUT INCLUDE MEMBER=DESTCH
//*-------------------------------------------------------------------*
//*        COBOL II COMPILE                                           *
//*-------------------------------------------------------------------*
//STEP01   EXEC  COB3CLG,LIBRARY='DVL.SOURCE.LIBMAST',PROG=IEFBR14,
//    OPT=DYNAM,LOPT='AMODE=31,RMODE=ANY'
//COMP.SYSIN DD  *
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
         PROGRAM-ID. COBTSO.
      ******************************************************************
      *                                                                *
      *   MODULE NAME = COBTSO                                         *
      *                                                                *
      *   DESCRIPTIVE NAME = ISSUE TSO COMMANDS FROM A COBOL PROGRAM.  *
      *                                                                *
      *   FUNCTION = THIS SAMPLE PROGRAM DEMONSTRATES HOW TO INVOKE    *
      *              TSO COMMANDS FROM A COBOL PROGRAM USING           *
      *              STANDARD TSO SERVICES AS DOCUMENTED IN THE        *
      *              TSO/E PROGRAMMING SERVICES MANUAL.                *
      *                                                                *
      *              MOST TSO COMMANDS, INCLUDING CLISTS AND REXX      *
      *              EXECS CAN BE EXECUTED USING THIS TECHNIQUE.       *
      *              TSO COMMANDS WHICH REQUIRE AUTHORIZATION          *
      *              (SUCH AS OUTPUT, SEND, TRANSMIT AND RECEIVE)      *
      *              WILL NOT WORK.                                    *
      *                                                                *
      *   AUTHOR   =  GILBERT SAINT-FLOUR <GSF@POBOX.COM>              *
      *                                                                *
      ******************************************************************
      /
       DATA DIVISION.
        WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
         01 FILLER.
           05 WS-DUMMY        PIC S9(8) COMP.
           05 WS-RETURN-CODE  PIC S9(8) COMP.
           05 WS-REASON-CODE  PIC S9(8) COMP.
           05 WS-INFO-CODE    PIC S9(8) COMP.
           05 WS-CPPL-ADDRESS PIC S9(8) COMP.
           05 WS-FLAGS        PIC X(4) VALUE X'00010001'.
           05 WS-BUFFER       PIC X(256).
           05 WS-LENGTH       PIC S9(8) COMP VALUE 256.
      /
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
      *----------------------------------------------------------------*
      *          CALL IKJTSOEV TO CREATE THE TSO/E ENVIRONMENT         *
      *----------------------------------------------------------------*
           CALL 'IKJTSOEV' USING WS-DUMMY
                                 WS-RETURN-CODE
                                 WS-REASON-CODE
                                 WS-INFO-CODE
                                 WS-CPPL-ADDRESS.
           IF WS-RETURN-CODE > ZERO
             DISPLAY 'IKJTSOEV FAILED, RETURN-CODE=' WS-RETURN-CODE
                                     ' REASON-CODE=' WS-REASON-CODE
                                     'INFO-CODE='    WS-INFO-CODE
             MOVE WS-RETURN-CODE TO RETURN-CODE
             STOP RUN.
      *----------------------------------------------------------------*
      *          BUILD THE TSO/E COMMAND IN WS-BUFFER                  *
      *----------------------------------------------------------------*

           MOVE 'ALLOCATE DD(SYSPUNCH) SYSOUT HOLD' TO WS-BUFFER.

      *----------------------------------------------------------------*
      *   CALL THE TSO/E SERVICE ROUTINE TO EXECUTE THE TSO/E COMMAND  *
      *----------------------------------------------------------------*
           CALL 'IKJEFTSR' USING WS-FLAGS
                                 WS-BUFFER
                                 WS-LENGTH
                                 WS-RETURN-CODE
                                 WS-REASON-CODE
                                 WS-DUMMY.
           IF WS-RETURN-CODE > ZERO
             DISPLAY 'IKJEFTSR FAILED, RETURN-CODE=' WS-RETURN-CODE
                                     ' REASON-CODE=' WS-REASON-CODE
             MOVE WS-RETURN-CODE TO RETURN-CODE
             STOP RUN.

      *----------------------------------------------------------------*
      *          CHECK THAT THE ALLOCATE COMMAND WORKED                *
      *----------------------------------------------------------------*
           DISPLAY 'ALLOCATE WORKED ! ' UPON SYSPUNCH.

           STOP RUN.
//LKED.SYSLIB  DD
//             DD
//             DD DSN=XXXX.YYY.DVL.BATLOAD,DISP=SHR
//LKED.SYSLMOD DD DSN=&&LOADLIB(COBTSO),DISP=(OLD,PASS)
//LKED.SYSIN DD *
  NAME COBTSO(R)
//GO.SYSUDUMP DD SYSOUT=*
//GO.SYSPRINT DD SYSOUT=*
//GO.SYSOUT   DD SYSOUT=*
//GO.SYSDBOUT DD SYSOUT=*

```

---

<a id="t-3cc8603b16"></a>
## Thread: Period for qualification — 71 msgs

### Period for qualification

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2009-10-02T18:38:10-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<F9wxm.45765$no1.26324@en-nntp-08.dc1.easynews.com>`

```
I don't remember all the details, but I can think of at least one problem with 
using a period for qualification.

First, I want to preface this with the fact that allowing
  A.B
as equivalent to
   B of A

was first proposed BEFORE OO came along.

  * * * * *

At least one problem with "A.B" has to do with "text words" and COPY/REPLACING 
or REPLACE processing.  Currently, a period (full stop) is ONLY a separator when 
it is followed immediately by a space.  (There are implicit spaces at the end of 
lines of text and end of source code).

This means that
  99.99
in a picture string is a single text word.  If a period was allowed to indicate 
qualification, then EITHER
 A.B
would be treated as a single text word
   or
99.99
would be come two text words with a separator.

(Remember that during "text manipulation" the compiler doesn't know what 
division one is in or any other "structural" information)

  * * * *

There may well have been other reasons why this was rejected, but this one (by 
itself) seems pretty serious to me.
```

#### ↳ Re: Period for qualification

- **From:** Clark F Morris <cfmpublic@ns.sympatico.ca>

```

<wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>I don't remember all the details, but I can think of at least one problem with 
>using a period for qualification.
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>99.99

While I would prefer using a period, any other separator character
would be and would have been acceptable.  If all numeric paragraph
numbers are still legal (did anyone ever use them?), that would have
been another problem.  (99 and 999 could be paragraph and section
names) 

>would be come two text words with a separator.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>itself) seems pretty serious to me.
```

#### ↳ Re: Period for qualification

- **From:** Paul <paul-nospamatall.raulerson@mac.com>

```
Bill, can you help me understand why COBOL would have trouble with this 
when everything else in the world does not?

By the time the compiler is evaluating these tokens, it should know 
what Division and Section it is in, and also
what kind of statement it is processing.

I mean, if compiler vendors can evaluate   B of A in the context of a 
MOVE statement or something, A.B should not be any more
difficult. I could see where some restrictions on variable names would 
come into play though.

-Paul


On 2009-10-02 18:38:10 -0500, "William M. Klein" 
<wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> said:

> using a period for qualification.
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> itself) seems pretty serious to me.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Period for qualification

- **From:** "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net>
- **Date:** 2009-10-03T22:32:37-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vHUxm.124864$Y83.88661@newsfe21.iad>`
- **References:** `<F9wxm.45765$no1.26324@en-nntp-08.dc1.easynews.com> <4ac7fc71$0$23762$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net>`

```
That isn't the point.  Various languages use different symbols for
qualification or concatenation or whatever.  Whenever the language is
defined the meaning of symbols from the character set is fixed.  I wouldn't
go so far as to say that ANY arbitrary meaning of a symbol could be
absolutely determined under all circumstances but most of the time it could.
But why bother?  A OF B is just as easy to use and understand as B.A - in
fact it is completely expicit so you wouldn't have to remember the correct
sequence of the variable names.  Remember that one of the original goals of
Cobol was to make the language easy to read (if not self-documenting):
therefore A OF B is preferable in this context.

Quite a few suggested changes to the Cobol language have come from people
used to other languages who prefer to retain their accustomed syntax.

But that is in fact an outlook that will cause a person trouble in using a
new language: if you can't revise your concept you will spin your wheels
until you switch.  E.g.: OO programming.

PL

Paul <paul-nospamatall.raulerson@mac.com> wrote in message
```

```
I would not say that B of A is preferable to A.B, and the reverse
is even worse (A of B or B.A). Then again, one of the issues I
sometimes have with COBOL is that it is wordy when conciseness
is more appropriate.

   Retirement_Date.year

   year of Retirement-Date.

The first line is "read" aloud exactly like the Cobol phrase.
In general, simple is better than complex. Concise is better than
verbose.  Smaller is better than larger.
```

```
On 2009-10-08 13:43:08 -0500, riplin <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> said:

> On Oct 8, 6:20ï¿½pm, Paul <paul-nospamatall.rauler...@mac.com> wrote:
>> On 2009-10-06 00:46:38 -0500, riplin <rip...@Azonic.co.nz> said:
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
> was not caused by the designers of Cobol.

(*sigh*)  They were not. They were discussing the most basic
elements of 'structured programming' - as in things like
how you code something like a subroutine.

All this had a direct, and very significant impact on the design
and implementation of COBOL.

Just in case you are not aware of it, Jackson did not publish his
ideas until the mid 1970s' or thereabouts. The basic structure of
COBOL would have been designed almost two decades before
then.

I did not jump around, you simply assumed you knew what I was
talking about.
```

```
but 'structured programming' is a specific form that was developed
much later and was the subject of Dijkstra in 1969.

> All this had a direct, and very significant impact on the design

structured, but 'structured programming' was 10 years after Cobol was
first implemented.
```
