- **References:** `<F9wxm.45765$no1.26324@en-nntp-08.dc1.easynews.com> <4ac7fc71$0$23762$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <vHUxm.124864$Y83.88661@newsfe21.iad> <4ac8f88e$0$23771$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <gp4kc5dqaq9qstjms7fumhgca99eva5mkj@4ax.com> <4acaccd1$0$23769$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <1fec4c0a-2d06-4e77-acb0-179d34cd0b9d@x6g2000prc.googlegroups.com> <4acd7681$0$23753$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <1bf2800f-5741-449d-bee9-1ffe16e34932@i12g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <4aced01f$0$23740$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <b925676c-eedd-42b7-acf8-5a3690da95af@f18g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`

```
Bill, your history in the front of the FAQ is very good, but I 
obviously feel it is not complete in some areas.
This is a hobby horse with me, as one of my favorite hobies is 
researching and documenting the history of
and evolution of computer languages.

There is an incredible amount of fascinating facts surrounding the 
early years of the development of
our industry, and especially around the COBOL, Fortran, Algol, and 
(much later!) C language trees.

I take this offline by saying that I do disagree with your history in 
some respects, but mostly my disagreement
revolves around the major impacts the basic developments, much earlier 
than the 1970's, had.
Starting at the idea of stored program code and progressing upward 
though the idea of using structure to control
the flow of program execution is a much more important chain of events 
than is usually credited.
We take all of that from granted today.

The impacts most of us remember, from events in the 1970's, all had 
their roots in the development of those
ideas by Aiken, Hopper, Mauchly, Eckert,  and more a in earlier 
decades. Especially the ideas that came out of
Burroughs. :)

It's really fascinating. :)

-Paul
```

On Oct 11, 10:43 am, Paul <paul-nospamatall.rauler...@mac.com> wrote:
> Bill, your history in the front of the FAQ is very good, but I
> obviously feel it is not complete in some areas.
> This is a hobby horse with me, as one of my favorite hobies is
> researching and documenting the history of
> and evolution of computer languages.

But you seem to be so bad at it.

May I recommend "A Comparative Study of Programming Languages" by
Bryan Higman. or perhaps "Programming Languages" edited by F. Genuys.
as a starter.

> There is an incredible amount of fascinating facts surrounding the
> early years of the development of
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> We take all of that from granted today.

"Early British Computers" has description and sample code for
programming machines with mercury delay lines. A hint: the layout of
the code in memory has no relationship with the execution sequence.

You may want to look for NCC's "Program Design Methods" by
G.B.Bleazard.


> The impacts most of us remember, from events in the 1970's, all had
> their roots in the development of those
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> -Paul

- **From:** Paul <paul-nospamatall.raulerson@mac.com>
- **Date:** 2009-10-08T00:27:09-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4acd782d$0$23775$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net>`
- **References:** `<F9wxm.45765$no1.26324@en-nntp-08.dc1.easynews.com> <4ac7fc71$0$23762$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <vHUxm.124864$Y83.88661@newsfe21.iad> <4ac8f88e$0$23771$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <gp4kc5dqaq9qstjms7fumhgca99eva5mkj@4ax.com> <4acaccd1$0$23769$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <tckmc51ssbvd781ili7k2t4vpq0iogk76f@4ax.com>`

```
On 2009-10-06 09:21:59 -0500, Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> said:

> On Mon, 5 Oct 2009 23:51:30 -0500, Paul
> <paul-nospamatall.raulerson@mac.com> wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> programming structured debate.

You kind of have to realize that the first generation of modern 
programmers were
all mathematicians, and tended to express all their solutions with an 
efficiency and
conciseness that would be considered stifling by anyone today.  <grin>

The idea of COBOL, a language that was *English* like, and bore a syntax
similar to *English*, and took up just massive amounts of core memory and
tape storage to run, was hard to deal with back then.

Remember, when COBOL was conceived, the idea of subroutines and stored program
architectures was *less than a decade old.* Talk about monolithic programs...

It was hotly debated, and I consider it a miracle that COBOL, and Fortran, even
were developed at all, much less became as willdy popular as they did.

Both languages are still quite good at what they do, because they are
both easy to understand, and efficient to degree unmatched by modern
languages.

If you don't believe that, decompose a program like Word, and measure
how much computing power it takes. I did, and was surprised to learn
that it takes about s much computing power as an IFL on an older z800
just to run the GUI for Windows.  That same horsepower, easily can
run thousands of users with COBOL programs and CICS.

-Paul
```

On Oct 8, 6:27 pm, Paul <paul-nospamatall.rauler...@mac.com> wrote:
> On 2009-10-06 09:21:59 -0500, Howard Brazee <how...@brazee.net> said:
>
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> all mathematicians,

No. Wrong. Of course this may depend on what you mean by 'modern
programmers' is this to distinguish them from 'programmers in ancient
Greece'.

Many programmers of _business_ systems came from a unit record
(tabulator and card based systems) background or from accountancy.

Your arguments fail when you claim 'all'. It tends to show a
shallowness of knowledge.

> and tended to express all their solutions with an
> efficiency and
> conciseness that would be considered stifling by anyone today.  <grin>
>

"efficiency and conciseness" were forced on them by lack of resources.

> The idea of COBOL, a language that was *English* like, and bore a syntax
> similar to *English*, and took up just massive amounts of core memory and
> tape storage to run, was hard to deal with back then.

Cobol did and does not take "massive amounts of core memory and tape
storage "to run". The compiler may take 'massive amounts'. But "to
run" only takes that required by the generated code.

That is, the size of the source code words (verbs and variables) has
no effect on the program size when run.

> Remember, when COBOL was conceived, the idea of subroutines and stored program
> architectures was *less than a decade old.* Talk about monolithic programs...
>

Cobol '61 was a language of its time. What is your point here ?

> It was hotly debated, and I consider it a miracle that COBOL, and Fortran, even
> were developed at all, much less became as willdy popular as they did.
>

Prior to Cobol there were many similar languages, each developed by
and ran on, particular manufacturers. You appear to completely miss
the point of the 'Common' in COBOL. It took features of several of the
languages and put these together in a way that would replace the
precursors.

> Both languages are still quite good at what they do, because they are
> both easy to understand, and efficient to degree unmatched by modern
> languages.
>

> If you don't believe that, decompose a program like Word, and measure
> how much computing power it takes. I did, and was surprised to learn
> that it takes about s much computing power as an IFL on an older z800
> just to run the GUI for Windows.  That same horsepower, easily can
> run thousands of users with COBOL programs and CICS.

That has nothing to do Cobol or the language that Word was written
in.
```

> On Fri, 9 Oct 2009 01:07:13 -0500, Paul
> <paul-nospamatall.raulerson@mac.com> wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> data portions).  In many ways it was more sophisticated than COBOL.

FlowMatic (Or Business Language Zero) is a direct predecessor to COBOL. 
Written by
Grace Hopper at Remington Rand.  I do not include it as a "similar" 
language because
it *is* a direct predecessor.  COMTRAN, is similar, but more related to 
Fortran,
and in some ways a predecessor of Algol, which is a very different 
train of thought
from COBOL.  I do not know much of anything about FACT; if your have 
some references
you would be willing to share, I would be much obliged.

On the other hand, I am not that qualifies as a "lot of languages" 
similar to COBOL.

-Paul

- **References:** `<F9wxm.45765$no1.26324@en-nntp-08.dc1.easynews.com> <4ac7fc71$0$23762$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <vHUxm.124864$Y83.88661@newsfe21.iad> <4ac8f88e$0$23771$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <gp4kc5dqaq9qstjms7fumhgca99eva5mkj@4ax.com> <4acaccd1$0$23769$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <tckmc51ssbvd781ili7k2t4vpq0iogk76f@4ax.com> <4acd782d$0$23775$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <cd6e2da4-90d4-4f47-bfba-6671eaf4d862@y10g2000prg.googlegroups.com> <4aced311$0$23784$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <dqkuc5d5pj74f4a5468kicvagufjrkqt86@4ax.com> <4ad0fd52$0$23752$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net>`

On Oct 11, 10:32 am, Paul <paul-nospamatall.rauler...@mac.com> wrote:
> On 2009-10-09 10:19:30 -0500, Clark F Morris <cfmpub...@ns.sympatico.ca> said:
>
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
> similar to COBOL.

When you put something in quotation marks it is supposed to be an
actual quote that you are arguing against and not something that you
made up in order to produce a 'straw man argument'.

I had said that there were "many" and had listed a handfull which does
count as "many".

Flow-Matic is _one_ of the languages that led to Cobol.

Here is an example of NCR's Language H.

http://docs.google.com/gview?a=v&q=cache:Fc8fsVxEBnoJ:www.thecorememory.com/LanguageHEx

COMTRAN is _not_ a predecessor of Algol, nor has any relationship to
it. Algol was 'mid 50s' and COMTRAN was later in 1957. COMTRAN brought
the PICTURE clause to Cobol. Sample at

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/COMTRAN

Your "COMTRAN, is similar, but more related to Fortran," is also
nonsense. It is said to be "the business programming equivalent of ...
FORTRAN" but is otherwise nothing like FORTRAN (or Algol).

FACT added several items to COBOL, see:

http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/FACT_computer_language

Sample available there in the FACT spec.
```

massive to think about) requires a run-time library.

On Oct 11, 10:33 am, Paul <paul-nospamatall.rauler...@mac.com> wrote:

'argumentitive', but perhaps others see what you say as
'misinformation'.

> >>>  But "to
> >>> run" only takes that required by the generated code.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The ""to run" only takes that required by the generated code." was to
counter your conflating of the 'english like' with 'run size' where
what you had written implied that the run size was large as a result
of Cobol's wordiness.

Whether COBOL requires a separate 'run-time' or not is entirely an
_implentation_ issue. For example with Microfocus 3.4 (for DOS) I
could compile to .int (intermediate code) and have this executed by
the run-time, or I could specify that the compiler output generated
and optimised code and build everything required into a single .exe
(or some other ways too).

Cobol on the ICL 1900 series always built each program as a single
executable that could be run without any 'run time' or anything other
that the OS (which included the loader).

…[21 more quoted lines elided]…

COBOL was a 'Common' language, it took features from existing single-
manufacturer languages and amalgamated these into one common language.
See the frontispiece of any Cobol manual. In particular it took parts
of Flow-Matic, FACT, and Commercial Translator.

Others were also working on similar commercial languages, such as ACL
(Atlas Commercial Language), NCR's Language H, and ICT's Nebula.

You idea that other languages of the time were restricted to being
'primitive assembler' is quite wrong. Both FORTRAN and Algol were well
established long before Cobol.


> >> Both languages are still quite good at what they do, because they are

…[11 more quoted lines elided]…

take all that computing power.

If the CICS programs were written in C they would still run thousands
of users.

The dissent was just about all centred around the incorrect idea that
"structured = no GOTO's".    Presented baldly like that it was unacceptable.
But it was always thus - catchy phrases instead of proper explanations.

It's worth noting, too, that several authors demonstrated that if structured
precepts were strictly and rigourously implemented, then the resulting
program would be four or five times longer than the "unstructured" version.
(I would like very much to be able to cite a reference but I don't have the
books any more, dammit).  And you can get arguments to this day over whether
structured is any clearer than non-structured.  (I'm not taking either
position, just pointing out the situation, so, please, no flames).

Whether or not "structured" supports your statements about conciseness I'm
not at all sure!

PL

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pABzm.3125$Xw3.1440@en-nntp-04.dc1.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<F9wxm.45765$no1.26324@en-nntp-08.dc1.easynews.com> <4ac7fc71$0$23762$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <vHUxm.124864$Y83.88661@newsfe21.iad> <4ac8f88e$0$23771$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <gp4kc5dqaq9qstjms7fumhgca99eva5mkj@4ax.com> <4acaccd1$0$23769$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <FkLym.58376$944.55000@newsfe09.iad> <4acd7893$0$23775$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <5059a3a8-fe0e-40b5-bfd1-856724e19094@u36g2000prn.googlegroups.com> <4aced60f$0$23775$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net>`

```
<all previous parts of thread have been snipped>

I have lost the ability to see who actually is saying what about those who 
"designed" COBOL.  If you want to read a (biased but MOST reliable) history of 
the early development of COBOL, just see the section "History of COBOL" in the 
'85 Standard.

There is no (I hope) doubt that the earliest COBOL "design" documents (and 
directions) were influenced by:

A) The desire to create an "English-like" programming language.  (In *theory*, 
one for which non-programming BUSINESS analysts could read the source code and 
"understand").

B) The original design was definitely done when both compile-time and run-time 
resources were "limited" ("memory" and "storage").  It was HEAVILY influenced by 
those doing "unit type" processing with paper tape, punched cards, and similar 
"input/output devices" (This was both for how source code was compiled and how 
object code was executed)

C) Run-time "performance" was (slightly) less of a concern than run-time 
"memory" requirements.  This lead (in part) to some actual "anti-structural" 
COBOL design issues (Sections with priority numbers, ALTER statement, etc).

D) When the "general" information processing (and to a lesser extent computer 
scientist) theoreticians gained "steam" on the topic of "structured" 
programming, CODASYL (who were then doing the "design" changes for COBOL) 
relatively quickly begin looking at and adding "structured" syntactic features. 
These included (but were not limited to:
 - inline performs
 - scope delimiters (End-xxxx)
 - NOT conditional phrases
  - CONTINUE statement
 - (eventually) nested programs

The cut-off for new FEATURES in what eventually became the '85 Standard was late 
in the '70s or very early in the '80s. At that time, "ANSI" (X3J4 COBOL 
committee) did *NO* new development work.  They only took features that had 
already been designed *and* added to / approved in  the JOD (CODASYL Journal of 
Development).  Then X3J4 had to "select" features to include in the next 
revision and then adapt them for inclusion in the ANSI Standard.

Bottom-Line:
 It is true that the very first COBOL document ('60 or '61) did NOT include 
"structured" design as a goal.
 It is also true that CCC started *relatively* early in determining what 
features needed to be added to the COBOL design to support (but NOT mandate) 
structured programming.
 It is also true that "verbosity" (at least with OPTIONAL words) has always been 
seen as a "goal" not a "problem" with COBOL.  (In recent decades, there has been 
movement to make the more "verbose" English-like features optional rather than 
required.)

If there was "contention" within CCC (or X3J4) any other "early) designers of 
COBOL about ALLOWING for structured programming, I certainly have not heard of 
such, nor do I see it in the actually history of the development and design of 
COBOL.  I do, however, see that it was not a "design" goal of the first 
specification and was not a PRIORITY until (at least) the publication of the '74 
Standard (and development of what eventually became the '85 Standard).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Period for qualification

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7j8ieiF34l387U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<F9wxm.45765$no1.26324@en-nntp-08.dc1.easynews.com> <4ac7fc71$0$23762$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <vHUxm.124864$Y83.88661@newsfe21.iad> <4ac8f88e$0$23771$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <gp4kc5dqaq9qstjms7fumhgca99eva5mkj@4ax.com> <4acaccd1$0$23769$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <FkLym.58376$944.55000@newsfe09.iad> <4acd7893$0$23775$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <5059a3a8-fe0e-40b5-bfd1-856724e19094@u36g2000prn.googlegroups.com> <4aced60f$0$23775$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <pABzm.3125$Xw3.1440@en-nntp-04.dc1.easynews.com>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> <all previous parts of thread have been snipped>
>
…[57 more quoted lines elided]…
> (and development of what eventually became the '85 Standard).

That pretty much gels with what I remember, Bill.

I think the important thing is that a group of commercial concerns 
(admittedly, partly motivated by the chance of landing lucrative contracts 
with DOD...) were prepared to sublimate their individual interests for the 
common good of advancing the infant science of computer programming. It 
wasn't ALL about DOD (although I would agree that Grace Hopper was a strong 
influence in getting everyone round a table) because later we saw DOD 
standardize on ADA, but ADA has never enjoyed the general popularity of 
COBOL.

COBOL was largely based on an idea from Univac (Sperry) embodied in a 
product called Flow-Matic which Univac generously agreed to put in the pot 
for the common good. I couldn't see companies today doing that, although 
IBM, Sun and MicroSoft have shown generosity on some occasions, allowing 
certain thing that were their proprietary to become public domain or freely 
accessible.

I remember much excitement about the '74 standard (I think that was the 
first ANSI one...? Not sure; it was a long time ago.)

Personally, I was dragged kicking and screaming into COBOL in 1967 and we 
used tape based compilers that took 40 minutes to compile a program, based 
on the 1961 standard. For this reason it was common practice to patch object 
code (which was usually punched into an object deck of cards). I remember on 
the 20th July, 1969, standing with others looking out the window at the moon 
in a blue sky over Auckland and listening to the crackled static of the 
Eagle landing and Neil Armstrong's famous quote, then being snapped back to 
reality by a punch girl telling me my source deck was ready... :-))

I guess COBOL "got serious" with the release of the 1985 standard. At that 
time, source code was king and COBOL was pretty much the only game in town 
for commercial computer programming. COBOL programmers were largely 
mainframe (although I remember some "mini-computers" that also ran COBOL) 
and could write their own tickets, as the need for commercial programmers 
expanded and companies started grabbing mainframes. I was making a very good 
living as a COBOL contractor, having already had 18 years COBOL and 20 years 
programming experience. None of us really understood that the seeds of 
destruction were already sown with the invention and release of "personal 
computers" a couple of years previously.

The majority of the professional programmers I worked with saw machines like 
Commodore (Amigas especially), Sinclair spectrum, and even the early "IBM 
PC" as "toys" and looked on them with disdain. Many of these same people 
were unemployed within 10 or 15 years because of the proliferation (and 
networking, and development) of these "toys". There was a certain kind of 
attitude that said if it couldn't run COBOL it wasn't a "real" computer. (I 
remember writing a casino blackjack program in BASIC on a Sinclair spectrum 
for some kids I knew... it was a faithful representation of the Vegas rules, 
using full colour card images, etc. One of my colleagues asked me why I was 
wasting my time... (I was doing it in my own time), never realising that the 
exercise was teaching me BASIC...or understanding why I might want to even 
learn it...("Everything I want to do I can do in COBOL... "  "Really? Could 
you shuffle these cards and deal them for me please..." :-))

I see arguments about when and how structured programmming came along as 
just pointless. I attended a meeting in London a while after Dijkstra's 
famous paper, where Michael Jackson (NOT the unfortunate rock star; the 
unfortunate inventor of Jackson Structured Programming), was presenting the 
case for JSP as a COBOL replacement. He had two main planks in his platform 
(leaving aside a strong ego, confidence, an air of infallibilty (I reckon he 
should have been Pope...), and very good verbal communication skills):

1. The abolition of the GO TO.

2. Dijkstra's paper.

When it was gently pointed out that his precompiler generated spaghetti 
involving huindreds of ALTERed GO TOs, and was a long way from being 
"structured" he made the defence that "Nobody should be looking at that; it 
is all under the covers."  In retrospect, I think he was probably right; I 
have systems that generate code and I don't expect people to operate on that 
code (although I like to think they can if they want to, and I'm certainly 
not ashamed for people to look at it...), but, at the time, especially to an 
audience composed largely of experienced COBOL people, presented amidst a 
P.T. Barnum atmosphere involving Colgate smiles and very glib patter, the 
whole thing seemed decidedly dodgy...

Later I worked with JSP "converts" who perceived it as the one true 
Religion.

One of these people was a Kiwi from Christchurch and it embarrassed me... (I 
always expect more from my countrymen :-)) This man and I had some long 
discussions which became quite heated and I was glad when his visa expired 
and he returned home... :-)

History has shown that it never really caught on, but some sites really 
tried to enforce it.

(I worked on one where a new DP manager who, sadly, had the Hitler gene, 
demanded that I rewrite a number of programs which had just been developed 
and accepted into Production, in JSP.

I refused. (Unlike Doc, I don't necessarily believe that contracting is all 
about keeping the person who writes the cheques happy, although I don't 
think Doc is "wrong" for holding this belief...). I'll never forget his 
twisted little face as he screamed at me: "It is MANDATORY!!! You WILL do 
it!". I explained that no amount of temper tantrums, yelling and screaming, 
would move me and invoked my right to raise the issue to the next level of 
Management. I presented my case which was largely along the lines of "Why 
take a risk, when you don't need to... sleeping dogs etc." and he lost. I 
was not required to do the rewrite. (In fact, nobody was). He got me in his 
office and told me (with malice):" I'll see you out of here." I shrugged. 
There were hundreds of COBOL jobs; life was good...

A year later he was leaving the company and came around to say Goodbye to 
everyone. When it came to me he shuffled and said that although we had had 
our differences, he realised I did a great job. I thanked him and wished him 
well in his new job. As he walked away I said :" Oh Jim... " He paused and 
looked at me. I grinned. "I'm still here..." He turned on his heel and left. 
It was sweet... :-))

I'm not sure how much Structured Programming can be judged or interpreted 
through JSP (at least as implemented in COBOL shops), but I AM sure it 
really doesn't matter in the broad scheme of things.

Pete.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<haq2ri$83$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<F9wxm.45765$no1.26324@en-nntp-08.dc1.easynews.com> <4aced60f$0$23775$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <pABzm.3125$Xw3.1440@en-nntp-04.dc1.easynews.com> <7j8ieiF34l387U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
In article <7j8ieiF34l387U1@mid.individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

[snip]

>(Unlike Doc, I don't necessarily believe that contracting is all 
>about keeping the person who writes the cheques happy, although I don't 
>think Doc is "wrong" for holding this belief...).

Skight correction, Mr Dashwood... while I've often said that 'a 
well-functioning system is one which keeps the person who signs the checks 
for it smiling' I have also said that if writing a particular style or 
subroutine or program violates what I hold as Professionally Ethical 
Programming I will, politely, tell the Lead to find someone else to code 
it.

Yes, the HR office might want a list of all people who are six months away 
from being fully vested in the company's pension plan for Very Good 
Reasons... but my experience is that once such a list is produced the 
folks who show up on it - in violation of *now* what is US Law - tend to 
get the Olde Heave-Ho and no further payments.

I do my best not to write code which can do such things to people and will 
take a hike to another client as I find it necessary.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT: Workplace cultures WAS:Re: Period for qualification

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-10-11T14:06:35+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jcpcqF34lgaeU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<F9wxm.45765$no1.26324@en-nntp-08.dc1.easynews.com> <4aced60f$0$23775$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <pABzm.3125$Xw3.1440@en-nntp-04.dc1.easynews.com> <7j8ieiF34l387U1@mid.individual.net> <haq2ri$83$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article <7j8ieiF34l387U1@mid.individual.net>,
> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Let me get this right...  You appear to be saying that it was common 
practice in certain US companies (no longer, because it is now illegal) to 
prepare lists of people who had been with the company for years and were now 
coming up to retirement, with the object of firing these people to avoid 
having to pay pensions?

I'm stunned.

Like I've said before, my experience of working in the USA is limited, but 
my experience working elsewhere is extensive. I haveNEVER come across a 
company that had such a policy or would even THINK about implementing such a 
policy. To deliberately target the most valuable asset the company has, just 
shows how blind some corporations apparently are. People contribute to their 
pension fund throughout their working lives. The company owes them and most 
companies recognise this and man up to it. I can only imagine the effect 
this must have on the morale of the work force. How do you motivate people 
who know their necks will be wrung the moment (actually, the moment BEFORE) 
they stop laying...?

Over the years, you and I have had disagreements about management and 
attitude. I start to understand why this has been so.  "Enlightened" 
management (and, believe me, Doc, there is some around, although not 
necessarily in the USA by the looks of it) recognises the staff as being the 
most valuable asset on the company's books, and a major corporate 
investment. Meeting their needs and ensuring a proper workplace with proper 
tools and proper support and encouragement, seems to follow naturally from 
that.

I have been priveleged to work with some very enlightened CEOs and it is a 
"given" that a "happy" workforce, is a productive workforce. Apart from the 
moral obligation to ensure that the people who are producing the profits get 
a share of them (through proper compensation and conditions, if not directly 
through shares in the company), it just makes sound economic sense to treat 
the workforce with respect.

In the UK, as the Class system slowly disintegrates (I have seen this at 
first hand over decades) the days when it was enough to wear the right tie 
and belong to the right clubs in order to ensure a management job are 
rapidly disappearing. (It still doesn't hurt to have the right "background", 
but it is no longer a guarantee of a sinecure.) There is an emerging 
"meritocracy" which includes men and women (unthinkable a few decades back) 
and they DON'T all come from Oxford or Cambridge. Some of the top jobs in 
Britain are held by these people and the "fresh blood" they have injected 
into industry has been a major contribution to the general success of the UK 
through difficult times. The "working class" are rapidly moving into the 
"middle class" and don't necessarily see management as the "enemy". Before 
Thatcher the parliamentary democracy was shot down by organised unions who 
simply overrode the elected Government and held the country to ransom. (I 
was in London during the "Winter of Discontent" where old folks died of 
hypothermia because power stations couldn't function due to industrial 
action. I remember getting in my car and driving around London just to get 
warm...) There is a perception now amongst "management" and "workers" that 
"us and them" just hurts everybody. With management jobs becoming attainable 
to all, a lot of the old divisions are simply disappearing. It is far from 
perfect, but in my own lifetime I have seen significant steps forward.

In Germany there is a generally good relationship between highly organised 
Unions and management. Again, drawing managers from the full spectrum of 
society contributes to this. Unlike in Britain, where militant Unionism set 
the country back decades, in Germany unions tend to be positive, understand 
the constraints on the business and make reasonable and fair demands which 
are affordable and can be implemented. I worked in Germany for around 4 
years and there was not a single industrial dispute (at least in the area 
where I worked) in the time I was there. My colleagues were generally upbeat 
and enjoyed coming to work. The social life (which they eagerly involved me 
in, although I was a stranger from a strange culture) was superb and I 
remember that time with fond affection. They worked hard and they played 
hard and overall it was very satisfying. (Not to mention that the beer is 
the best in the world... :-))

In New Zealand we are brought up to be indepenent and not to expect that the 
"world owes us a living". (At least, my generation was brought up with that 
ethic, derived from Victorian settlers who carved a land with hard work and 
determination. I believe the influences of Scots and Irish as well as 
English may have had a bearing on this, but I can't prove it.) The rising 
generation are not so attached to this and we do have kids here now who 
leave school and go straight onto welfare, with no intention of ever 
working. (They are a minority but they exist; there never used to be 
such...) We are taught to treat all people with respect but not to look down 
or up to anyone. There wasn't a Class system but I see one emerging, based 
on affluence. In the time when I was growing up here there were very few 
really rich people. It was pretty much a grey society where most people were 
happy with a house, a car, 2.3 children, and a small boat for fishing at the 
weekend. Mediocrity was encouraged and summed up in the expression:"She'll 
be right, Mate..."  Only 8% of students went on to tertiary education, jobs 
were plentiful (the Government employed 1 in 5 New Zealanders) and the 
affluence of the nation was provided by over 60 million sheep, which gave us 
the 4th highest living standard in the world. In the latter part of the 20th 
century this all fell apart when the use of wool for carpets declined 
(oddly, it is making a comeback now, but we are down to a mere 30 million 
sheep...) and the U.K., which was our sole market, entered the Common Market 
and shafted us royally. It took a while (about 20 years) to recover from 
this and wake up and realise that we better get off our arses (or the 
sheep's back) and find other markets, products, and services.

(The result was "Lord of the Rings", "Flight of the Conchords", John Rowles, 
and Ladyhawke. Serves you all right for buying nylon carpets... :-))

With the lifting of financial regulation and the relaxing of our Immigration 
policy we are becoming a clourful and diverse society which, personally, I 
absolutely enjoy. However, with this comes affluence (affluence is not a bad 
thing; only the misuse of the power it brings and the undermining of basic 
values can be problematic...) and we now have some extremely wealthy people 
living here. This has affected the society in subtle ways which are not 
always positive.

The nation is sports and outdoors mad (to be fair, we have an environment 
that encourages this) and this can spill over into the workplace, inasmuch 
as I have heard of people being favoured for certain jobs over other 
candidates because of sporting connections...

Working in NZ companies is a generally pleasant experience. (Of course, 
people who don't like work will be unhappy anywhere they work, but I'm 
speaking generally...) Everyone uses first names and the "lower levels" of 
the corporate hierarchy (janitor, tea lady - it is the jobs that are lower 
level, not the people...) would greet the CEO (actually, some CEOs I have 
worked with greet everyone, by name, as they walk through the building or 
take the elevator to the "corridors of power"...) . In the old days, before 
the emergence of affluence and multi-nationals coming in, it would have been 
normal for "the Boss" to have a few beers after work with the rest of the 
workforce. Nowadays, he probably repairs to an exclusive restaurant or club 
or someone's yacht, where the business of business continues over cocktails. 
He mixes with and gets to know the workforce at orgainsed corporate outings 
and "Team building" exercises... (yeah, I know, I'm sceptical too, but I 
have to say that, in spite pf myself I have actually enjoyed one or two of 
these... paintballs and laser tag really bring out the beast in me... there 
is something very satisfying about pinging a senior manager in the arse with 
a paint ball and it doesn't seem to do any harm...white water rafting was 
also excellent although I declined bunjy jumping...I guess my character 
still needs building work... :-))

Whether the upper echelons of management mix with the workforce or not, they 
certainly value them. It is ingrained in the culture here that everyone gets 
a "Fair go".

So, having placed tongue-in-cheek and described the workplaces I have been 
privy to, let's take a look at the US. I am quite shocked by what Doc wrote 
because I expected better from the "cradle of the best and the worst".

Is there a "need" for management to be seen as hardnosed bullies who use up 
the work force in the pursuit of the almighty dollar? (If so, then it is 
time they moved out of the 1940s) It seems to me that if nobody valued this 
image and it was socially unacceptable, there wouldn't be people so ready to 
espouse it.

Is the society so morally bankrupt that people will sit back and let these 
practices happen? (Obviously, Doc won't and his position is on record, but 
what about others? The US Government (full credit to them) has moved to ban 
this practice, but what do you do about the attitude that engendered this 
practice in the first place? What other amoral and immoral practices are 
going on in US companies? Is there still a perception by the workforce that 
they are "cannon fodder" for management?

Can we get some comment from people who live and work in the US and see if 
anyone actually enjoys going to work?

> I do my best not to write code which can do such things to people and
> will take a hike to another client as I find it necessary.

So would I, Doc... so would I.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Workplace cultures WAS:Re: Period for qualification

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2009-10-10T23:27:09-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38-dnTY3Z44azUzXnZ2dnUVZ_vudnZ2d@earthlink.com>`
- **References:** `<F9wxm.45765$no1.26324@en-nntp-08.dc1.easynews.com> <4aced60f$0$23775$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <pABzm.3125$Xw3.1440@en-nntp-04.dc1.easynews.com> <7j8ieiF34l387U1@mid.individual.net> <haq2ri$83$1@reader1.panix.com> <7jcpcqF34lgaeU1@mid.individual.net>`

```

"Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message 
news:7jcpcqF34lgaeU1@mid.individual.net...
> docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>> In article <7j8ieiF34l387U1@mid.individual.net>,
>> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>>

<<snip>>

> So, having placed tongue-in-cheek and described the workplaces I have been 
> privy to, let's take a look at the US. I am quite shocked by what Doc 
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> So would I, Doc... so would I.

Management in private companies today must serve the stockholder above all. 
Their jobs and their salraies and bonuses depend upon it.  Money and profit 
are all, and they will sacrifice the long term to the short term.  The 
practice of letting people go to avoid paying them a pension may still go on 
with older workers, but it is going the way of the dodo because most private 
companies today do not offer retirement programs. Companies like GM and 
Chrysler find them too expensive and they adversely affect the ability to 
compete.  I guess some still offer IRA's but I wonder how many offer any 
matching of employee's contributions.  Some people who have already retired 
have lost their pensions or had them severly reduced.  People now must 
provide for themselves and it seems to be a common mistake for them to place 
all their eggs in the company stock basket instead of creating and 
maintaining a diversified portfolio.  When the stock tanks due to corruption 
or mismanagement their retirement is gone.

Workers today are viewed as overhead and as interchangeable cogs in the 
machinery.  That is why there is so much outsourcing.  The overseas cogs are 
much.cheaper.  Experience, bah, experienced people demand higher salaries. 
Replace them with those fresh out of college.  Communication, bah, we can 
communicate internationally just as easily as down the hall.  Security, bah, 
we can enforce it half a world away.  Management swallows all this and more 
whole, because of the greed and desire for profit.  Overseas works cost less 
so there cannot be any negatives to outsourcing which counteract that 
benefit.  Of course if the economy tanks and needs consumers to pull it out 
of recession you might encouter problems due to unemployed workers not 
having any money to spend.

I am sure there are exception to this but in general the landscape is bleak.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Workplace cultures WAS:Re: Period for qualification

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-10-12T19:28:32+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jg0kiF35lkmqU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<F9wxm.45765$no1.26324@en-nntp-08.dc1.easynews.com> <4aced60f$0$23775$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <pABzm.3125$Xw3.1440@en-nntp-04.dc1.easynews.com> <7j8ieiF34l387U1@mid.individual.net> <haq2ri$83$1@reader1.panix.com> <7jcpcqF34lgaeU1@mid.individual.net> <38-dnTY3Z44azUzXnZ2dnUVZ_vudnZ2d@earthlink.com>`

```
Charles Hottel wrote:
> "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
> news:7jcpcqF34lgaeU1@mid.individual.net...
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> above all.

And do they actually ask stockholders what they want or do they just assume 
that everyone wants more money?


> Their jobs and their salraies and bonuses depend upon it. Money and profit 
> are all, and they will sacrifice the long term to
> the short term.

Then they are terminally stupid and deserve what they get.

> The practice of letting people go to avoid paying
> them a pension may still go on with older workers, but it is going
> the way of the dodo because most private companies today do not offer
> retirement programs.

Well, I guess that is one solution and workers can make a choice before 
joining.

The point about having these schemes in the first place is to ensure that 
the workforce is valued and properly recompensed for years of loyal service, 
and to make sure that people don't just get chucked on the scrapheap when 
they can no longer work. The company subsidy should make the sceme 
attractive to people and it is another way of ensuring that the people 
creating the wealth get to share in it.

As a contractor and freelance I never had such an opportunity so I had ot do 
my best to ensure that when the time came ot stop I could still survive. (I 
almost made it... :-) Can't quite afford to quit yet but it is getting 
there...)

 >Companies like GM and Chrysler find them too
> expensive and they adversely affect the ability to compete.  I guess
> some still offer IRA's but I wonder how many offer any matching of
> employee's contributions.

I notice they don't find a lifestyle of opulence for their senior executives 
inhibits their ability to compete though...

Who decides the policy? Gosh! it is the senior executives... where is the 
answerability?

The Stockholders? Why aren't they asking questions? Because they are along 
for the ride? Time someone woke them up...



> Some people who have already retired have
> lost their pensions or had them severly reduced.

Is that legal? I guess if a Pension Fund goes broke it could happen but that 
usually involves a big investigation and ultimate government bail-out.

I wouldn't think that a company can legally "change their minds" once a 
contract is entered into, unless there are extreme circumstances that make 
it impossible to honour the contract.

> People now must
> provide for themselves and it seems to be a common mistake for them
> to place all their eggs in the company stock basket instead of
> creating and maintaining a diversified portfolio.  When the stock
> tanks due to corruption or mismanagement their retirement is gone.

Measures are being taken here to regulate financial advisers and 
institutions to ensure that proper disclosure is made and proper checks and 
balances are in place. We've had some collapses that have wiped out people's 
life savings. It doesn't help when one of the principals is still continung 
the build of a 30 million dollar home in Auckland and it is discovered he 
has private assets offshore in excess of 500 million.(I realise that is 
small by American standards, but you must remember we had comparatively few 
millionaires here before 1980, so this is quite a shock to Kiwis...)

Dishonesty and corruption are a fact of life all over. But an attitude that 
says "screw you, give me gold" is something that each of us can counter.

In the workplace, whether you are running your own company or not, whether 
you are middle management or not, valuing your colleagues and staff cannot 
be wrong. Being on record as opposing the culture of greed and not "going 
along with it" can make a difference.

I remember a distraught (and actually incompetent) manager demanding that I 
work a team through the night once (they had been working long hours for the 
preceding week) and I refused. His theory was that if everyone collapsed he 
could be seen to have done his job. People under stress lose perception. It 
never occurred to him that if I said: "No" there was really very little he 
could do about it. What's he going to do? Fire me? Risk the team going on 
strike or quitting in sympathy. Threaten me (and them)? I'm not easily 
intimidated and I stick up for the people I manage. I got him calmed down 
and told him the best course was for everyone to get some sleep (including 
him). We got the job done and he learned something about managing people.

(I remember another occasion where a team DID work through the night with me 
to save my bacon. I never asked them to, they just did it because they knew 
what was at stake. I have never forgotten it. Maybe it was BECAUSE they knew 
I would never ask them to that they did it...)

Profit is good and money is important, but people are beyond value.


>
> Workers today are viewed as overhead and as interchangeable cogs in
> the machinery.

Then that is a very shortsighted view. I don't believe that the authorities 
on business management are teaching this because it is so obviously wrong.


>That is why there is so much outsourcing.  The
> overseas cogs are much.cheaper.

Yes, they are. And you can buy a Yugo for much less than a Ford. But the 
world isn't driving Yugos...

(I'm not suggesting that all third world programmers are Yugos; it is an 
analogy...)

If you have local workers who demand an unreasonable recompense for the work 
they do (managers are not the only ones blinded by greed) then you can't 
blame companies for outsourcing. The workplace is a marketplace; the rates 
are set by supply and demand.

Wise workforces will be competitive and offer added values that outsourcing 
can't (better communication, local knowledge,  proximity for interaction, 
etc.)

If, despite this, short-sighted managers, driven only by profit, still go 
and outsource, then that company won't be around too long. Even if they do 
manage to survive it, people are not exactly going to be queuing up to work 
there, and other people are not going to buy their products if they are seen 
to have treated their workforce shabbily.

The attitudes of all of us can make a difference. Workers are also 
consumers.



>Experience, bah, experienced people
> demand higher salaries. Replace them with those fresh out of college.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> cannot be any negatives to outsourcing which counteract that benefit.

I think that lesson is being slowly and painfully learned, Charlie.

> Of course if the economy tanks and needs consumers to pull it out of
> recession you might encouter problems due to unemployed workers not
> having any money to spend.
That's OK, the Government can just print more money, right? :-)

> I am sure there are exception to this but in general the landscape is
> bleak.

There's really no excuse.

The United States of America has some of the brightest and most capable 
people on the planet. American "know-how" and a pragmatic approach to 
problems has provided the whole world with major advances in technology. The 
best American seats of learning are respected as such throughout the world.

Surely they are not teaching at Harvard (for example) "consume the workforce 
and shaft the peasants, all that matters is profit."

It seems that a balance has been lost. The nineties, with their culture of 
greed, may have had some influence, but we are supposed to be more grown-up 
now and know better.

Profit is necessary and good, but you don't pursue it at ANY price.

I remember many years ago attending the AGM of a household name German 
company in which I was a stockholder.

It seemed that the company had opened a new division in Africa and the 
workers did not enjoy the priveleges that other workers in the company did. 
(subsidised canteen for meals, recreation areas and facilities for after 
work, free or subsidised medical insurance, etc.) A question was raised from 
the floor and the President of the company responded that the African 
workers were very well treated, much better than the general standard in the 
area, the company was paying the top end of the local salary range, and 
there was no industrial unrest. He also pointed out that to bring local 
workers to par in terms of benefits would cost significant money resulting 
in loss of profit and cut dividends for the people in the meeting. (there 
were around 2000 people attending this AGM.) To my astonishment the original 
questioner then demanded that a vote be taken so the stockholders could 
decide for themselves whether they felt comfortable with this. The vote was 
taken and it passed with a two thirds majority that the African workers 
would be given the same benefits that other workers in the company enjoyed 
around the world, notwithstanding that this would affect the dividend 
cheques.  I saw the President smile as he accepted the decision and I think 
he was not averse to it. People putting Humanity before profit. (I actually 
found myself voting for it too and that surprised me... :-)) I left the 
meeting humbler but very satisfied.

Right here in my home town of Tauranga (population 100,000) we have a large 
utility company that has set up a trust to redistribute some of its profits 
back to the community. They spend around 24 million a year in the community 
and it supports activities and facilities for both the young and old. The 
population of Tauranga (the English equivalent of this Maori name is:"safe 
anchorage"... I'm certainly finding it so :-)) all benefit from the trust, 
and people who buy their electricity from this company (I am one) receive a 
"dividend" cheque every year. I believe the number of customers is around 
18,000; and we all receive a cheque every year. It's not often you get 
companies giving you money because they made more profit than they consider 
proper :-). Anyway, the trustees were up for election this year so they 
decided to review the proportion of the available trust fund that should be 
re-distributed. They told us we could have around $450.00 if they stopped 
supporting local groups.

But the problem with this is that many of these local groups simply couldn't 
run if the trust withdrew funding. (some of them would be funded from 
community government funding, with a consequent increase in rates and 
taxes). Apart from things like drama and music most of these groups are 
small sports teams that use the trust fund to pay for their strip and the 
grounds they use. Other things are groups that work with old folks and a 
local hospice, animal shelters, battered wives shelters, food banks, 
festivals, in fact, if you and a group of friends have a community oriented 
project you need a few bucks to get started, the trust will generally help. 
They pay out anything from a couple of hundred dollars to tens of thousands. 
So those of us who buy our power from them had to decide: Get $450.00  and 
screw the community, or get $369.00 and keep the community activities 
running?

Now, this is a private postal  secret ballot. Nobody will know which way you 
voted and the papers are not identifiable to a given household.

What would you expect?  yeah, me too... I made my vote knowing full well 
that it would be overwhelming for $450.00. People are greedy and 
self-centred, aren't they?

It was a landslide for $369.00.

It's easy to lose faith in people when you see the kind of things going on 
in companies, which we have been discussing here. But companies are run by 
people, and people are influenced by other people.

What is the point of having the ultimate bed with the finest silken sheets 
if you can't sleep nights?

Make a stand. Don't condone the cult of greed. If you're a manager, point 
out that profit at any price (especially at the expense of the people who 
helped you get it) is a bad result.  Don't lionize and admire the greedy fat 
cats who are destroying your country, or seek to emulate them.

People can make a difference and the attitudes of people can shape a 
society.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Workplace cultures WAS:Re: Period for qualification

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2009-10-12T12:29:47-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<-PCdnaIMmd_txE7XnZ2dnUVZ_v-dnZ2d@earthlink.com>`
- **References:** `<F9wxm.45765$no1.26324@en-nntp-08.dc1.easynews.com> <4aced60f$0$23775$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <pABzm.3125$Xw3.1440@en-nntp-04.dc1.easynews.com> <7j8ieiF34l387U1@mid.individual.net> <haq2ri$83$1@reader1.panix.com> <7jcpcqF34lgaeU1@mid.individual.net> <38-dnTY3Z44azUzXnZ2dnUVZ_vudnZ2d@earthlink.com> <7jg0kiF35lkmqU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
<snip>

>> Management in private companies today must serve the stockholder
>> above all.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

For large companies listed on the stock exchanges Wall Street  makes 
projections and if companies fall below these projections their stock rating 
is downgraded.  This makes management look bad and stock holders scream. 
Managers and chairmen of the board can get fired over this.

<snip>

>> Some people who have already retired have
>> lost their pensions or had them severly reduced.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Sometimes it is a case like Enron where the stock becomes worthless and the 
pensions disappear.  I may be wrong but I believe that a part of the GM and 
Chrysler bankruptcies involved reducing the pension of some already retired. 
I don't know if it is "legal" but it is not "right".

> I wouldn't think that a company can legally "change their minds" once a 
> contract is entered into, unless there are extreme circumstances that make 
> it impossible to honour the contract.
>
<snip>

> I remember many years ago attending the AGM of a household name German 
> company in which I was a stockholder.
…[73 more quoted lines elided]…
> society.

There was a banker down south who retired and received a large (IIRC around 
$20 million) bonus/golden parachute (sorry I don't remeber all the details). 
What I do remember is that he quietly and without fanfare distributed the 
money among the bank employees.  Even though he did not seek publicity of 
course the story came out .  He simply said that it was  the help and good 
work of those people that enabled their success.  Unfortunately mangers like 
him are the exception and not the rule.

I have never been a manager and I am certain being one is harder than I 
imagine, but most of them in my view take a wrongheaded approach.  They want 
to be treated as more important than the workers and they view the workers 
as serving them and their realm of power. They should view themseleves and 
the workers as serving the company and its customers.  In fact a large part 
of a managers job should be serving the workers by taking care of matters 
that would otherwise distract the workers from doing their jobs and doing 
things that make their jobs easier and more productive.

It is amazing but many companies even treat their customers badly. Ever 
tried to get IT support for a problem with your PC?  The automated telephone 
answering systems save money but they usually do not serve the customer very 
well. Well you know this from your experience with trying to buy a new COBOL 
compiler.

See this URL: 
http://www.washingtonpost.com/wp-dyn/content/article/2009/09/02/AR2009090203887.html

Now these people paying thousands of dollars for football tickets have to 
accept some responsibility, but the fact is that the football team resold 
the tickets and did not lose much if any money.  Also they did not sell the 
tickets to people on a list waiting to get tickets.  This is the kind of 
total disrespect for customers that is leading to companies here going out 
of business.

There are companies here where you cannot get any employee to help a 
customer find what they are looking for or suggest what materials to use to 
solve a problem. I do not go back there unless I have absolutly no choice. 
I stopped going to Circuit City because of the crappy way they treated their 
employees.  I guess I was not the only one as they are now out of business.
```

On Sat, 10 Oct 2009 23:43:51 -0500, Paul
<paul-nospamatall.raulerson@mac.com> wrote:

>Just to back up Doc, I have seen this exact kind of thing happen for 
>real. Unisys, for
>instance, in the 1980s. I was there and watched it happen. It can 
>easily happen again,
>in almost any U.S company.

I have no idea whether these policies were "common".   I suppose we
need to define that word first and then get some numbers.   I haven't
seen it in any place I've worked, but have been familiar with the
business gossip about it.

I've read that Microsoft has a variation of it - hire lots of young
guys straight out of college with decent pay - but don't give raises
based upon seniority.   Figure that when people get a certain amount
of experience, they will move out to better paying jobs.    The stated
advantage is to keep the mind set young - but it also keeps pay &
retirement benefits down.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Workplace cultures WAS:Re: Period for qualification

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-10-14T12:26:41+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jkgljF35p2gaU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<4acaccd1$0$23769$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <FkLym.58376$944.55000@newsfe09.iad> <4acd7893$0$23775$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <5059a3a8-fe0e-40b5-bfd1-856724e19094@u36g2000prn.googlegroups.com> <4aced60f$0$23775$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <pABzm.3125$Xw3.1440@en-nntp-04.dc1.easynews.com> <7j8ieiF34l387U1@mid.individual.net> <haq2ri$83$1@reader1.panix.com> <7jcpcqF34lgaeU1@mid.individual.net> <4ad16285$0$23762$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <v069d5l0r46606ot630jivmdinvntsqb61@4ax.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On Sat, 10 Oct 2009 23:43:51 -0500, Paul
> <paul-nospamatall.raulerson@mac.com> wrote:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> retirement benefits down.

I've not heard that before Howard, but it seems likely.

Nevertheless, I see no problem with this as far as ethics go.

In fact, it is an imaginative and fair approach if paying pensions is really 
a problem (although I wouldn't have thought that was a problem for 
Microsoft). The point is that employees have the choice, and it is certainly 
true that many people join Microsoft with the express purpose of putting 
"Microsft" on their CV and with no intention of making a career at the 
company.

Most of the companies I know that have a pension scheme invest it into 
managed funds so the burden is not entirely on the company. Sometimes this 
can go seriously wrong, but, for the most part, it works well.

Since reading Doc's original post on this I have discussed it with a number 
of people here and the general reaction was the same as mine, shocked 
disbelief. Two of these people are CEOs, one of them runs a small company 
and the other runs a pretty large corporate. Everyone I spoke to was 
unanimous that this is simply a dreadful practice stemming from a 1940s 
mindset. None of them would even consider doing anything like it. (One of 
them pointed out to me that he was approaching retirement himself and would 
be pretty upset if he got shafted like that.)

I'd still like to hear from anyone who is very happy with their employer, or 
who is doing business studies in the US and can confirm whether this kind of 
approach is actually endorsed by Acadaemia.

Is employment in the USA REALLY a battle between the workforce and 
management, or is it that a few small minded managers THINK the hardnosed 
approach is the way to the top? What about employees? Is the general 
consensus that the company is the enemy and ripping them off is fair game?

My own observation is that people in the US generally work pretty hard and 
have less holidays than workers in other parts of the world.

But if the mindset is that there is a war going on between employers and 
employees, it will be very hard for progress to be made with improving 
conditions.

Maybe innovative and fair policies like Howard described for Microsoft will 
gradually turn the balance as a new generation who are enthusiastic about 
work emerges.

I hope so.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Workplace cultures WAS:Re: Period for qualification

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2009-10-14T07:34:52-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bhkbd51kplg615b2d19go59vnesfkbvjc4@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<4acd7893$0$23775$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <5059a3a8-fe0e-40b5-bfd1-856724e19094@u36g2000prn.googlegroups.com> <4aced60f$0$23775$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <pABzm.3125$Xw3.1440@en-nntp-04.dc1.easynews.com> <7j8ieiF34l387U1@mid.individual.net> <haq2ri$83$1@reader1.panix.com> <7jcpcqF34lgaeU1@mid.individual.net> <4ad16285$0$23762$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <v069d5l0r46606ot630jivmdinvntsqb61@4ax.com> <7jkgljF35p2gaU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Wed, 14 Oct 2009 12:26:41 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>In fact, it is an imaginative and fair approach if paying pensions is really 
>a problem (although I wouldn't have thought that was a problem for 
>Microsoft). 

Microsoft is old enough now to have long term employees.   Sometimes a
new company will promise pensions that are unfunded based upon hopes
and air.    Sometimes unions and companies agree to this.    When the
future isn't as rosy as hoped, bad things happen.   We have some huge
companies in the U.S. defaulting on their pension promises.

>Most of the companies I know that have a pension scheme invest it into 
>managed funds so the burden is not entirely on the company. Sometimes this 
>can go seriously wrong, but, for the most part, it works well.

In my opinion, all pension plans should be funded and diversified. I'm
in favor of passing a law that defines what is a pension and what is
profit sharing.   We could still pick our poison, but don't let the
company and/or union feed us empty promises and tell us it's a full
meal.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Workplace cultures WAS:Re: Period for qualification

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** Paul <paul-nospamatall.raulerson@mac.com>
- **Date:** 2009-10-17T00:37:20-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4ad95810$0$23739$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net>`
- **References:** `<F9wxm.45765$no1.26324@en-nntp-08.dc1.easynews.com> <4ac7fc71$0$23762$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <vHUxm.124864$Y83.88661@newsfe21.iad> <4ac8f88e$0$23771$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <gp4kc5dqaq9qstjms7fumhgca99eva5mkj@4ax.com> <4acaccd1$0$23769$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <FkLym.58376$944.55000@newsfe09.iad> <4acd7893$0$23775$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <5059a3a8-fe0e-40b5-bfd1-856724e19094@u36g2000prn.googlegroups.com> <4aced60f$0$23775$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <pABzm.3125$Xw3.1440@en-nntp-04.dc1.easynews.com> <7j8ieiF34l387U1@mid.individual.net> <haq2ri$83$1@reader1.panix.com> <7jcpcqF34lgaeU1@mid.individual.net> <4ad16285$0$23762$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <v069d5l0r46606ot630jivmdinvntsqb61@4ax.com> <7jkgljF35p2gaU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On 2009-10-13 18:26:41 -0500, "Pete Dashwood" 
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> said:

> Howard Brazee wrote:
>> On Sat, 10 Oct 2009 23:43:51 -0500, Paul
…[66 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

It all depends Pete. My day job is for a company I really like, and 
trust. However, that is because I trust the top people. I know
them personally, and trust them because of that. We have a pension plan 
where the company contributes money each year, and that is distributed 
based upon seniority alone. (Okay, not entirely, there are federal 
mandated maximums based upon your salary, but that isn't the company's 
doing...)

 We are smart about raises, and morals, and telephone bills, and other 
similar issues. The founder tells people that they can use the company 
to get rich or whatever their personal goals are, so long as they are 
willing to play by the rules and pay the price. He is right, though 
most of us are unwilling to pay the price to get rich. We do have 
families.

But in general, most people work for companies that could care less 
about the workers - they are simply interchangeable parts. Easily 
hired. Easily fired. Easily replaced with offshore labor. The stories 
you hear about the top honcho's taking home huge earnings, even while 
companies are failing, are pretty much true. Golden Parachutes are 
indeed, one defense against a hostile takeover. Unfortunately, they 
also mean a CEO can come in and run a company in a terrible manner, 
making it an easy target for acquisition, and be highly rewarded 
financially for such poor behavior.

All this really took off back in the 1980's when the idea of hostile 
corporate takeovers really took off and started being employed.  My 
company for instance, protects against this with a dual stock option. 
There are other methods.

But the end result, is that the employees are always - and invariably - 
shafted. American Airlines is a great example. Huge management bonuses 
are paid out, while pilots, flight attendants and mechs have to take 
reductions in salary and benefits.

Hewlett Packards takeover of Compaq is another great, if now 
historical, example.

-Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Workplace cultures WAS:Re: Period for qualification

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-10-16T23:15:14+13:00

```

Paul <paul-nospamatall.raulerson@mac.com> wrote in message
news:4ac8f88e$0$23771$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net...
> I would not say that B of A is preferable to A.B, and the reverse
> is even worse (A of B or B.A). Then again, one of the issues I
> sometimes have with COBOL is that it is wordy when conciseness
> is more appropriate.

I'd say that everybody who has used Cobol would agree that it is wordy.  But
that's because of the original intent.  The designers were also a talented
bunch of people who did thier best to strike a compromise between clarity
and conciseness.  They came up with some constructs which did not survive
very long: e.g., the NOTE construct which has been utterly superceded by the
common, ugly, simple asterisk.

>
>    Retirement_Date.year
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>


"Retirement_Date.year" would suggest multiplication to me if I didn't know
better.

Conciseness is a virtue up to the point where it obscures meaning.  Would
you like to try to live all aspects of your life using "texting"
vocabulary???

Consider this statment - valid in one language that I know of, perhaps
others.

A *= B.

Simple enough when you're used to it but pretty cryptic until then.
Consider also the operators used in C.  Mix a dozen of them in one concise
statment and you have a challenge.

Possibly, your argument is a transformation of "I understand it so it must
be easy for everybody".  A very easy trap to fall into.

PL


>
```

don't require a space to delimit an arithmetic operator.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Period for qualification

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2009-10-05T02:54:58-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nDhym.636$yQ3.183@en-nntp-03.dc1.easynews.com>`
- **References:** `<F9wxm.45765$no1.26324@en-nntp-08.dc1.easynews.com> <4ac7fc71$0$23762$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net>`

```
Paul,
  At the time that COPY/REPLACING and REPLACE are processed, the compiler does 
NOT know any syntax (except for those statements).  That is why, for example, in
   Pic 9(10)9.99

the TEXT words are:
  9
  10
  9.99
with the separators ( and ).

". " (period followed by a space) is a separator while "." (period without a 
following space) is NOT a separator.

As others have pointed out, different languages have different rules and 
different things are significant in different ways.

What, in COBOL is called the "text manipulation phase", where COPY, 
COPY/REPLACING, and REPLACE are processed is quite different than other phases 
of COBOL compilation.  Making a period with no following space a separator (for 
qualification) would have DRAMATIC impact on other (existing) code.

FYI,
  When the '85 Standard added the colon as a new separator, this had surprising 
impact on some existing code and compilers.  Depending on which vendor you are 
(were) using, you might want to look at its migration information from the '75 
to '85 Standard for some implications of this.
```

#### ↳ Re: Period for qualification

- **From:** riplin <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-10-11T16:15:42-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e2ccf7c6-ddcf-4c80-85c8-4328153b4342@h40g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<F9wxm.45765$no1.26324@en-nntp-08.dc1.easynews.com>`

```
On Oct 3, 12:38 pm, "William M. Klein" <wmkl...@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
wrote:
> I don't remember all the details, but I can think of at least one problem with
> using a period for qualification.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> was first proposed BEFORE OO came along.

The B OF A syntax came from the FACT language. FACT also allowed A B
format, with a space separator, and any combination of these.  See
page 14 of:

http://bitsavers.org/pdf/honeywell/fact/160-2M_FACT_spec_1960.pdf

    WAREHOUSE PART NUMBER

or  NUMBER OF PART IN WAREHOUSE

or  NUMBER OF WAREHOUSE PART

There must have been a good reason to only use the 'OF' qualification
format when taking this into COBOL.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Period for qualification

- **From:** Clark F Morris <cfmpublic@ns.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2009-10-11T22:46:55-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0525d5t8knebp6vtmkg91q2rdfd99b24ng@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<F9wxm.45765$no1.26324@en-nntp-08.dc1.easynews.com> <e2ccf7c6-ddcf-4c80-85c8-4328153b4342@h40g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Sun, 11 Oct 2009 16:15:42 -0700 (PDT), riplin <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
wrote:

>On Oct 3, 12:38ï¿½pm, "William M. Klein" <wmkl...@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
>wrote:
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I suspect the construct ADD A B TO C D which means ADD A TO C ADD B TO
C ADD A TO D ADD B TO D would not have been possible.  Even if the
parser had been able to distinguish group level, it would not have
known what to do with MOVE A TO B C. if B was a group level containing
C.  Since data names must contain at least one Alphabetic, if
qualification rules required that paragraph names could not be
qualified in the format section-name.paragraph-name because the
section name could be 1111 and the paragraph name could be 0012, would
using A11.23B where both A11 and 23B are data names be ambiguous?
```

Pete Dashwood wrote:

> Clark F Morris wrote:
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> ONE"?

"With the exception of paragraph-name, section-name, level-number, and
segment-number, all user-defined words must contain at least one
alphabetic character".

COB1 (BS2000) COBOL Compiler Reference Manual Part1 Edition August 1986.
That's my oldest manual as my previous office burned down in 1986.

4.2.2.1 COBOL Words
    A COBOL word is a character-string of not more than 30 characters  
which forms a user-defined word, a system-name, or a reserved word.  
Each character of a COBOL word is selected from the set of letters,   
digits, and the hyphen. The hyphen may not appear as the first or last
character. Each lowercase letter is considered to be equivalent to its
corresponding uppercase letter. Within a source program, reserved  
words and user-defined words form disjoint sets; reserved words and
system-names form disjoint sets; system-names and user-defined words
form intersecting sets. The same COBOL word may be used as a
system-name and as a user-defined word within a source program; and the
class of a specific occurrence of this COBOL word is determined by the
context of the clause or phrase in which it occurs.

4.2.2.1.1 User-Defined Words
 A user-defined word is a COBOL word that must be supplied by the user
to satisfy the format of a clause or statement. Each character of a
user-defined word is selected from the set of characters 'A', 'B', 'C',
 . . . , 'Z', 'O', . . . , '9', and '-', except that the '-' may not
appear as the first or last character.

ANSI X3.23-1985, page IV-5 and 6.

No older version here available.
```

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-10-12T23:37:52+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jgf81F33l9gbU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<F9wxm.45765$no1.26324@en-nntp-08.dc1.easynews.com> <e2ccf7c6-ddcf-4c80-85c8-4328153b4342@h40g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <0525d5t8knebp6vtmkg91q2rdfd99b24ng@4ax.com> <7jfpb1F34edd6U1@mid.individual.net> <4ad2f50b$0$284$14726298@news.sunsite.dk>`

Fred Mobach wrote:
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>
…[47 more quoted lines elided]…
> No older version here available.

Thanks Fred. That was Burroughs? (I wrote COBOL (and Assembler) for the B500 
around 1971...)

I learned COBOL for an ICL 1900 in 1967 and I distinctly remember the 
teacher telling us the first letter had to be alphabetic.

The trouble with vendor manuals is that they don't always reflect the 
standard, and the trouble with the standard is that it takes too long to 
produce...

Pete.
```

Pete Dashwood wrote:

> Fred Mobach wrote:
>> Pete Dashwood wrote:
…[52 more quoted lines elided]…
> the B500 around 1971...)

It wasn't Burroughs (never met those systems), it was Siemens BS2000
mainframes (and the predecessors) which I've used from 1970-1976 at the
Dutch Railways IT company. And from 1980-1999 in my own company.

> I learned COBOL for an ICL 1900 in 1967 and I distinctly remember the
> teacher telling us the first letter had to be alphabetic.

They didn't tell me that, and it wasn't necessary either. :-)

> The trouble with vendor manuals is that they don't always reflect the
> standard, and the trouble with the standard is that it takes too long
> to produce...

Nowadays a standard is a download away, but before the internet
standards could be bought sometimes for extreme high prices.
```

On Oct 12, 5:23 pm, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> Clark F Morris wrote:
> > On Sun, 11 Oct 2009 16:15:42 -0700 (PDT), riplin <rip...@Azonic.co.nz>
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
> alphabetic character. Has it been changed to  "MUST CONTAIN AT LEAST ONE"?

It didn't change.

You can get images of an ICL 1900 Serries COBOL format reference Card
at http://www.icl1900.co.uk/techpub/c19ref.html

It also has { OF / IN } as options for qualified data names.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Period for qualification

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2009-10-13T14:55:24-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<t3q9d55ft086o385oth0f5abonhctbiv9s@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<F9wxm.45765$no1.26324@en-nntp-08.dc1.easynews.com> <e2ccf7c6-ddcf-4c80-85c8-4328153b4342@h40g2000prf.googlegroups.com> <0525d5t8knebp6vtmkg91q2rdfd99b24ng@4ax.com> <7jfpb1F34edd6U1@mid.individual.net> <925db26e-e0b6-4e26-8fdf-24edcb31653c@a39g2000pre.googlegroups.com>`

```
On Tue, 13 Oct 2009 12:55:01 -0700 (PDT), riplin <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
wrote:

>> I remember learning (in 1967) that COBOL datanames MUST START with an
>> alphabetic character. Has it been changed to ï¿½"MUST CONTAIN AT LEAST ONE"?
>
>It didn't change.

I tend to learn the rules that apply to my environment.   Sometimes
this means I still follow those rules when I move towards a less
restrictive environment.    In the case of CoBOL, that can actually
have an advantage.    For instance, I once was part of a project
converting small Burroughs CoBOL to large Burroughs CoBOL, and one of
the changes was to change the keyword PC to PIC.    

Even if the code is 100% CoBOL standard, if I migrate to an
environment that is more restrictive - it can cost to correct for it.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Period for qualification

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-10-12T17:20:40+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jfp4qF35s9l6U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<F9wxm.45765$no1.26324@en-nntp-08.dc1.easynews.com> <e2ccf7c6-ddcf-4c80-85c8-4328153b4342@h40g2000prf.googlegroups.com>`

```
riplin wrote:
> On Oct 3, 12:38 pm, "William M. Klein" <wmkl...@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
> wrote:
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> format when taking this into COBOL.

I believe that both "OF" and "IN" are/were acceptable as qualifiers in 
COBOL. I remember writing code just like that in the second line above, but 
I don't remember where or when... (senility :-)) I think, like most things, 
you fall into a habit. These days if I HAVE to use qualification in COBOL I 
automatically write "OF" so I don't know whether "IN" is still supported or 
not.

(All of the code generated by the PRIMA Toolset uses "OF" (hardly surprising 
seeing as I wrote the original templates... :-))

I also remember coding "OTHERWISE" instead of "ELSE" in COBOL, but you 
certainly don't see much of that nowadays... :-)

Pete.
```

<a id="t-fa16fdbf63"></a>
## Thread: IBM announced newest release of product including "IBM COBOL for Windows" — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### IBM announced newest release of product including "IBM COBOL for Windows"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2009-10-09T02:05:09-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<HgBzm.5625$Gs.1499@en-nntp-01.dc1.easynews.com>`

```
This week, IBM announced the latest release of the *ONLY* product that they now 
sell that include a currently supported version of "IBM COBOL for Windows".

See:

    http://www-01.ibm.com/common/ssi/rep_ca/0/897/ENUS209-380/ENUS209-380.PDF

It appears (but I haven't seen the details) that there are new installation 
options that will allow a user to better "tailor" which features of RDz they 
want installed.

NOTE WELL:
  This does NOT mean that there are options for only BUYING those features that 
you want to use.  If you are a shop that *only* wants COBOL (not PL/I, for 
example) and ONLY want it for programs to be deployed under Windows (not on z/OS
and no CICS or IMS support), then you still will be required to BUY those other 
features - whether or not you will actually install them.

You can see (get to) online documentation for V7.6 from:
   http://www-01.ibm.com/software/awdtools/rdz/library/index.html#pd-v76
```
