<a id="t-a6551d804f"></a>
## Thread: Data compression — 11 msgs

### Data compression

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-10-10T19:38:29+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jaofrF33ccrdU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
I have been preparing some video tutorials for the PRIMA Toolset.

This has brought me into the new (to me...) world of video toasters, video 
formats, and video codecs (code/decode).

The problem I have is that a 15 minute video in its raw .MOV form takes 
around 1.6 GB. So, the 45 minute session that describes Transformation of 
COBOL code (this is where your existing applications have all the indexed 
file access removed and replaced with invokes of an Object Oriented Data 
Access Layer), is heading towards 5 GB...

I converted the .MOV files (I made the video in sections) to be .AVI files 
and that brought it down to 4 GB, then I Zipped that and finished up with 
1.4 GB. (Video in 2 parts of around 25 minutes each)

This is still a pretty heavy chunk of data to be passing about. And it is 
stretching the friendship with my web hosting SP for me to park it on the 
PRIMA web site. (I COULD cut it to a DVD and mail it to individual clients, 
but at least one of them needs it urgently and so I need to have it 
downloadable by Monday. Besides, I need it to be public so it can be browsed 
by interested parties who may then become prospects for the toolset.)

I had a look around the web and found there are a number of places where you 
can park stuff (apart from the obvious YouTube) and some of them are even 
free (with conditions), but the most likely one (which allowed uploads up to 
5 GB) would take around 23 hours to upload it, at the rate it ran when I 
tested it with a smaller file...

I sought help from an ISP who should know about these things and he pointed 
me at various software but none of it seemed to really make the files much 
smaller. He claimed I should be able to get 4 GB into around .5 GB without 
too much loss, but so far, I haven't been able to achieve  anything like 
that.

I tried 9 different codecs (these are the "engines" that encode and decode a 
video file into a compressed format). Some were good (DivX/Xvid) some were 
not. I tried converting the .AVI files to .WMV (for Windows Media Player). 
This was the worst of the lot... certainly it cut the size significantly, 
but it also rendered the file almost unusable due to data loss which left 
text unreadable.

In this tutorial I walk through COBOL code and show exactly how a legacy 
program in standard COBOL can invoke methods on objects that deliver data 
services. There are a number of places where transformed code is shown 
alongside the original so that the COBOL can be compared line by line, and 
there is a fair bit of jumping around in code. This means the  video 
definition has be high enough to render program source clearly.

If any of you have been playing with video (maybe toasted screens, or even 
family movies) and have found a favourite video manipulator 
(Editor/converter) that allows video to be compressed to a manageable size 
for streaming or download, could you please comment here?

Cheers,

Pete.

PS If I do manage to crack this and get the current 1.4 GB into something 
more manageable, I'll post how I did it.

Maybe a video tutorial... :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: Data compression

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> I have been preparing some video tutorials for the PRIMA Toolset.
>
…[58 more quoted lines elided]…
> Maybe a video tutorial... :-)

Well, it is what it is.

At my latest upload speed test of 2.5Mb/sec, I compute it would take about 
45 minutes to upload 1.4GB (and about 21 minutes to download it).

Maybe you could convert it to a slide show (with narration) instead of a 
video?

Or, if uploading is the stumbling block, you can mail the CD to me and I'll 
upload it for you.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Data compression

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-10-11T02:30:01+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jbgjeF341o63U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<7jaofrF33ccrdU1@mid.individual.net> <o4adne-vDpTB5k3XnZ2dnUVZ_jidnZ2d@earthlink.com>`

```
HeyBub wrote:
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>> I have been preparing some video tutorials for the PRIMA Toolset.
…[67 more quoted lines elided]…
> of a video?

That's a possibility. However, I already spent many hours on this and I am 
loath to write it all off. The finished product is actually pretty good and 
covers everything. I just need to get it compressed.
>
> Or, if uploading is the stumbling block, you can mail the CD to me
> and I'll upload it for you.

Thanks Jerry, that's very kind. I'm thinking that for the immediate urgent 
situation I'll set up a direct connection with the client  and just let him 
pick up the individual parts from my system.

Since I posted this I've had a couple of recommendations for software which 
I'll try out and see how it goes.

If I REALLY can't make it any smaller, perhaps I'll take you up on your 
offer.

(I have already blown my Broadband limits experimenting with this so I will 
be on "strangled" Broadband for the rest of the month... that's the first 
time this has ever happened to me. Video just chews through bandwidth like 
Pacman...)

Many thanks.

Pete.
```

```
No problem. Always eager to do a favor for a pal. I don't have a "strangled"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Data compression

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-10-11T15:36:47+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jcum1F35hge4U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<7jaofrF33ccrdU1@mid.individual.net> <o4adne-vDpTB5k3XnZ2dnUVZ_jidnZ2d@earthlink.com> <7jbgjeF341o63U1@mid.individual.net> <2uKdnY6KAal1KU3XnZ2dnUVZ_rednZ2d@earthlink.com>`

```
HeyBub wrote:
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>> HeyBub wrote:
…[103 more quoted lines elided]…
> Does your neighbor have an unprotected wi-fi...

Funny you should say that... :-)

I have ADSL (no cable) at 3.7 Mbs down and 284Kb up. Generally it is fine. 
But after 5 GB it gets "strangled" by the ISP to encourage me to buy a more 
expensive plan... As I generally use around 3GB a month this has never been 
a problem..

I have an "unprotected" Wi-Fi. Except that anyone trying to use it can't get 
in. It uses MAC address and only allows known machines to connect.  The 
nature of the data being transmitted over it is not such that it requires 
secrecy and there is a slight performance edge in NOT using WEP.

I guess someone COULD spoof a MAC address but they'd be sitting outside my 
house for a couple of days to do so...

Pete.
```

```
<<how to compress lot of video data>>

> decode a video file into a compressed format). Some were good
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

In case licenses and patents might worry you, the least worries are with
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Theora
```

```
<Hangs head, shuffles feet and fiddles with eyepatch, while parrot squawks

Er...while I don't condone piracy and will obey the rules wherever possible,

then I'd probably use it. I have no requirement for, or intention of, using

DVDs, for example.

Thanks for the information, Fred.

Pete.
```

- **References:** `<7jaofrF33ccrdU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
I expect you have already looked at a variety of web pages, but
perhaps some of those products used specifically for PACS, especially
in the health services, which are accumulating masses of patient data,
might be useful, though they do seem expensive.

Another thought if you haven't already tried it, is to reduce the
colour palette down to 256 colours, sometimes the default is millions,
which presumably means that each pixel needs a larger reference field
for them.

Another time as someone already said you might try animation and/or an
efficient screen capture utility such as that from Balesio for at
least part of the presentation.

Good luck with it.

Robert
```

```
Sorry for previous blank post... finger trouble :-)

Real response is at the end of quoted below...

Pete Dashwood wrote:
> I have been preparing some video tutorials for the PRIMA Toolset.
>
…[58 more quoted lines elided]…
> Maybe a video tutorial... :-)

OK, we finally got there. (Many thanks to all who responded.)

We tried direct FTP to the client and it would have taken around 23 hours...

VPN couldn't work (although I managed to sign in successfully) because of 
security keys and subnet address ranges on both ends. We probably could have 
got this working but there was no guarantee it would transfer any faster 
anyway so we abandoned it.

Finally, with help from my long-suffering web-host ISP, we got the 1.4 GB 
onto the PRIMA site, and the client downloaded it from there. I shouldn't 
think many of you are interested in the actual video but, in case I'm wrong, 
here's the link:

http://primacomputing.co.nz/Downloads/Tutorials/PRIMATransformation.zip

(I probably won't be leaving it there too long and will put it somewhere 
else as soon as I have a smaller version. It will probably end up as a 
series of files - see point 4 below.)

The feedback from the client has been glowing. He said that, thanks to the 
video he was able to do the process on his first attempt. He said a number 
of other things which made me blush but I won't repeat them here, suffice to 
say, he is a VERY happy customer and will be a reference site for PRIMA.

Here's what I learned from all of this:

1. Video tutorials are a really good way to document processes for training 
(they don't completely obviate the need for documentation, but they 
certainly allow you to impart a large amount of information in a quicker 
time than people could read it), and they are actually fun to do.
2.  Video is a resource hog and it doesn't matter what codec you use, it 
will take HEAPS of space.
3. Most of the video editors and converters you will see advertised simply 
don't live up to their advertising. (I ended up trying 8 different video 
editors. Most of them were very good and let you do neat stuff like 
transitions, but the huge plethora of legal and illegal codecs out there 
makes it a jungle. The link that Fred posted about codec comparison should 
be required reading BEFORE you start...) The disappointment is that none of 
them REALLY do a good lossless compression (at least, not good enough for 
something like a program listing), and some of them take hours to render a 
file into a different format. I liked AviTricks and HandBrake, AVS suite is 
probably worth buying (I did), GOM was hopeless (although the ogm codec gave 
the best compression, the GOM reader couldn't read its own files and kept 
complaining that the codec was missing. In the end I uninstalled it.)
4. Keep videos short and split them into parts which should not exceed 
around 10 minutes. You don't want your .AVI file to exceeed 300 MB as that 
is the limit for an upload in photobucket.
5. .AVI files can be loaded as a playlist for Windows Media Player, so if 
you have a series of short videos, they will play as one contiguous file, 
without you actually having to convert them to such.
6. There are a number of free video toasters on the Net, and all of them 
seem to work fine.

Pete.
```

<a id="t-91e50e314c"></a>
## Thread: Performance and early/late binding — 2 msgs

### Performance and early/late binding

- **From:** Clark F Morris <cfmpublic@ns.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2009-10-10T23:30:16-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hp42d513lj6kukfti4bgcnkvggoi3ljoth@4ax.com>`

```
There are some very interesting trade-offs among time of binding,
performance and maintainability.  The situation is not as cut and
dried as it might seem.  For example, IBM z series COBOL binds
virtually all of the system sub-routines including the I-O modules at
run-time for batch programs.  Many shops including the three that I
have been at also had the default compile option for batch as DYNAM,
bind at run time.  This has a slight but measurable cost in CPU time
for both the initial bind and for each call. The binding at compile
time of modules written in the same language (COBOL, C/C++, etc.) can
allow for very interesting optimizations.  On the z/OS system the bind
needed to create an executable module (the compile produces executable
code that must be wrapped) cuts down on the inter-module overhead
versus execution time bind but doesn't eliminate it.  While I have not
used OO, the requirement for parameter verification (something also
recommended by the SHARE/Guide Language Futures Task Force in the
1980's for calls) adds to the initial bind overhead.  Remote Procedure
Calls would have even more overhead because of the communications
links.  There are reasons why the World Wide Web is sometimes called
the World Wide Wait.  In general it take resource to move data across
boundaries, especially when you add a communications link where the
speed of light matters and there are relay delays.  As someone who
remembers 1200 baud dedicated lines where you could watch the data
flow on a monitor, the speed of my banking transactions amazes me
especially given that I am using an encrypted link.  However this all
takes compute and communication time and both resources are finite.
Efficiency still matters although not as much.  If you don't believe
it matters, then why did Microsoft put so much effort into improving
performance in Windows 7 and Norton have to put a major amount of
effort into improving the performance of their security programs.  I
know that I am happier with both my e-mail package responsiveness and
web responsiveness since I upgraded from Zone Alarm 9 to Zone Alarm
10.

The other interesting question is when do you want the change in the
requested service to take effect.  Early binding may be chosen so that
the change only takes effect when the requesting service is changed or
at least rebound.  This option may be chosen so that testing can be
phased.  Late binding means that changes and fixes are immediately
available which can sometimes lead to undesired results and also means
that the scope of prior testing may be large.  

The situation becomes even murkier as more of our applications are
driven and controlled by external data tables be they in data bases or
random access files.  The loading and referencing of this information
takes time.  Despite these drawbacks, the terminal driven billing
system where I worked in 1969 was data table driven (ISAM later VSAM
file).  Data entry to the tables was not rigorously checked.
Relatively little testing was done since we didn't have a simulation
or test system.  I suspect that many organizations today that use data
tables are not as careful with the changes they put in production by
table entry changes nor are they as well controlled through promotion
mechanisms as program changes.  I wonder how many fiascos have
occurred due to this and how many have never been known.

Performance, flexibility and control are all balancing acts.
```

#### ↳ Re: Performance and early/late binding

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-10-12T15:32:59+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7jfiquF35kb3bU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<hp42d513lj6kukfti4bgcnkvggoi3ljoth@4ax.com>`

```
Clark F Morris wrote:
> There are some very interesting trade-offs among time of binding,
> performance and maintainability.  The situation is not as cut and
…[52 more quoted lines elided]…
> Performance, flexibility and control are all balancing acts.

Thanks for an interesting post, Clark.

While I agree completely with your final statement, I don't share your 
reservations about late binding.

I have been using late bound OO components for over 15 years now and have 
had absolutely no problems with them, performance or otherwise.

Your statement about parameters needing to be recognised and processed is 
true, but the overheads in this are simply negligible. Certainly, I always 
keep parameter passing to a minimum (most of my components have ONE 
parameter or NONE), but I don't do this out of performance considerations; 
it is purely because I want interfaces to be as simple, consistent and 
unchanging as possible. Having interfaces that don't change means huge 
savings in maintenance.

Admittedly we are talking about different environments so zOS may do things 
differently from Windows. The flexibility that late binding gives in terms 
of system design far outweigh any performance implications as far as I'm 
concerned. I like to be able to look in the toybox and pull out the pieces I 
need when developing new apps. It would be disastrous if these were 
statically linked, with all the problems of versioning and maintenance that 
that entails. Not to mention the inherent inefficiency of duplicated code, 
which kind of defeats the purpose of having ONE copy that can be 
multi-instanced as required at runtime.

I really like late binding and it would take some very powerful arguments to 
persuade me away from it.

(Having said all of that, I agree that for batch processing (which I don't 
do; the systems I develop process everything on line and there is no 
requirement for Batch processing. Reports are handled by specific software 
designed for that purpose - StimulSoft and Crystal, usually...) static 
linking could make sense.)

Pete.
```

## Thread: Programmers — 13 msgs

### Programmers

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2009-10-15T12:39:02-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<r1red5l6glut2kfqn3u93fnoug7lri9npc@4ax.com>`

```
The old story where the wife tells her programmer husband:

"Go down to the shop and bring me a stick of butter. If they have
eggs, 
bring ten."

Sometime later, the husband comes home, puts ten sticks of butter on 
the table and says "They had eggs".
```

```
Nice joke. Try the following, and can anyone name all of the
languages?

The Evolution of a Programmer
High School/Jr.High
===================

10 PRINT "HELLO WORLD"
20 END


First year in College
=====================
program Hello(input, output)
begin
writeln('Hello World')
end.


Senior year in College
======================
(defun hello
(print
(cons 'Hello (list 'World))))



New professional
================
#include
void main(void)
{
char *message[] = {"Hello ", "World"};
int i;

for(i = 0; i < 2; ++i)
printf("%s", message[i]);
printf("\n");
}




Seasoned professional
=====================
#include
#include

class string
{
private:
int size;
char *ptr;

public:
string() : size(0), ptr(new char('\0')) {}

string(const string &s) : size(s.size)
{
ptr = new char[size + 1];
strcpy(ptr, s.ptr);
}

~string()
{
delete [] ptr;
}

friend ostream &operator <<(ostream &, const string &);
string &operator=(const char *);
};

ostream &operator<<(ostream &stream, const string &s)
{
return(stream << s.ptr);
}

string &string::operator=(const char *chrs)
{
if (this ]= &chrs)
{
delete [] ptr;
size = strlen(chrs);
ptr = new char[size + 1];
strcpy(ptr, chrs);
}
return(*this);
}

int main()
{
string str;

str = "Hello World";
cout << str << endl;
return(0);
}



Master Programmer
=================
Y
uuid(2573F8F4-CFEE-101A-9A9F-00AA00342820)
?
library LHello
{
// bring in the master library
importlib("actimp.tlb");
importlib("actexp.tlb");

// bring in my interfaces
#include "pshlo.idl"

[
uuid(2573F8F5-CFEE-101A-9A9F-00AA00342820)
]
cotype THello
{
interface IHello;
interface IPersistFile;
};
};


[
exe,
uuid(2573F890-CFEE-101A-9A9F-00AA00342820)
]
module CHelloLib
{

// some code related header files
importheader();
importheader();
importheader();
importheader("pshlo.h");
importheader("shlo.hxx");
importheader("mycls.hxx");

// needed typelibs
importlib("actimp.tlb");
importlib("actexp.tlb");
importlib("thlo.tlb");

Y
uuid(2573F891-CFEE-101A-9A9F-00AA00342820),>

#include "ipfix.hxx"

extern HANDLE hEvent;

class CHello : public CHelloBase
{
public:
IPFIX(CLSID_CHello);

CHello(IUnknown *pUnk);
~CHello();

HRESULT __stdcall PrintSz(LPWSTR pwszString);

aggregatable
?
coclass CHello
{
cotype THello;
};
};
private:

static int cObjRef;
};


#include
#include
#include
#include
#include "thlo.h"
#include "pshlo.h"
#include "shlo.hxx"
#include "mycls.hxx"

int CHello::cObjRef = 0;

CHello::CHello(IUnknown *pUnk) : CHelloBase(pUnk)
{
cObjRef++;
return;
}

HRESULT __stdcall CHello::PrintSz(LPWSTR pwszString)
{
printf("%ws\n", pwszString);
return(ResultFromScode(S_OK));
}


CHello::~CHello(void)
{

// when the object count goes to zero, stop the server
cObjRef--;
if( cObjRef == 0 )
PulseEvent(hEvent);

return;
}

#include
#include
#include "pshlo.h"
#include "shlo.hxx"
#include "mycls.hxx"

HANDLE hEvent;

int _cdecl main(
int argc,

char * argvY?
) {
ULONG ulRef;
DWORD dwRegistration;
CHelloCF *pCF = new CHelloCF();
hEvent = CreateEvent(NULL, FALSE, FALSE, NULL);

// Initialize the OLE libraries
CoInitializeEx(NULL, COINIT_MULTITHREADED);

CoRegisterClassObject(CLSID_CHello, pCF, CLSCTX_LOCAL_SERVER,
REGCLS_MULTIPLEUSE, &dwRegistration);

// wait on an event to stop
WaitForSingleObject(hEvent, INFINITE);

// revoke and release the class object
CoRevokeClassObject(dwRegistration);
ulRef = pCF->Release();

// Tell OLE we are going away.
CoUninitialize();

return(0); }


extern CLSID CLSID_CHello;
extern UUID LIBID_CHelloLib;

CLSID CLSID_CHello = { /* 2573F891-CFEE-101A-9A9F-00AA00342820 */
0x2573F891,
0xCFEE,
0x101A,
{ 0x9A, 0x9F, 0x00, 0xAA, 0x00, 0x34, 0x28, 0x20 }
};

UUID LIBID_CHelloLib = { /* 2573F890-CFEE-101A-9A9F-00AA00342820 */
0x2573F890,
0xCFEE,
0x101A,
{ 0x9A, 0x9F, 0x00, 0xAA, 0x00, 0x34, 0x28, 0x20 }
};

#include
#include
#include
#include
#include
#include "pshlo.h"
#include "shlo.hxx"
#include "clsid.h"

int _cdecl main(
int argc,
char * argvY?
) {
HRESULT hRslt;
IHello *pHello;
ULONG ulCnt;
IMoniker * pmk;

WCHAR wcsTY_MAX_PATH?;
WCHAR wcsPathY2 * _MAX_PATH?;

// get object path
wcsPathY0? = '\0';
wcsTY0? = '\0';
if( argc > 1) {
mbstowcs(wcsPath, argvY1?, strlen(argvY1?) + 1);
wcsupr(wcsPath);
}
else {
fprintf(stderr, "Object path must be specified\n");
return(1);
}

// get print string
if(argc > 2)
mbstowcs(wcsT, argvY2?, strlen(argvY2?) + 1);
else
wcscpy(wcsT, L"Hello World");

printf("Linking to object %ws\n", wcsPath);
printf("Text String %ws\n", wcsT);

// Initialize the OLE libraries

hRslt = CoInitializeEx(NULL, COINIT_MULTITHREADED);

if(SUCCEEDED(hRslt)) {


hRslt = CreateFileMoniker(wcsPath, &pmk);
if(SUCCEEDED(hRslt))
hRslt = BindMoniker(pmk, 0, IID_IHello, (void **)&pHello);

if(SUCCEEDED(hRslt)) {

// print a string out
pHello->PrintSz(wcsT);
Sleep(2000);
ulCnt = pHello->Release();
}
else
printf("Failure to connect, status: %lx", hRslt);

// Tell OLE we are going away.
CoUninitialize();
}

return(0);
}



Apprentice Hacker
===================
#]/usr/local/bin/perl
$msg="Hello, world.\n";
if ($#ARGV >= 0) {
while(defined($arg=shift(@ARGV))) {
$outfilename = $arg;
open(FILE, ">" . $outfilename) !! die "Can't write $arg: $]\n";
print (FILE $msg);
close(FILE) !! die "Can't close $arg: $]\n";
}
} else {
print ($msg);
}
1;



Experienced Hacker
===================
#include
#define S "Hello, World\n"
main(){exit(printf(S) == strlen(S) ? 0 : 1);}



Seasoned Hacker
===================
% cc -o a.out ~/src/misc/hw/hw.c
% a.out



Guru Hacker
===================
% cat
Hello, world.
^D



New Manager
===================
10 PRINT "HELLO WORLD"
20 END


Middle Manager
===================
mail -s "Hello, world." bob@b12
Bob, could you please write me a program that prints "Hello,
world."?
I need it by tomorrow.
^D


Senior Manager
===================
% zmail jim
I need a "Hello, world." program by this afternoon.



Chief Executive
===================
% letter
letter: Command not found.
% mail
To: ^X ^F ^C
% help mail
help: Command not found.
% damn]
]: Event unrecognized
% logout
```

```
The old story where a programmer meets another programmer in the park:

> > > "Hey, you have a new bicycle?"

> > "Yeah, i have."

> "When did bought it?"

"Well, i didn't. Heh, that's weird story. Last time when i walked in the 
park some women jumped from her bicycle, she pulled off all of her 
clothes and said 'Take whatever you want!'".
```

```
>> > > "Hey, you have a new bicycle?"
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>clothes and said 'Take whatever you want!'".

I was in one of those cartoon desert islands, just me and a palm tree,
when a beautiful woman swims up in a wet suit.    She offered me a
cold beer, which she pulls out of her suit.   After I drink it, she
offered me a cigar, which I smoked with pleasure.    Then she asked me
if I wanted to play around.    So I exclaimed in surprise - you have
golf clubs in there?
```

```
In article <r1red5l6glut2kfqn3u93fnoug7lri9npc@4ax.com>, howard@brazee.net 
wrote:

>The old story where the wife tells her programmer husband:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>the table and says "They had eggs".

Bwah!

Years ago, with procedural languages, we used things like loops, function
calls, arrays, linked lists, queues and stacks. Now, in the 21st century
and with the advent of object-oriented programming, we've evolved to using
loops, function (sorry, method) calls, arrays, linked lists, queues and
stacks.
```

<a id="t-1accd61100"></a>
## Thread: Anybody using MF .Net Express Personal Edition? — 4 msgs

### Anybody using MF .Net Express Personal Edition?

- **From:** Roy Fenimore <rfeni@webspanning.net>
- **Date:** 2009-10-16T16:58:57-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<l2rhd5tuc4avg3hdmbrut4lrauqnlq8g14@4ax.com>`

```
Is anyone here (besides me) trying to learn COBOL using MicroFocus
.NET Express Personal Edition? It won't install because it refuses to
admit you have Visual Studio 2008 installed, even if you do. It's
driving me crazy! (Or maybe it only works with the commercial VS, and
not Visual Studio 2008 Express?)

Anybody have any ideas?

By the way, I'm assuming you are all geniuses and COBOL gurus, don't
disappoint me. I am but a blushing COBOL virgin, but am eager to learn
the ways of this, your world.

                                                Roy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Trends

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Ken <klshafer@att.net>
- **Date:** 2009-10-20T13:48:39-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<22c48c85-188c-4ebe-a8ce-7e2de48c7a68@m13g2000vbf.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<4aa9b2ef$0$23769$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <7gu756F2r3g6uU1@mid.individual.net> <oflka5tvaogoq8qh72kdmf71031v3cgj9f@4ax.com> <7h15c1F2rlac0U1@mid.individual.net> <h8h26g0273o@news5.newsguy.com> <7h2tijF2sbk6gU1@mid.individual.net> <h8r72i11d46@news2.newsguy.com> <4ab2ca18$0$23783$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net>`

```
On Sep 17, 7:45 pm, Paul <paul-nospamatall.rauler...@mac.com> wrote:
> On 2009-09-16 11:40:25 -0500, Michael Wojcik <mwoj...@newsguy.com> said:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Silly me. I thought that E-mail originated as a "store-and-forward"
application. That's why they called it "mail", right? Just like the
USPS still does today with their paper letters - collect them, store
them, sort them, and forward them.

Wasn't the first widespread "electronic transport" for E-mail the uucp
(unix-to-unix copy program, I believe it is) protocol? Didn't uucp pre-
date TCP/IP and Arpanet?

Store-and-forward seems to me to be much more closely aligned to
Michael's peer-to-peer than client/server. Who was the client and who
was the server on Usenet?

Remarkable that Michael is getting such resistance when the very Forum
that we are using has a Usenet legacy; Usenet discussion groups
likewise were originally store-and-forward.

For those old enuf or dumb enuf to care. :-)

And for those who bothered to notice I've been MIA...

Yeah, posters here are entirely missing the point on where the
software cycles are!  I've been watching satellite TV and working up
my home theatre system. Anybody else spent $3000-$5000 on video
elecronics lately? Just what _processors_ are in those components!
Sheesh! Surely enough to rival the auto electronics.

Seems peer-to-peer to me - oh wait! Maybe the satellite is the server,
and the DirecTV receiver is the client!  Or maybe the BluRay DVD
player is the server and the remote clicker is the client? Or is it X-
Windows type of client/server "inversion" whereby the TV is the
Display Server and the VHS player is the application client???

I'm soooooooo confused!

That's why I'm doing a computer paradigm shift to electronic
synthesizers and MIDI. Enough of ya'll arguing about what Antoine de
St. Exupery would aurely say are matters of no consequence.

Ken

P.S. Didja miss me DD? It is so comforting to me to read CLC after a
hiatus and see that the more things change the more they stay the
same.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Trends

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-10-21T11:35:39+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7k6s9rF38237vU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<4aa9b2ef$0$23769$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <7gu756F2r3g6uU1@mid.individual.net> <oflka5tvaogoq8qh72kdmf71031v3cgj9f@4ax.com> <7h15c1F2rlac0U1@mid.individual.net> <h8h26g0273o@news5.newsguy.com> <7h2tijF2sbk6gU1@mid.individual.net> <h8r72i11d46@news2.newsguy.com> <4ab2ca18$0$23783$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <22c48c85-188c-4ebe-a8ce-7e2de48c7a68@m13g2000vbf.googlegroups.com>`

```
Ken wrote:
> On Sep 17, 7:45 pm, Paul <paul-nospamatall.rauler...@mac.com> wrote:
>> On 2009-09-16 11:40:25 -0500, Michael Wojcik <mwoj...@newsguy.com>
>> said:
>>
<snip>
> Yeah, posters here are entirely missing the point on where the
> software cycles are!  I've been watching satellite TV and working up
> my home theatre system.

Yeah, me too. (Not satellite or SKY, though... no need. I get 139 channels 
from 32 countries directly off the internet. My SONY Vaio (soon to be 
retired, when it will become a full time entertainment centre, providing 
TiVO like facilities, connects directly on a HDMI port to my LCD TV.). The 
Vaio has an internal HDMI drive and an external BluRay (which I have used 
maybe 3 times...). Digital TV will be arriving in NZ in the next 5 years or 
so but you can buy satellite dishes and set top boxes for services like 
SKYand Freeview right now. I can't see the point if I can pull it off the 
internet for a very moderate one-time charge. (Less than the SKY or Freeview 
installation cost, never mind the monthly fees for SKY.)

I have a fourteen speaker Dolby 5.2 (very unobtrusive; most of my friends 
don't even know they are there) surround sound system,  which I built myself 
for a cost of $NZ100. It gives superb full surround for movies etc., 
however, I don't have remote control for it so it must be switched on 
manually. :-))


Anybody else spent $3000-$5000 on video
> elecronics lately?

My total replacement value, includng  the written down TV (it is 3 years old 
now) and Vaio driving it, is around $NZ2000 ($US1492.00  funny, that... :-)) 
on today's exchange rate.

Just what _processors_ are in those components!

Mine is dual core Intel  Centrino Duo :-)

I like to recycle old computers into other uses. I have a Windows 98 Pentium 
3 which drives my LAN printer and will become a PABX when I get the time... 
:-) It also handles NAS and all the LAN backups are driven through it. The 
people at Seagate (Malaysia) told me it was not possible to drive their 
drive with Windows 98; funny, it has been working perfectly for over a year 
now (I did have to do some driver "tweaking" when I set it up). I think we 
just discard stuff because we get blinded by what is "newer! better!" and 
forget that hardware still functions and has capabiliites it always had, no 
matter what the new software is doing. I do have some notebooks which are 
packed away in storage and it bugs me that I don't use them. I kind of 
justify it by thinking of them as "living archives" to a time when things 
were arguably simpler (although I have no desire to go back to 16 bit COBOL 
on DOS 3.2).

At least the Vaio (appropriately running XP Media Edition), which has served 
me very well for just over 3 years now, will serve a very useful full-time 
purpose when it is retired in February.

<snip>>

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT: Hi-Def TV / Radio (Was: COBOL Trends)

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Ken <klshafer@att.net>
- **Date:** 2009-10-21T09:57:41-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<505a1179-141c-4791-a419-29460bff9d1c@l35g2000vba.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<4aa9b2ef$0$23769$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <7gu756F2r3g6uU1@mid.individual.net> <oflka5tvaogoq8qh72kdmf71031v3cgj9f@4ax.com> <7h15c1F2rlac0U1@mid.individual.net> <h8h26g0273o@news5.newsguy.com> <7h2tijF2sbk6gU1@mid.individual.net> <h8r72i11d46@news2.newsguy.com> <4ab2ca18$0$23783$bbae4d71@news.suddenlink.net> <22c48c85-188c-4ebe-a8ce-7e2de48c7a68@m13g2000vbf.googlegroups.com> <7k6s9rF38237vU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Oct 20, 6:35 pm, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> Ken wrote:
> <snip>
> > Yeah, posters here are entirely missing the point on where the
> > software cycles are!  I've been watching satellite TV and working up
> > my home theatre system.

>
> I have a fourteen speaker Dolby 5.2 (very unobtrusive; most of my friends
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> manually. :-))

Speaker technology also has improved immensely over the years. I'm
using a Polk 7.1, and the six satellites are about the size of a pop
bottle and yet give a tremendous sound. You are right to mention your
Dolby 5.2... what is not obvious is just _how much_ sound adds to the
home theatre experience. I had the 42" TV for quite a while and
thought I had a pretty good thing. Now, same size TV, but add the
surround sound system, and it just comes _alive_!

I use a "video switching receiver" so I can switch among my components
(VCR, Blu-Ray, satellite, 2nd VCR/DVR, Cassette tape, CD player, CD
recorder, XM/MP3 player). "Legacy support" :-) is still important.
Yeah, I go with the newest interface (HDMI), but also still connect
the old composite (RCA-plug style) interface as "least common
denominator." Except for the copy-guarded content, I can play from
anything that plays and record to anything that records.

The feeds you get off the Internet? They're video? Can you see them
real-time streaming or do you have to store and playback?

I had an interesting (to me, anyway ;-)) discussion with a friend who
I showed my portable XM satellite radio and he poo-poo'd it claiming
the future of radio was Internet stations. Now here I am kinda on the
other side of the client/server vs. peer-to-peer/point-to-point
discussion. I prefer XM (some 200 or so radio channels) because of the
content a la the expertise of the DJ's and the specific channels.
That's worth the $13/month or so that I pay.

Technology-wise, yeah, Internet radio probably would do nearly the
same thing for me if I had a good wireless broadband with good
geogaphic coverage and a little Netbook in my car. But I'm not
confident I could get the content. (As an aside, XM recently actually
has Internet streaming capability for most of their channels and has
recently announced an app to run on the iTouch and iPhones. But you
still have to pay for the XM "service" to get the content.)

XM may not make sense for everybody, but I have a daily three-hour
commute, and I put about 40K miles on my vehicles yearly, so it's
especially good for long drives where you get no station "fade-out".

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Hi-Def TV / Radio (Was: COBOL Trends)

```
Ken wrote:
> On Oct 20, 6:35 pm, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> surround sound system, and it just comes _alive_!

Yeah, it is brilliant :-) I had some kids in to watch Godzilla a while back 
(just after I first put it together ) and the little ones like to lie on the 
floor. Of course, with surround on, every time the creature takes a step the 
whole house shakes and the floor vibration goes right through them. They 
loved it... :-)

>
> I use a "video switching receiver" so I can switch among my components
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> anything that plays and record to anything that records.

Well, functionality is what's important. Sounds like you have it well 
covered.


>
> The feeds you get off the Internet? They're video? Can you see them
> real-time streaming or do you have to store and playback?

Yes, these are digital video and they can be streamed or stored. More and 
more are appearing in hi def and I will be increasing my Broadband 
allocation to cover it. It is still much cheaper than SKY rental. I don't 
really use it a lot (mainly for important (to me..) sports events like NZ 
teams playing away, and the odd movie. I watch Science stuff  and I like to 
try and keep my language skills up by watching streamed news in German and 
Spanish, but overall, my TV time on this would be a total of around 12 hours 
a month. I am still experimenting with it as the Vaio is not retired yet and 
I use it for normal work. :-)
>
> I had an interesting (to me, anyway ;-)) discussion with a friend who
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> That's worth the $13/month or so that I pay.

I had not encountered XM until my last visit to the US a couple of years 
back. I rented a car to drive from Chatanooga to Atlanta and the lady kindly 
gave me a free upgrade which included XM in the new car. (I think she liked 
my accent... :-)) It was fantastic! I stayed on comedy channels and laughed 
all the way. I think it is superb. We probably won't get it here because we 
don't have the population to justify it, but, if I was living in America, I 
would certainly get it.


>
> Technology-wise, yeah, Internet radio probably would do nearly the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Yes, that makes sense.



> XM may not make sense for everybody, but I have a daily three-hour
> commute, and I put about 40K miles on my vehicles yearly, so it's
> especially good for long drives where you get no station "fade-out".
>

Exactly. I was very impressed; clear as a bell and no fade...

Pete.
```

```
me and everything ran on the server.

I later had my own server still using a dumb terminal (I still have an
ADM-3a somewhere around here) and used uucp to collect and distribute.
```

```
On 2009-10-20 15:48:39 -0500, Ken <klshafer@att.net> said:

> On Sep 17, 7:45ï¿½pm, Paul <paul-nospamatall.rauler...@mac.com> wrote:
>> On 2009-09-16 11:40:25 -0500, Michael Wojcik <mwoj...@newsguy.com> said:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> was the server on Usenet?

Actually, the first e-mail was sent between two PDP-10 computers over 
the very young
but very much existent ARPANET. Ray Tomlinson is usually accepted as 
having sent
the first e-mail when he worked at BBN, in 1970 or 1971 I think.  You 
can google it
easily enough and probably get a ton of references.

But besides that, email has always been transmitted server <---> 
server, and read
with a client.  Not sure what your point is.  Client programs usually 
accounted for
more processing cycles than the server transmissions. I'm sure there 
were exceptions.

I hate to point out that the entire network is based on a store and 
forward concept.
This router grabs a packet, decodes it, stores it in local memory, 
figures out what to
do with it, and sends it on along. In fact, switches act that way 
today, as they do a lot
of routing, especially between VLANS.   The "store" time may be very 
small, but it is there.


> 
> Remarkable that Michael is getting such resistance when the very Forum
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> I'm soooooooo confused!

<grin> All depends upon your point of view of course.

The video server on your digital TV is the client for the content 
server from your blue ray player, and a client for your digital video 
recorder, which is also the client for the cable broadcast network, 
except when it is acting as a server to provide reports back to the 
cable company, or an audio server to drive your amplifier, which is 
definitely serving your speakers.

Except when it is a client being controlled by your iPhone.

Simple eh?

-Paul
```

```
"""Email is much older than ARPANet or the Internet. It was never
invented; it evolved from very simple beginnings.

Early email was just a small advance on what we know these days as a
file directory - it just put a message in another user's directory in
a spot where they could see it when they logged in. Simple as that.
Just like leaving a note on someone's desk.

Probably the first email system of this type was MAILBOX, used at
Massachusetts Institute of Technology from 1965. Another early program
to send messages on the same computer was called SNDMSG."""

ARPANET was not a public system, even when they changed the protocols
from NCP to TCP it only slowly turned into the internet for general
use.

"""public access and hobbyist networking systems grew in popularity,
including unix-to-unix copy (UUCP) and FidoNet"""

So Michael was correct that the "first _WIDESPREAD_ "electronic
transport" for E-mail" was uucp.
```

```
On 2009-10-22 13:23:05 -0500, Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> said:

> On Oct 22, 6:11ï¿½pm, Paul <paul-nospamatall.rauler...@mac.com> wrote:
>> On 2009-10-20 15:48:39 -0500, Ken <klsha...@att.net> said:
…[58 more quoted lines elided]…
> transport" for E-mail" was uucp.


Are you talking about e-mail?  You know, the stuff defined in the dictionary
as:

a system for sending messages from one individual to another via 
telecommunications links between computers or terminals.


ARPANET was not open to general public, no, but I certainly had an 
arpanet account. So did a heck of a lot of college kids, and a lot of 
people in defense and aerospace. It got a heck of a lot of use. Bitnet 
was about the only other comparable system, and it was far more 
restricted.

The ARPA plan for ARPANet was submitted and approved in mid 1968 - 
about one year before we walked on the moon.

UUCP protocols usually required a map to find where they were going, as 
in "bang addresses" (which is why most of us from that time still call 
the "!" character a bang...) Note that in those days, the "internet" 
was small enough most people kept entire maps of the net on their host 
computers.  Bill Joy and DNS made all that irrelevant, and I think 
Tomlinson is credited with coming up with the at address style. (I may 
be wrong there.)

UUCP is a protocol, layered over a communications network. It was 
adapted for use with modems, which spread the popularity. But that 
didn't happen until the late 1970's. The foldest UUCP map I can 
remember would show wolfvax and ucbvax connected by at least 10 or 12 
connections. Most of them would have been UUCP over network, though the 
long hauls would have been over dialups. There were certainly 
connections to ARPANet, Berknet, and others on that map as well.

As late as 1984 and 1985, I was pulling e-mail and newsgroups to a 
small AT&T 3B2 computer in my home office, and allowing folks to dial 
in and use it from the local area. I believe my primary connection was 
to Rutgers at that time.

All this to point out that e-mail was in pretty good use before UUCP 
became the de-facto standard. It was not the the first widespread 
system, though it did become the most used, at least for a period.


> 
>> But besides that, email has always been transmitted server <--->
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> cycles.


Clients more long the lines of mail, pine, or emacs.


> 
> 
…[48 more quoted lines elided]…
>> -Paul
```

```
Call "PUT"

Anything beyond that is unnecessary as COBOL is self-documenting.
```

```
On Mon, 26 Oct 2009 14:45:22 -0500, "HeyBub" <heybub@NOSPAMgmail.com>
wrote:

>Well, admittedly, the Data Division is not self-documenting, but that can be 
>solved by the liberal application of COPY books. 

Very good.

I was thinking about CoBOL's self-documenting characteristic.

All code in any? language can be documented.    But the best
documentation is that which is up-to-date and trustworthy.    This is
pretty obvious, but how many shops guarantee that text documentation
is trustworthy?

If we programmers have to document anyway, it is preferable to have
documentation tied to the code.    Meaningful names doesn't take more
effort than comments.     Designing code to be read isn't that hard.

Of course, if documentation isn't our thing, we can write cryptic,
disposable code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Documenting COBOL WAS: Re: Flow chart generators

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-10-28T12:40:15+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7kpen1F3bc69qU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<185888c0-1e05-4f67-84e3-02f36bb0f266@b18g2000vbl.googlegroups.com> <Kc2dnbSEoO1fSn3XnZ2dnUVZ_tudnZ2d@earthlink.com> <7kf329F39disfU1@mid.individual.net> <P9ydnehPGIXEYXjXnZ2dnUVZ_v-dnZ2d@earthlink.com> <f50ee5t0nvl2ol7bas8tjok2v1b40k0m9k@4ax.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On Mon, 26 Oct 2009 14:45:22 -0500, "HeyBub" <heybub@NOSPAMgmail.com>
> wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> disposable code.

I like the fact that some languages have an "auto-document" feature (Java, 
C#) but I don't use it. :-) (Sorry, that should be "I haven't used it 
YET...I'm planning to have a go at this but it is way down a list of current 
priorities...)

If constructs are simple and variables are named in a meaningful way (names 
don't have to be long to be meaningful) then any professional programmer 
should be able to understand what is going on (This applies to most 
languages, including COBOL.)

I think the difference is in emphasis. With COBOL, everything is about 
source code, so the source code has to be easily understandable. It simply 
costs more if it isn't. With OO languages you may be using objects and 
components from third parties that you don't even HAVE the source code for, 
so the emphasis is on the functionality working as documented, rather than 
the source being well documented. I learned a long time ago to provide help 
files with components, not just for people I was passing components to, but 
for myself as well. Yes, you CAN use an Object Browser to reveal the secrets 
of an object component but this gets pretty tedious and these days I only do 
that as a last resort when I have an undocumented component. (It is 
increasingly common practice for components to document themselves and you 
can specifically ask them to show you this documentation...)

I had occasion to discuss this with a Client a few days ago. He is not from 
a COBOL background and he felt that the use of an asterisk in column 7 and 
comments then inserted as lines made the source LESS readable. He was 
talking about some COBOL source that is part of a system he is migrating for 
someone else.

I asked him if he had a problem with the extensive comments that the Toolset 
adds to transformed source (I deliberately made it generate comments so that 
programmers coming to do maintenance on Legacy COBOL AFTER migration, and 
not having been privy to the process, could understand what was happening.)

One of the options in Transformation allows you keep the original code in 
the program and the tool comments it out, or, you can simply have it removed 
and be left with the transformed code. (Most people opt for retaining it, at 
least until they come to "trust" the transformation tool...)

Surprisingly, he said he had no problem with comments generated by the 
Toolset. Further discussion revealed that he didn't mind the use of "*>" as 
this usually indents the comment to where it is needed or can appear on the 
same line as the construct it is commenting on. (The toolset uses "*>" for 
comments.)

So * in column 7 is not as useful as *>, at least to a non-COBOL eye. (I 
concede it is a sample of one, but it was interesting to me.)

It would seem that the LOOK of the comments has an effect on their 
usefulness.

When I first started writing COBOL there was a local standard that required 
a paragraph starting with "NOTE" at the beginning of each section.

The trouble is that many programmers are not wizards of English and don't 
like writing anything other than code... :-)

So you end up with : "This code accesses the Master file. Then it processes 
it. Then it closes it."

All of which is pretty easily gleaned from the procedure names and verbs 
used...

For myself, when writing COBOL I add comments to explain the concept of what 
is going on or why a particular approach was chosen, or pitfalls to be aware 
of when maintaining the code. The mechanics are (or should be...) clear from 
the code itself.

But overall, I much prefer the object approach where Overview/concepts, 
Methods, Properties, and Events are all documented but you don't need to 
read it unless you want to.

Pete.
```

```
document into the source code at the appropriate points and then

…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Documenting COBOL WAS: Re: Flow chart generators

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2009-10-28T12:46:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hc9efn$lip$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<185888c0-1e05-4f67-84e3-02f36bb0f266@b18g2000vbl.googlegroups.com> <P9ydnehPGIXEYXjXnZ2dnUVZ_v-dnZ2d@earthlink.com> <f50ee5t0nvl2ol7bas8tjok2v1b40k0m9k@4ax.com> <7kpen1F3bc69qU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
In article <7kpen1F3bc69qU1@mid.individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

[snip]

>I think the difference is in emphasis. With COBOL, everything is about 
>source code, so the source code has to be easily understandable. It simply 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>the source being well documented.

With all due respect to those who write and use such systems, Mr Dashwood, 
my first response to the above was 'Holy Ned... what set of competent 
auditors of an even moderately secured system would be satisfied by 
hearing 'We don't have the source code or anything else that would give us 
deeper knowledge of this part of the application; all we gotta do is plug 
in a value here and Something Else comes out there and we run with that'?'

(I use 'moderately secured' to refer to things like Stock-trading or 
Hospital-Admission/Treatment or Payroll systems, things which have aspects 
covered by Federal Law (eg the Health Insurance Portability and 
Accountability Act, or HIPAA) in the United States of America.)

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Documenting COBOL WAS: Re: Flow chart generators

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-10-29T12:52:34+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ks3q3F39lngrU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<185888c0-1e05-4f67-84e3-02f36bb0f266@b18g2000vbl.googlegroups.com> <P9ydnehPGIXEYXjXnZ2dnUVZ_v-dnZ2d@earthlink.com> <f50ee5t0nvl2ol7bas8tjok2v1b40k0m9k@4ax.com> <7kpen1F3bc69qU1@mid.individual.net> <hc9efn$lip$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article <7kpen1F3bc69qU1@mid.individual.net>,
> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> there and we run with that'?'

I understand your concern, Doc, and I shared it when I first embarked into 
using third party components. However the keyword here is "application" 
["...that would give us deeper knowledge of this part of the _application_ 
"]

You don't have source code for the IO housekeeping routines that transfer 
data to and from your COBOL program, yet you find this an acceptable risk. 
By the same token, I don't need source code for a third party Grid component 
or serial port server, I plug them into my application and they work as 
documented. If they don't (and, oddly enough, that has NEVER happened in 
over 10 years now), they would get unplugged pretty quickly and either 
replaced or referred to the supplier. Only as a last resort whould I st down 
and write this functionality myself.

I only have one lifetime. I cannot and do not want to be an expert on 
EVERYTHING, and I simply don't have time or inclination to re-invent wheels 
that someone spent a considerable amount of time inventing already. 
(Sometimes, components encapsulate specialist knowledge that I would need 
years to acquire, even if I wanted to.) Sooner or later we have to trust 
other people to do their job.

"Applications" are built by plugging components together. The system 
architect/designer has complete control of that and can ensure that audit 
trails for financially sensitive information (for example) are included. You 
don't have to have a brick factory in order to build a brick house...(But 
you might want to have a technical standard that the bricks must comply 
with...)

>
> (I use 'moderately secured' to refer to things like Stock-trading or
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I have seen component based implementations of two of these (Hospital and 
Payroll) and they complied with legal requirements just as they would if 
they weren't component based.

Pete.
```

```
ago, when I asked Mr Dashwood about loading tables with 60,000,000 rows)

[snip]

>> (I use 'moderately secured' to refer to things like Stock-trading or
>> Hospital-Admission/Treatment or Payroll systems, things which have
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

Are these systems you have written?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Documenting COBOL WAS: Re: Flow chart generators

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-10-30T23:23:15+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7kvt4jF39ambbU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<185888c0-1e05-4f67-84e3-02f36bb0f266@b18g2000vbl.googlegroups.com> <7kpen1F3bc69qU1@mid.individual.net> <hc9efn$lip$1@reader1.panix.com> <7ks3q3F39lngrU1@mid.individual.net> <hccdb7$vh$1@reader1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article <7ks3q3F39lngrU1@mid.individual.net>,
> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> auditor's question of 'How was this number generated?'

The answer to that is: "This number was generated by a componented designed 
specifically to accurately generate such numbers. Here is the documentation 
on HOW it does it and here are the logs, journals, and audit trails it 
produced while doing it." If it was written in-house, of course the source 
is also available, but that would be a last resort and I have never seen an 
auditor who was not satisfied with the above.

It isn't about accepting more uncertainty, it is about getting things done 
and not re-inventing wheels which may well have been designed and built by 
people much better qualified than yourself to design and build them.
>
>> By the same token, I don't need source code for a third party Grid
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 60,000,000 rows)

I have written (and worked on ) major systems for  4 household name Banks 
(even an American Bank) and 3 Insurance Companies. I am familiar with audit 
and security requirements and have even been  employed on more than one 
occasion to check security of systems in such organizations. I don't believe 
there is anything you can teach me about computer system audit requirements 
or security. (And I work alongside  local legal experts in different 
jurisdictions...)

There is nothing wrong or fraudulent about using certified components in 
system design. (Unless you are building a bent system, but you could do that 
just as easily in COBOL. In spite of the availability of source code I can 
think immediately of two system s I know where people got away with 
defrauding (for a limited time...) so having source is no guarantee of 
purity.

All the source visibility in the world won't protect you from things like 
the famous Equity Funding Insurance scam where policies were sold to 
imaginary people or real people who didn't know they had "bought" them. (And 
that was a mainframe COBOL system...)

>
> [snip]
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Are these systems you have written?

No. Absolutely not.

There are also components available for things like Tax calculations (a very 
legal requirement) which are endorsed  and certified by IRD (In UK and NZ). 
If you buy the component you don't get the source code. (Neither do you need 
it...)

But don't take my word for it... try a GOOGLE search on "software components 
for Payroll" then try it substituting "Hospital" and "Tax" in place of 
"Payroll".

If you buy a package like Siebel or SAP you don't get source code (although 
with SAP you may have "tailoring" in ABAPS).

If you manage your small business with MYOB, SAGE, or even Microsoft Money 
you don't get source code and yet your accounts are subject to audit just 
like anybody else's.

Source code (or at least, the maintenance of it) is nowhere near as 
important in the modern world as it was in the last century.

Objects doing what they should, and performing as specified, is still a 
matter of interest though...

Pete.
```

```
On Thu, 29 Oct 2009 12:52:34 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>I understand your concern, Doc, and I shared it when I first embarked into 
>using third party components. However the keyword here is "application" 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>and write this functionality myself.

My environment is somewhat different.  We have a major application
that gets updated periodically along with mods that aren't in the
base.    So a very large part of programmer's work is in code
comparisons and putting the mods back into the changed application.   

Fewer people and fewer hours are spent in applications development and
far more people and hours are spent in what users would consider
"black box" work.    This is counter to the computer room stereotype
that we thought we were moving away from.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Documenting COBOL WAS: Re: Flow chart generators

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2009-10-29T18:32:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hccn48$si7$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<185888c0-1e05-4f67-84e3-02f36bb0f266@b18g2000vbl.googlegroups.com> <hc9efn$lip$1@reader1.panix.com> <7ks3q3F39lngrU1@mid.individual.net> <d6lje5l92gf4ej8t5m0tqbkendrve8kkkh@4ax.com>`

```
In article <d6lje5l92gf4ej8t5m0tqbkendrve8kkkh@4ax.com>,
Howard Brazee  <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

[snip]

>My environment is somewhat different.  We have a major application
>that gets updated periodically along with mods that aren't in the
>base.    So a very large part of programmer's work is in code
>comparisons and putting the mods back into the changed application.   

That sounds like 'We received an update and someone needs to change PROGA, 
PROGB and PROGC again'.  Changing the same programs over and over again 
is, in my experience, almost a sure way to guarantee programmer boredom, 
stagnation and job-hunting.

>
>Fewer people and fewer hours are spent in applications development and
>far more people and hours are spent in what users would consider
>"black box" work.    This is counter to the computer room stereotype
>that we thought we were moving away from.

If a 'black box' is the replacement for a 'glass house' then not much 
seems to have changed except how readily one can see inside.

Corner-Office Idiot: 'How come this costs so much?  I thought the New 
System was supposed to save us money!'

Tech Lead: 'The New System saves a lot of money on development.  It also 
requires more money to be spent on maintenance.'

Corner-Office Idiot: 'That's just not satisfactory... maybe what we need 
is Yet Another New System.'

Applications Programmer: 'How will any New System take into account 
satisfying the needs our users have expressed?'

(both others, simultaneously): 'That's *your* job.'

DD
```

- **References:** `<185888c0-1e05-4f67-84e3-02f36bb0f266@b18g2000vbl.googlegroups.com> <Kc2dnbSEoO1fSn3XnZ2dnUVZ_tudnZ2d@earthlink.com> <7kf329F39disfU1@mid.individual.net> <P9ydnehPGIXEYXjXnZ2dnUVZ_v-dnZ2d@earthlink.com> <f50ee5t0nvl2ol7bas8tjok2v1b40k0m9k@4ax.com> <7kpen1F3bc69qU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Wed, 28 Oct 2009 12:40:15 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>So you end up with : "This code accesses the Master file. Then it processes 
>it. Then it closes it."
>
>All of which is pretty easily gleaned from the procedure names and verbs 
>used...

Way, way too common, in my experience.

>For myself, when writing COBOL I add comments to explain the concept of what 
>is going on or why a particular approach was chosen, or pitfalls to be aware 
>of when maintaining the code. The mechanics are (or should be...) clear from 
>the code itself.

The most useful comments I have seen are the comments that tell us why
a particular approach was taken and which user put that requirement
in.    Stuff that would be in the requirements, if we could find them
(and if they were updated when the writer actually got the details
about what was wanted).     
```

<a id="t-0ce5781fbc"></a>
## Thread: COBOL syntax — 38 msgs

### COBOL syntax

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-10-23T14:30:53+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7kcfafF39jt77U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
I got bitten a few days ago by COPY statements in column 8 for a COBOL 
program.

The Transformation Tool in the Migration Toolset has to process legacy COBOL 
source, and this is where the problem occurred.

This is in very ancient COBOL (some of the programs are dated 1982) but I 
know that many sites use COPY currently.

My Migration Toolset COBOL  parser uses reserved words as part of the 
parsing and doesn't expect to find reserved words (other than COBOL Headers) 
in the A margin.

(This is NOT the parser that is incorporated into the cobdata tool; that one 
doesn't use reserved words. See:
http://primacomputing.co.nz/cobol21/newcobdata.aspx )

On checking the Fujitsu implementation (based on the COBOL '85 standard), I 
found that COPY in the A margin is correct. The Migration Toolset has been 
amended to accommodate it, but it irritated me, for reasons I shall now 
explain...

This, to me seems completely inconsistent.

A COBOL parser is expecting to find COBOL structure. (We are not talking 
free format COBOL here...) The A and B margins have specific purposes and 
COBOL verbs in the A margin are not supposed to happen (as far as I know). 
If you write MOVE in the A margin you get a syntax error. So how come COPY 
is OK?

What is the consensus on this? Sure, I know everyone uses COPY and has done 
for decades, and they write it in the A margin (on many sites), and it works 
fine, but it really is inconsistent, in my opinion.

Does anyone NOT write it in the A margin? (I have worked on sites where I'm 
pretty sure they didn't, but I'm not positive...it was a long time ago.)

Can anyone think of any other COBOL verbs that can legally appear in the A 
margin? (I'm not sure about EJECT...)

To me, COPY is an ancient anachronism that was helpful in the time when 
source was everything. If 6 programs all referenced the same file structure 
then there would be 6 COPYs of it. It was necessary to keep everything 
synchronized and it led to versioning nightmares that led to complex 
versioning software like PANVALET and ENDEVOR.

(If things are organized properly there is no need for this stuff. You have 
a test version; you have a production version, they are stored in separate 
libraries and work in different areas of the system.  (In my case they are 
separate virtual machines))

In component based systems there is only one definition that is needed, and 
that is in the interface. The interface itself is an object and it gets 
instantiated ("implemented" is the technical term...) into anything that 
needs it. There is only ever ONE copy and ONE version.

(I don't personally use COPY, even in COBOL,  as I don't like propagating 
source code and I have no requirement for it anyway; record definitions are 
incorporated into Data Access Layer objects where they occur just once and 
are generated by the Toolset. DAL objects manage table sets on a Relational 
database, but they can also manage flat files if necessary. Standard COBOL 
applications which use the DAL objects will have a record definition in them 
(this is populated and read by the DAL object, and is part of the interface 
to that object) and this is typically provided by COPY in legacy code. Once 
the legacy is converted to OO, there is no more need for it; application 
objects and DAL objects simply implement the SAME interface.)

(I've been telling you for years that OO reduces maintenance... :-))

OK, I know that for most people reading this the idea of NOT using COPY 
would be Heresy and Sacrilege but you'd need to use OO programming to 
understand it doesn't have to be the way it is. If you deal with objects 
(rather than source) the world becomes "different"...

I'd be interested to hear your opinions about this and I'd appreciate it if 
anyone can offer more examples of verbs in the A margin.

Thanks,

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL syntax

- **From:** Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-10-22T19:10:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<92eab328-d62a-4884-bb32-7941b167b38b@12g2000pri.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<7kcfafF39jt77U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Oct 23, 2:30 pm, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> I got bitten a few days ago by COPY statements in column 8 for a COBOL
> program.
…[78 more quoted lines elided]…
> "I used to write COBOL...now I can do anything."

COPY isn't a 'verb'. COPY and REPLACE are part of the library module.
A COPY can appear anywhere a character string may appear. It may be
that the COPY could appear in the code at the same indentation level
as the content. If the copyfile contained an FD or an 01 level then
these would normally appear in column 8 so the COPY being in column 8
would indicate that the content is at that level.

Does C# not have 'using' ?

using System;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Text;

Same thing.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL syntax

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-10-23T17:35:04+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7kcq3qF37qe7gU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<7kcfafF39jt77U1@mid.individual.net> <92eab328-d62a-4884-bb32-7941b167b38b@12g2000pri.googlegroups.com>`

```
Richard wrote:
> On Oct 23, 2:30 pm, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[112 more quoted lines elided]…
> COPY isn't a 'verb'. COPY and REPLACE are part of the library module.


OK, thanks for that. I was operating on the common definition of what 
constitutes verb, rather than the COBOL one. My bad.

> A COPY can appear anywhere a character string may appear. It may be
> that the COPY could appear in the code at the same indentation level
> as the content. If the copyfile contained an FD or an 01 level then
> these would normally appear in column 8 so the COPY being in column 8
> would indicate that the content is at that level.

Yes, that's a good point which I missed.Thanks.
>
> Does C# not have 'using' ?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Same thing.

Not at all.

Using, in C# is nothing like COPY in COBOL. It does not introduce source 
code into your program. It attaches a library, or collection for reference 
by the program. This is just a shortcut to save you having to write fully 
qualified paths to objects.

(And it is not required to start in a particular column)>

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL syntax

- **From:** Rene_Surop <infodynamics_ph@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2009-10-22T20:34:59-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ea4a726-022a-4cf6-9514-f87e087e6df2@s21g2000prm.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<7kcfafF39jt77U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
>
> (I don't personally use COPY, even in COBOL,  as I don't like propagating
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I guess COPY statement is precisely created for such purpose. It is
one of the strength of Cobol. Most company (including myself) uses
COPY statement to map data structures (SELECT & FDs) that was already
designed and placed as a data library. It is not meant for logical
codes, for logical codes we used INVOKEs or CALLs.

COPY reduces maintenance to data file organizations as well... because
we don't normally change previously predefined data structures.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL syntax

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-10-23T18:11:42+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7kcs8fF2psvekU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<7kcfafF39jt77U1@mid.individual.net> <9ea4a726-022a-4cf6-9514-f87e087e6df2@s21g2000prm.googlegroups.com>`

```
Rene_Surop wrote:
>> (I don't personally use COPY, even in COBOL, as I don't like
>> propagating source code and I have no requirement for it anyway;
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> we don't normally change previously predefined data structures.

I think it is one of the weaknesses of COBOL, inasmuch as it propagates 
source code, and requires versioning. I KNOW "most companies use it" but 
that, in itself is neither here nor there. Many companies use many things 
that are not necessarily ideal...

The more source code there is, the more maintenance is required.

I wouldn't mind a dollar for every time I've had to fix production COBOL 
because someone changed a COPY book and failed to re-compile EVERYTHING that 
used it.

It isn't even like the program tells you it is using the wrong COPY book... 
you get a S0c7 (or equivalent data exception on other platforms) because the 
data passed is not in the format expected, then you pore through a dump to 
find why the field doesn't contain what it should, then you realise the 
buffer is formatted incorrectly and next day someone pipes up, "Oh, yes, I 
changed that COPY book yesterday and I re-compiled, tested and promoted all 
the programs in the subsystem. It was fine."

"Did you not remember that data from that subsystem is passed to other 
subsystems?"

Embarrassed silence and shuffling of feet...

Systems like ENDEVOR are supposed to prevent this kind of scenario, and, 
generally, they do, but they are not foolproof.

I'd rather write a component that "objectifies" the data definition and let 
anything that wants that definition instantiate the component. There are a 
number of benefits in doing this, apart from the obvious one of only having 
ONE definition that does NOT require re-compilation of everything that uses 
it, every time it gets changed. For example...

If you use a COPY statement, EVERY program that includes that COPY statement 
will receive a definition of that data structure and EVERY field in it, 
whether it uses every field or not. If it is an object, you can reference 
ONLY the properties that are actually used. That is part of the advantage of 
using a Data Access Layer; programs don't have to include file IO; they can 
simply get and put fields to an object (which handles all of the necessary 
IO automatically.) Obviously, it is even better if you have a tool that 
writes these objects for you, and PRIMA customers get exactly that. If you 
move from flat files to databases, it has absolutely NO impact on the 
applications. Move from RDBs to CAS or LINQ or whatever new technology is 
announced next week and you can implement it WITHOUT  the slightest impact 
on your applications. Parallell processing for data? No problem. Functional 
lambdas representing queries? No problem.

COPY just ties you into the past. (However, if you haven't moved to OO you 
are tied into the past anyway, so I guess it doesn't matter... :-))

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL syntax

```
In article <7kcs8fF2psvekU1@mid.individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

[snip]

>I think it is one of the weaknesses of COBOL, inasmuch as it propagates 
>source code, and requires versioning. I KNOW "most companies use it" but 
>that, in itself is neither here nor there. Many companies use many things 
>that are not necessarily ideal...

To paraphrase my Sainted Paternal Grandmother - may she sleep with the 
angels! - 'Many companies jump off the Brooklyn Bridge so you will, too?'

What is seen as 'necessarily ideal' might depend on one's views of 
necessity and ideality... until then, the code's out there to deal with.

>
>The more source code there is, the more maintenance is required.

This seems to make as much sense as 'the less source code there is, the 
more New Version Releases are required'.

>
>I wouldn't mind a dollar for every time I've had to fix production COBOL 
>because someone changed a COPY book and failed to re-compile EVERYTHING that 
>used it.

You charged less than a dollar for that?  In my experience - limited as it 
may be - 'someone's' changing a copybook is ONLY done after it is 
determined what kind of effect it will have on other programs and how much 
testing is going to be needed to deal with the change.

To do otherwise invites... well, folks who charge a dollar to fix Prod, 
among other things.

>
>It isn't even like the program tells you it is using the wrong COPY book... 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>Embarrassed silence and shuffling of feet...

... and, in a necesarrily ideal company, a passing of an invoice to a 
different department or team or lead with 'you made the mess, you pay for 
cleaning it up'.

Perhaps looking at it from a different view might be enjoyable.  What 
reward would 'someone' incur from expending the energy necessary to 
remember stuff or Look Stuff Up?  What kind of company would encourage 
unrewarding activities?

The people I have seen have had a tendency to reflect their 
surroundings... at least to a certain extent.  Perhaps this is a 
limitation of my experience, perhaps it is a reflection of 'birds of a 
feather flock together' or 'tell me whom you associate with and I'll tell 
you who you are' (a misremebering of a translation of a French aphorism 
from my Kollidj Daze which used the verb 'hante', a cognate of the English 
'haunt' (or so I recall)).

DD
```

```
It isn't always because there was deliberate negligence or laziness. 
Sometimes the tools are not in place to give the necessary information, 
sometimes (as in the instance quoted , which is taken from real life, 
BTW...) someone honestly thought they had done what was required, but they 
were mistaken. Sometimes accepted processes and practices are simply error 
prone. Having multiple copies of something is generally more error prone 
than having one copy of it,... generally... (having an unused backup copy or 
two is often a good thing...)

> surroundings... at least to a certain extent.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL syntax

- **From:** Fred Mobach <fred@mobach.nl>
- **Date:** 2009-10-23T11:28:59+02:00

```
Rene_Surop wrote:

> I guess COPY statement is precisely created for such purpose. It is
> one of the strength of Cobol. Most company (including myself) uses
> COPY statement to map data structures (SELECT & FDs) that was already
> designed and placed as a data library. It is not meant for logical
> codes, for logical codes we used INVOKEs or CALLs.

In my source code many COPY directives are in the PROCEDURE DIVISION,
never heard (or read) that it doesn't belong there.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL syntax

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-10-24T13:19:22+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7kevgcF3a1ijdU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<7kcfafF39jt77U1@mid.individual.net> <9ea4a726-022a-4cf6-9514-f87e087e6df2@s21g2000prm.googlegroups.com> <4ae1775b$0$275$14726298@news.sunsite.dk>`

```
Fred Mobach wrote:
> Rene_Surop wrote:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> never heard (or read) that it doesn't belong there.

Thanks for reminding us of that, Fred.

I can honestly say that I can't recall ever seeing COBOL code that used COPY 
in the PROCEDURE DIVISION, but it is certainly legal and you can bet that if 
something is legal SOMEBODY will use it... :-)

Your post made me check the Transformation tool and I am happy it would 
handle this OK. :-)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL syntax

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "tlmfru" <lacey@mts.net>
- **Date:** 2009-10-23T20:16:37-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hAsEm.1507$KA5.1499@newsfe12.iad>`
- **References:** `<7kcfafF39jt77U1@mid.individual.net> <9ea4a726-022a-4cf6-9514-f87e087e6df2@s21g2000prm.googlegroups.com> <4ae1775b$0$275$14726298@news.sunsite.dk> <7kevgcF3a1ijdU1@mid.individual.net>`

```

Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:7kevgcF3a1ijdU1@mid.individual.net...
> Fred Mobach wrote:
> >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> I can honestly say that I can't recall ever seeing COBOL code that used
COPY
> in the PROCEDURE DIVISION, but it is certainly legal and you can bet that
if
> something is legal SOMEBODY will use it... :-)
>
> Your post made me check the Transformation tool and I am happy it would
> handle this OK. :-)
>

Several places I worked at had a standard routine to set up standard
information that a program might or might not need - run date, processing
date, run time, program & jobstream name, program and build/link version #,
etc.  All of which were of course accessible by various CALL's or ACCEPT's
but it was rightly thought worthwhile to enforce one standard method (non-OO
meaning) to get them all and one standard set of names.  On very rare
occasions this required recompilation of many, many programs - when the
picture of an existing item changed.

PL.

PL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL syntax

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Fred Mobach <fred@mobach.nl>
- **Date:** 2009-10-24T13:03:22+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4ae304dd$0$281$14726298@news.sunsite.dk>`
- **References:** `<7kcfafF39jt77U1@mid.individual.net> <9ea4a726-022a-4cf6-9514-f87e087e6df2@s21g2000prm.googlegroups.com> <4ae1775b$0$275$14726298@news.sunsite.dk> <7kevgcF3a1ijdU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:

> Fred Mobach wrote:
>> Rene_Surop wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> can bet that if something is legal SOMEBODY will use it... :-)

Just one example for now. We supported a COBOL generator which (of
course) also generated the subroutines for I/O processing like
sequential and indexed-sequential files. The start of the procedure
division (and most of its source code) was :

000830 PROCEDURE DIVISION USING
000840              R000WORKAREA    RECORDAREA.
000850     COPY PDISAM REPLACING
000860         NAME-OF-FILE BY
000870         F900INVENTARISBESTAND200
000880         NAME-OF-RECORD BY
000890         R900INVENTARISBESTAND200
000900         NAME-OF-KEY BY
000910         900INVENTARISBESTAND200KEY
000920     .
000930 P00.
000940 EOS. EXIT.
000950     COPY PDFILE.

No need to let the generator produce 700 lines of code instead of this
mere 13 lines. Per subroutine. In the time of removable hard disks, 14"
with 70 MB capacity. Space saving in source code worked very well. :-)

> Your post made me check the Transformation tool and I am happy it
> would handle this OK. :-)

Good job done.
```
