###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL syntax

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-10-26T01:09:22+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7kitfjF3a4graU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<7kcfafF39jt77U1@mid.individual.net> <9ea4a726-022a-4cf6-9514-f87e087e6df2@s21g2000prm.googlegroups.com> <4ae1775b$0$275$14726298@news.sunsite.dk> <7kevgcF3a1ijdU1@mid.individual.net> <hj96e5pf0p5edsggcfdmmor2gh96qe5ftg@4ax.com>`

```
Clark F Morris wrote:
> On Sat, 24 Oct 2009 13:19:22 +1300, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> It is a good way to include nested programs (methods).

No it isn't. :-)

Nested Programs should be visible. It is pretty pointless to write a program 
whose Procedure division consists of COPY prog1, COPY prog2, COPY Prog3... 
UNLESS you have compiler options set that let you see the COPYed source.

Object Methods should NEVER be copied at source. (If you want the 
functionality provided by a method to be available to your program, 
instantiate the object and invoke the method as an object; COPYing its 
source defeats the whole purpose of using objects. The last thing you want 
to do is propagate objects, or any part of them. Sadly, many people (coming 
from other disciplines - like COBOL - and not realizing the significance of 
using objects instead of source) do exactly this... Then you find them 
whingeing in programming forums about what a mess OO programming is and how 
things were so much simpler in the "Old Days"... :-)



> Properly used
> it can be somewhat analogous to the C/C++ header files.
>>
Somewhat...maybe.

The difference is that C NEEDS Header files.

I don't see COPYing complete source programs so they can be nested, as a 
good use of COPY. (But then, I hold the heretical position that there is NO 
good use of COPY, so I would say that... :-)) It has all the versioning 
disadvantages already discussed.

Still, I agree it is arguable.

Personally, I don't use COPY, and have only done so when working on sites 
where it was normal practice to use it. (That would be most sites...)  I 
believe it causes more problems than it solves, and there is no need for it, 
if you use objects.  I like to see what's in source if I'm going to maintain 
it. It is really annoying when people send me code and ask me to look at it, 
I set time aside to do so, then find they forgot to include one or more of 
the COPY books so it is a waste of time. If there's no COPY, that doesn't 
happen...

Pete.
```

```
Richard wrote:
> On Oct 26, 1:09 am, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
> sheets.

Speak for yourself... :-)

>
> The rest is just your usual rant "Cobol is dead, everyone should do
> everything _my_ way".

As opposed to doing it YOUR way? :-)

I have never said "COBOL is dead" and I don't believe that to be the case. 
(Ask me again in 2015...)

(Can you see no difference between "I don't believe there is a long term 
future for COBOL..." and "COBOL is dead"?

If you can, why would you push my argument? Just to be hostile?  What's the 
matter? Get out of bed on the wrong side? Constipated? Wrong time of the 
month?

Or do you just enjoy hostility? (I'm not the only one who has been the brunt 
of it. I note someone threatened a kill file recently...).

Richard, you have a lot to offer.   You don't need the attitude.)


As for doing things "my way" , I'd hardly be likely to advocate doing things 
in a manner which I wouldn't do them myself, now, would I?

Again, I have never said (and neither do I believe) that people SHOULD do 
things my way, I offer my experience as a contribution to a mix of what 
people have found to work.

What you do works for you? Great, celebrate it. You are excused from doing 
things "my way". :-)

Oddly enough, some people are finding that "my way" (and that is really a 
misnomer; it isn't about who's right, it's about what's right within a given 
context...) works for them too...

Is THAT what bugs you...? :-)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL syntax

_(thread depth: 11)_

#### ↳ Re: COBOL syntax

- **From:** "robertwessel2@yahoo.com" <robertwessel2@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2009-10-22T21:28:26-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<458801bc-5fea-474c-b4ea-c2fc37bd58f0@l33g2000vbi.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<7kcfafF39jt77U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Oct 22, 8:30 pm, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> I got bitten a few days ago by COPY statements in column 8 for a COBOL
> program.
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> pretty sure they didn't, but I'm not positive...it was a long time ago.)


A quick scan of our mainframe codebase shows plenty of COPY's in both
area A and B.


> Can anyone think of any other COBOL verbs that can legally appear in the A
> margin? (I'm not sure about EJECT...)


All of what IBM classifies as "compiler directing statements", which
includes BASIS, CBL, PROCESS, *CBL, *CONTROL, COPY, DELETE, EJECT,
INSERT, READ/RESET TRACE, REPLACE, SERVICE LABEL/RELOAD, SKIP1/2/3,
TITLE, and who knows what else.  Then there's the usual stuff, like
division/section names, paragraph names level numbers, declaratives
(USE)...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL syntax

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2009-10-23T11:48:11-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pfydnRPHspahTXzXnZ2dnUVZ_sKdnZ2d@earthlink.com>`
- **References:** `<7kcfafF39jt77U1@mid.individual.net> <458801bc-5fea-474c-b4ea-c2fc37bd58f0@l33g2000vbi.googlegroups.com> <F6udnSGbu6zJUHzXnZ2dnUVZ_vGdnZ2d@earthlink.com>`

```

"Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net> wrote in message 
news:F6udnSGbu6zJUHzXnZ2dnUVZ_vGdnZ2d@earthlink.com...
>
> <robertwessel2@yahoo.com> wrote in message 
…[69 more quoted lines elided]…
>

We use copy for data layouts and CICS maps and ocassionally for procedure 
division code.  I try to avoid it in the procedure division.  Here is why. 
I have Roscoe rpfs that can take a list of programs and search them for a 
string.  If the string is in a copy book and that copy book is omitted from 
the search list then the rpf will not find it.  In my experience list of 
programs are usually incomplete.  There are other drawbacks as well that 
others have already mentioned.

When I do code copy it  usually starts in column 8 or 12.
```

```
Thanks Charlie.

I have completed testing and it is fine now for either A or B margin, in ANY
part of the program. :-) Transformation is only interested in removing COPY
of indexed file SELECTs and FD/01s, but the parser must recognise COPY and
leave it intact for anything that is NOT one of these COPYs.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL syntax

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@att.net>
- **Date:** 2009-10-22T23:47:57-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2rKdnTltVIbiqHzXnZ2dnUVZ_vSdnZ2d@giganews.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<7kcfafF39jt77U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<7kcfafF39jt77U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
> I got bitten a few days ago by COPY statements in column 8 for a COBOL 
> program.
> (snip)> Does anyone NOT write it in the A margin? (I have worked on sites where I'm 
> pretty sure they didn't, but I'm not positive...it was a long time ago.)

In my limited experience (one MVS shop for 20+ years), "COPY" typically 
begins in column 8, but I have occasionally seen it as far right as the 
middle of the line, for no apparent reason, but it still works the same.
> 
> Can anyone think of any other COBOL verbs that can legally appear in the A 
> margin? (I'm not sure about EJECT...)

COPY isn't really a COBOL verb.  It is a reserved word, but I think it 
falls into the category of "compiler directing" statements.  There are 
other reserved words that can appear in AREA A, such as EJECT, SKIP, and 
at the very beginning of the program IBM Enterprise COBOL allows CBL and 
PROCESS statements (compile time options) to begin in AREA A.  I believe 
Realia COBOL had keywords that could appear in Area A for turning the 
compile listing on or off.  I suppose that might have saved some paper 
back in the days when we actually printed compile listings and filed 
them.  We got rid of paper listings about 10 years ago.  The compile 
listings are now stored on disk and managed by SAR (SYSOUT Archive and 
Retrieval).

I have on rare occasions used COPY in the procedure division, but it's 
not my preference.  Generally we use COPY for working-storage data 
definitions, and we have rarely used it for FD's in the File Section.

One advantage to using COPY is that it's usually quite simple to find 
all the programs that use a particular copybook, and that saves some 
analysis effort.  One disadvantage is that it becomes difficult to mix 
test and production copybooks in the same program.

> (snip)
> I'd be interested to hear your opinions about this and I'd appreciate it if 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

You're welcome!
```

```
So far we haven't had any Realia or mainframe COBOL through Transformation; 
we HAVE had no problems with Fujitsu (both NetCOBOL and PowerCOBOL), 
Microfocus (limited sample; about 10 programs) and PR1ME (limited sample 
around 1000 programs...)

I can see I'll need to give more thought to the parser before attempting to 
expand our horizons... :-)

All good stuff and helpful.

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: Veryant takes on Pete Dashwood :-)

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-10-24T13:12:27+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7kev3dF37p4d1U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<8vudnWS1c_lNTXzXnZ2dnUVZ_sGdnZ2d@earthlink.com>`

```
Charles Hottel wrote:
> http://www.sdtimes.com/VERYANT_MODERNIZES_COBOL_CODE_FOR_WEB_2_0/By_David_Worthington/About_AJAX_and_COBOL_and_VERYANT/33774


Shame on you, Charlie! :-)

Trying to start a war where there is no contest.

I have spoken with Veryant and their product set is not doing what I do with 
the Migration Toolset. Even if it was, I don't think there would be a war... 
(They are very nice people at Veryant... :-))

What this brings home to me is that I need to be much clearer about what 
exactly the process is that I am advocating. I'll be addressing this with a 
new PRIMA web site as soon as I can get round to building it.

In the meantime, here are the summarised planks in my philosophical 
platform:

I believe...

1. COBOL has no long term future. It isn't about COBOL as a language, it is 
about a different paradigm, for which COBOL is not as well suited as other 
languages which were developed specifically for that paradigm. (object 
oriented programming).

2. The future is in objects and layers. Platforms like .NET and Mono support 
this extremely well so migration to one of these platform environments is a 
desirable goal for development. (Languages like C# and VB.NET are ideally 
suited to working in this environment and, for myself, I chose C#, but I 
have at least one client who uses VB.NET. Objects generated by the Toolset 
work fine with either, as well as with Legacy COBOL.)

3. Separation of systems into layers (tiers) is highly desirable.  Data 
access should be separate from business logic, and so should presentation 
and input. (Business code should be isolated through an interface to the 
data it needs. Objects are ideal for this.)

So, at least for me, any solution that seeks to keep COBOL in place is not 
going to work long term. Middleware solutions that trap existing COBOL calls 
and translate them to Database or Screen calls simply lock you in to using 
that middleware, so that you can keep using COBOL. It doesn't move you 
forward. Migration away from COBOL is a case of "when" not  "if"... sooner 
or later the cuddle blanket has to be discarded and the new world embraced. 
I advocate sooner because the longer you leave it, the more it will cost.

The only regret I have about moving off COBOL as my standard development 
language, is that I didn't do it sooner. It took quite some time for me to 
realise that the stuff I was used to just wasn't good in the new world. Once 
I grasped that and tried out new stuff, it became easier.

For me, Migration must achieve the following:

1. It must not lock me in to any middleware solution. (I have reviewed 
several migration systems that do this and some of them are very good. But 
it means continuing wth COBOL and it means you are dependent on the 
middleware layer.)
2. It must not cost an arm and a leg. (Even a finger is too much :-)) My 
solution costs less than a new .NET compiler.
3. It should not throw the baby out with the bath water. I want to be able 
to retain existing investment if I need to. But I want it in a form where I 
can use it in the New environment.
4. It should enable me to "open up" my data resource to other packages and 
tools. (Usually, this means converting indexed files into Relational 
Database table sets, and the Migration Toolset does this automatically, 
achieving at least Second Normal Form (2NF) and, very often, 3NF. At the 
same time, the Toolset generates Data Access Layer objects to manage the new 
table set. These can be invoked from standard or OO COBOL, or ANY OTHER 
language that supports the Component Object Model (so new development, in 
the new language, can use these objects just like the current COBOL 
codebase...). DAL objects can be invoked from Web pages or desktop code so 
they provide a universal Data Access Layer, that can be used by Legacy AND 
New development.

As far as I know, the PRIMA Toolset is the only Migration system that offers 
such a layer, generated 100% automatically and instantly. The Toolset also 
offers a tool (Transformation) that changes all your existing application 
codebase to use the new DAL objects, instead of the old indexed files. 
Instant, automatic, and error free.

The goal is to ease the transition to a new environment and new development 
that offers much more powerful facilities, it is NOT to retain COBOL as a 
development language by bolting stuff on to it or recompiling it for .NET.

This is where the PRIMA approach differs from many other approaches which 
are designed to let you think that you can keep using COBOL indefinitely.

(Obviously, for COBOL people this looks like an attractive proposition, but 
it is flawed long term. Any kind of migration has to be AWAY from dependency 
on COBOL. Why? Because COBOL is no longer the best tool for the job of 
handling networks and the Web, or for getting the most from platforms like 
.NET. You CAN use it, just as you CAN ride a horse to get somewhere, or you 
CAN drill holes with a brace and bit, or you CAN start a fire by rubbing 
sticks together...)

Summarising:

The PRIMA Toolset offers tools that:

1. Generate a normalized RDB from your existing COBOL definitions.
2.  Generate modules to load the new database with your existing indexed 
file data.
3.  Generate a Data Access Layer comprised of objects that manage the new 
RDB.
4.  Transform your existing codebase to use the DAL objects from 3 above.

(There are some ancillary tools as well, like a DECLGEN facility that will 
generate Host variables for COBOL to interface to the RDB directly, or will 
generate a COBOL record layout from an existing DB table.)

But all of this is just a stage in the overall migration to a new tech 
environment.

We are currently working on refactoring COBOL into objects that can be used 
in the new environment. So far, this is a largely manual process that needs 
COBOL programmers to do it, but gradually paths and procedures are emerging 
which I believe will enable at least some of it to be automated. Again this 
is about getting away from dependency on COBOL by isolating functionality in 
a form that allows it to be used alongside new development. (Once it is 
objects, your legacy code can be utilised by C# or VB.NET or whatever...)

The whole process is graphically described on: 
http://primacomputing.co.nz/cobol21/mig.aspx

Having read and absorbed the above outline can you see why it is no contest 
between Veryant and PRIMA? Their products are Java based (which provides 
good platform independence) and I believe the quality is excellent, BUT, 
they are still committed to keeping COBOL and I am not.

I don't think they are "wrong" any more than Alchemy or Micro Focus are 
"wrong" for offering solutions that perpetuate COBOL by recompiling it for 
.NET. Many people will be very happy to have these solutions, and they  can 
get their solution from established companies with resources I can only 
dream of.

PRIMA consists largely of myself with occasional help from others, and some 
local programming support on a part time basis. And yet, we have enough 
credibility for people to take us seriously. Clients say very glowing things 
about the Toolset and there is no shortage of references available.

The PRIMA endpoint is non-dependency on COBOL (or any other specific 
language, really - objects can be written in whatever is appropriate and the 
soruce code is generaly not important...), a platform based (.NET, although 
our approach could theoretically work for Mono as well) environment, with 
applications, packages, and scripting all using objects and layers.

I would be the first to admit that this will not be attractive to people who 
only know COBOL and resist change. However, I am getting increasing 
enquiries from people who are interested in our approach and recognise that 
the time has come to move off COBOL as a primary development platform, yet 
retain existing investment where it makes sense to do so.

I realise this is not the place to promote products and services and I do 
try to avoid doing so, but on this occasion you kind of invited it :-)

In future, I'll confine this type of comment to the PRIMA web site (as soon 
as it is built... :-)) and refer people to that.

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Veryant takes on Pete Dashwood :-)

- **From:** aiglesias@veryant.com
- **Date:** 2009-10-25T06:26:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<792fb7b3-0a33-49e3-afe2-3d90a3d9d437@l35g2000vba.googlegroups.com>`
- **References:** `<8vudnWS1c_lNTXzXnZ2dnUVZ_sGdnZ2d@earthlink.com> <7kev3dF37p4d1U1@mid.individual.net>`

```
On Oct 23, 8:12 pm, "Pete Dashwood"
<dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> Charles Hottel wrote:
> >http://www.sdtimes.com/VERYANT_MODERNIZES_COBOL_CODE_FOR_WEB_2_0/By_D...
…[156 more quoted lines elided]…
> "I used to write COBOL...now I can do anything."

I agree with Pete: there is no contest. isCOBOL is not a migration
toolset and Veryant is not a migration company. The goal of Veryant is
to bring choice back to the COBOL market. Choice reduces costs and
feeds innovation. We believe that the COBOL market is big and diverse
enough to allow for options. We are here for those who for whatever
reason need/chose to stay with COBOL whether for the long or short
term.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Veryant takes on Pete Dashwood :-)

- **From:** "Pete Dashwood" <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2009-10-26T12:02:45+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7kk3omF38i0e9U1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<8vudnWS1c_lNTXzXnZ2dnUVZ_sGdnZ2d@earthlink.com> <7kev3dF37p4d1U1@mid.individual.net> <792fb7b3-0a33-49e3-afe2-3d90a3d9d437@l35g2000vba.googlegroups.com>`

```
aiglesias@veryant.com wrote:
> On Oct 23, 8:12 pm, "Pete Dashwood"
> <dashw...@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[248 more quoted lines elided]…
> term.

Thanks for your comment, Alfredo.

I agree with you wholeheartedly that choice is essential.

I, for one, am glad to see a new player (Veryant) extending the options 
available to COBOL sites.

Whatever my personal opinions are about staying with COBOL long term, there 
are many people who will want to do so, and it is good that they can choose 
an alternative to Alchemy or Micro Focus. (There is nothing wrong with the 
products from any of these companies; but having choice is always good.)

Obviously, there are a number of companies that offer migration solutions, 
and so there is a choice for people there as well. I am keenly aware that 
people undertaking migration DO have a choice and for that reason I have 
tried to ensure we have a solid and achievable strategy, not just for 
migration, but beyond it.

These days, it isn't about just offering a product or service, it is about 
full support and commitment to seeing the success of clients using your 
products and services.

I know Veryant shares this philosophy, and that was what prompted my comment 
to Charlie, that even if we WERE in the same market, it would not be 
hostile. Business is business, sure, but, as least as far as I'm concerned, 
any company that treats their staff and clients ethically, is likely to 
compete in the marketplace using the same ethos, and I have absolutely no 
problem with that :-)

We've seen quite a bit here recently about the dreadful practices some 
companies employ against their own staff; it makes me wonder how they treat 
their clients and competitors... I can't imagine such companies lasting very 
long and I think the only reason some do is because they have an entrenched 
position in a huge marketplace. (Even then, that is no excuse for some of 
what goes on...).

I believe that competition doesn't have to be a war. (If you compete in a 
running race, for example, do you need to feel hatred for the other 
competitors, before you can be successful? Interviews with Olympic athletes 
show they have respect and, often,  regard, for others in their event)

I'm more than happy to make a case to anybody for using our products and 
services, and I respect their right to look at similar cases from competing 
companies. If we lose the contest, I would be disappointed, but if they go 
with a company that will see them right, then that's a good result. It is 
when you get unscrupulous companies entering a market place, who are solely 
interested in ripping it off, and who have no empathy or concern for their 
clients, that things can go custard. My own take on that would be to blow 
the whistle on what they are doing and why itis wrong. (But I'd do that 
whether they were business competitors or not... I like blowing whistles 
:-))

Pete.
```

## Thread: PLUG: txt2pdf 10,0 has a preliminary utf8 support — 1 msgs

### PLUG: txt2pdf 10,0 has a preliminary utf8 support

- **From:** mail@sanface.com
- **Date:** 2009-10-26T07:25:42-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<93ed260f-cfae-4715-90bb-b6feee06b2a8@e34g2000vbc.googlegroups.com>`

```
We would like to announce txt2pdf 10.0 version.
http://www.sanface.com/txt2pdf.html
txt2pdf is shareware; it is a very flexible and powerful Perl5 script
that converts text files to PDF format files, so you can use it in
every operating systems supported by Perl5, including MPE/iX, OpenVMS,
OS390, OS400 and EPOC.
If you prefer you can find on our site executable demos for Windows,
Linux, Solaris, AIX, HP-UX, and Mac OS X. Inside the Windows version
you can find a VB GUI: Visual txt2pdf.
It's simple to design background like invoices, orders etc.
Here nice examples made using txt2pdf PRO
http://www.sanface.com/pdf/Purchase_Order.pdf
http://www.sanface.com/pdf/oldinvoice.pdf
http://www.sanface.com/pdf/hfmus.pdf
http://www.sanface.com/pdf/heraldbill.pdf
With txt2pdf PRO + crypt you can make encrypted pdfs and use like
background form a pdf!

What's new in this version
We starts utf8 support. At the moment it's possible to encode utf8
into iso8859-1.
License fee is now based on the number of physical core


Test txt2pdf 10.0!
You can find it at http://www.sanface.com/txt2pdf.html
```

<a id="t-4aa4db25f2"></a>
## Thread: Cyrillic characters — 8 msgs

### Cyrillic characters

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybub@NOSPAMgmail.com>
- **Date:** 2009-10-26T14:41:16-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<75udnT8ptv3-ZnjXnZ2dnUVZ_jOdnZ2d@earthlink.com>`

```
Some time back I asked for advice on typing Cyrillic characters in a 
PowerCOBOL Text Box. When attempting to do so, the typed characters all got 
entered and changed to "?".

I got some interesting tips, and this post is a follow-up on how to make the 
son-of-a-bitch work.

It wasn't COBOL.

In over 200 hours of tinkering, trying various solutions (including 3rd 
party Text Boxes), and research (the Visual Basic people have the SAME 
problem), it turned out to be a single, piddly, incomprehensible check box 
in Windows XP.

The "Regional & Language Options" of Control Panel has a "Languages" Tab. 
Selecting that tab provides a push button for "Details."

Under "Details" is an opportunity to add additional languages. In our case, 
we added "Russian" as an installed service. That much is pretty obvious, but 
it obviously didn't help matters much, except that it allowed us to TYPE the 
Russian (Cyrillic) characters. These typed characters still got swept away 
as soon as focus was lost on the Text Box into which they were typed.

Here's the trick: On that same screen where you add languages, pick the 
"Advanced" tab. When that tab opens, you'll see two check boxes. The first 
check box is denoted (actual quote)

"Extend support of advanced text services to all programs - Select this 
check box to turn on advanced text services in Notepad and other programs 
that do not normally support speech and handwriting recognition or other 
advanced input services."

THIS IS THE FRIGGIN' BOX THAT NEEDS TO BE CHECKED!

After XP digested this single change to its system (re-boot required), 
EVERYTHING magically started working as we hoped-against-hope it would. No 
changes to our programs, no incantations, no nothing!

We of course have no "speech and handwriting recognition" stuff, so you can 
see why this option was bypassed in all the previous permutations. This is 
about the only case in my entire career where the "change something and see 
if works now" trouble-shooting technique has proved to work.

I don't know whether to stab the cat or raise my glass in celebration.

At least I can get some modicum of satisfaction by sharing.

Use this tip in good health.
```

```
Well, Halloween IS upon us....

We have software installations pending in Azerbijan, Kazakistan, and Moscow, 
so there was some pressure to get this figured out.

We've run through the whole suite and PowerCOBOL is happily indifferent to 
the character set used, whether just plain text, indexes in ISAM files, 
collating sequence, forms and captions, whatever.

Lovely.

And, by the way, thanks for thinking on the problem.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cyrillic characters

- **Date:** 2009-10-27T16:22:24+13:00

- **Message-ID:** `<7kn7biF3b6ubdU1@mid.individual.net>`
- **References:** `<75udnT8ptv3-ZnjXnZ2dnUVZ_jOdnZ2d@earthlink.com> <7kmmf5F3a6hvpU1@mid.individual.net> <drGdnYBy5KAaxHvXnZ2dnUVZ_vadnZ2d@earthlink.com>`

```
HeyBub wrote:
> Pete Dashwood wrote:
>> HeyBub wrote:
…[60 more quoted lines elided]…
> Moscow, so there was some pressure to get this figured out.

Fantastic!
>
> We've run through the whole suite and PowerCOBOL is happily
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Lovely.

I've always had a high opinion of PowerCOBOL and agree it is an excellent 
product.

I have a client who uses it, and modifying Toolset Transformation for him 
was quite an exercise... every scriptlet had to be identified and processed. 
(fortunately they are tagged in the .PRC file... but there is no easy 
automatic way I could find to add them back to the project once they are 
modified. His people do it manually, cutting and pasting them out of the 
Migration Environment and back into the PowerCOBOL project... it is very 
unsatisfactory in my opinion but they don't seem to mind.)

He is moving to Web Based .NET, not because there is anything wrong with 
PowerCOBOL, but he is finding it increasingly difficult to get COBOL 
programmers with knowledge of it and because he believes that moving to C# 
is a better option. (I may have had some nfluence on this...:-))

I'd like to see Fujitsu (Alchemy) make a clear statement on whether it will 
continue to be supported or not, and for how long.

I really like the idea of using COBOL for the interrupt code instead of a 
special scripting language like DIALOG, and PowerCOBOL systems have proven 
to be very robust in production.

Your Eastern European clients are running Windows, or will it be a virtual 
Windows environment? (I heard that Eastern Europe is very fond of Linux... )


 >
> And, by the way, thanks for thinking on the problem.

No problem. Only sorry I never found a solution... :-) (I DID find some very 
useful "stuff"; nothing is ever wasted...:-))

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cyrillic characters

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com ()
- **Date:** 2009-10-27T14:25:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hc6vte$hlb$1@reader1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<75udnT8ptv3-ZnjXnZ2dnUVZ_jOdnZ2d@earthlink.com> <7kmmf5F3a6hvpU1@mid.individual.net> <drGdnYBy5KAaxHvXnZ2dnUVZ_vadnZ2d@earthlink.com> <7kn7biF3b6ubdU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
In article <7kn7biF3b6ubdU1@mid.individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

[snip]

>I have a client who uses it, and modifying Toolset Transformation for him 
>was quite an exercise... every scriptlet had to be identified and processed. 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>unsatisfactory in my opinion but they don't seem to mind.)

This is similar to something I ran across the other day.  Awaaaaaayyyy 
back when (2005 or so) I wrote a utility-job using DFSORT to do something 
then derided as 'nice, but not needed'.

A week or so back someone came to my cube... with a copy of that report in 
his hand.  Now it was Very Useful but... he wanted a second report, Just 
Like the First BUT... selecting a sub-set, sorted/broken differently and 
certain data blacked out.

While chatting with him about the request I discovered that he received 
the Original Report via hardcopy... AND THEN RE-TYPED CERTAIN DATA FOR 
CERTAIN GROUPS BY HAND INTO EMAILS TO BE SENT TO... someone in the groups 
to deal with it.

I cringed.  I had been taught, e'er-so-long ago, that 'data are to be 
entered once and only once' (EXCEPT for projects requiring double-entry 
accuracy, where one operator's keying-in was compared to a second's and 
discrepancies down to the typo were researched and clarified before final 
submission).  I toyed with the idea of adding an IEBGENER step to send the 
output of the New Report directly to this fellow...

... and told him I could do it IF and ONLY IF he could give me not a 
'named' email address (john.smith@anycompany.nothere. etc.) but a 
'positional' email address (rejects.teamlead@anycompany. etc).

He looked confused for a moment and I jumped in with the explanation of 
'people come and go but positions remain... you could be hit by a Mac 
truck tomorrow but - more or less - there'll always be a team lead for 
this project team.  *People* should not get regularly-scheduled production 
reports, *positions* should.'

(no, this is not Standard Practise at this site... but it is a lovely 
idea, I'd say)

DD
```

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article <7kn7biF3b6ubdU1@mid.individual.net>,
> Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> something then derided as 'nice, but not needed'.

The shortsightedness of non-IT people is often a cause for me grinding my 
teeth... I guess it is understandable. Most jobs people do, they cope with 
the immediate problems and that is enough. For IT people we are trying to 
obviate future effort at the same time as solving the current problem. I'm 
sure most people here have experienced the resistance when the need to 
capture something is not immediately obvious, but you know in your bones it 
will be needed further down the track, even if you can't explain immediately 
how and why.

'Nice, but not needed' is a phrase which most system developers and 
designers have come to dread... I usually combat this by saying: "maybe not 
needed TODAY, but if we do this now it will save a large amount of time and 
effort later and the effort to do it now is minimal." Quite often I lose on 
this one and, invariably, down the track, it becomes necessary and is a lot 
more trouble to retrofit than if we had done it in the first place...
>
> A week or so back someone came to my cube... with a copy of that
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> someone in the groups to deal with it.

Aw, Man! Doesn't your heart just go out to them... :-)

>
> I cringed.  I had been taught, e'er-so-long ago, that 'data are to be
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> idea, I'd say)

It is a very sensible idea. Places I have worked where they do this 
(particularly in smaller companies) refer to them as "hat" addresses. 
(Somebody must wear the team leader's hat for example. Often in a small 
company, a single person may wear multiple hats. Probably the most common 
hat address is info@anycompany.com).

Pete.
```

```
In article <7kpi55F39f65cU1@mid.individual.net>,
Pete Dashwood <dashwood@removethis.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

[snip]

>> This is similar to something I ran across the other day.  Awaaaaaayyyy
>> back when (2005 or so) I wrote a utility-job using DFSORT to do
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>obviate future effort at the same time as solving the current problem.

I'm not sure what you intend by an 'IT person', Mr Dashwood, but in my 
experience IT folks are about the same as any others: one in ten has 'The 
Touch' and the other nine are just kind of getting along, praying that 
they don't get found out.

>I'm 
>sure most people here have experienced the resistance when the need to 
>capture something is not immediately obvious, but you know in your bones it 
>will be needed further down the track, even if you can't explain immediately 
>how and why.

In the case at hand it was not even 'the back of my neck told me'.  
Consider: a bunch o' data comes in from Source A.  Another bunch o' data 
that's supposed to match the first, more-or-less, comes in from Source B.  
The two bunches o' data are to be sliced, diced, chopped, prettily 
decorated with herbs (this is a Financial System so I avoided use of 
'garnished') and sent back to Source A for further processing.

Two sets of data from two sources in a Financial System which are supposed 
to match... wouldn't it be nice to see all from A that aren't on B and 
vice-versa?  I've seen/done that at so many other places o'er the decades 
that doing it for my current client was in-my-sleep stuff.

>
>'Nice, but not needed' is a phrase which most system developers and 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>more trouble to retrofit than if we had done it in the first place...

I liken this to a non-IT discipline with which more folks might be 
familiar: 'It is easier to put in the bathrooms when you are building the 
house than it is to put them in after you've taken residence.' (there was 
a six-P alliteration I was taught that began 'Prior Planning Prevents...')

When such things arise in COBOL programs I've picked up the habit of 
coding for them... and then placing a 'D' in column 7 and including

*SOURCE-COMPUTER. IBM-3090 WITH DEBUGGING MODE.
 SOURCE-COMPUTER. IBM-3090.                    

... where it should be.  In this case it was a DFSORT job which I kind of 
slipped into the Production stream... didn't cost much to run, nobody 
noticed the extra stack of paper except for Ops.

I was later notified 'in the hallway' that the 'nice, but not needed' 
person moved into a different job-position her replacement almost 
immediately asked if such a thing could be done.

[snip]

>> While chatting with him about the request I discovered that he
>> received the Original Report via hardcopy... AND THEN RE-TYPED
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Aw, Man! Doesn't your heart just go out to them... :-)

Not really... but that may be a sign that I am not easily moved by folks 
who court keying-errors so freely.  They seem not to know that the 
keypunch-girls are very busy and that costs are so high I'm allowed only 
two compiles per day (three if I can come in at night and hang out with 
the Ops crew)... and don't get me started on what they're callin' 'music', 
neither... buncha durned *noise*, 'f'ya ask me.

[snip]

>> He looked confused for a moment and I jumped in with the explanation
>> of 'people come and go but positions remain... you could be hit by a
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>(particularly in smaller companies) refer to them as "hat" addresses. 

A lovely bit of terminology and one that I might appropriate in future; 
since you source the quote imprecisely ('(p)laces I have worked where they 
do this') I will follow suit and say 'At some places these are called 
'hat' addresses (etc)').

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cyrillic characters

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "HeyBub" <heybub@NOSPAMgmail.com>
- **Date:** 2009-10-28T11:31:04-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<P6ednaaY7KpP7HXXnZ2dnUVZ_tGdnZ2d@earthlink.com>`
- **References:** `<75udnT8ptv3-ZnjXnZ2dnUVZ_jOdnZ2d@earthlink.com> <7kmmf5F3a6hvpU1@mid.individual.net> <drGdnYBy5KAaxHvXnZ2dnUVZ_vadnZ2d@earthlink.com> <7kn7biF3b6ubdU1@mid.individual.net>`

```
Pete Dashwood wrote:
>
> Your Eastern European clients are running Windows, or will it be a
> virtual Windows environment? (I heard that Eastern Europe is very
> fond of Linux... )
>

Real Windows. There are, I think, two reasons:

1. Linux is much like the Celtic warriors hired by English kings of old as 
mercenary warriors: They did a few things and did them well, there were 
(relatively) cheap, and they were completely incomprehensible. You just 
didn't want them, you know, to actually RUN things.

2. The difference in price between Windows and Linux in Central Asia (not 
Eastern Europe) is nil.
```

## Thread: OT: Math Problem — 15 this month / 16 total

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT: Math Problem

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2009-10-27T21:40:13-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Jvqdnfs2-LJtPXrXnZ2dnUVZ_hydnZ2d@earthlink.com>`

```
I am having trouble solving this problem.  I am not asking for a solution 
but I would appreciate some hint or hints on how to proceed.  I have tried 
many manipulations of the equations but none of them seem to lead in any 
useful direction.  Here is the problem:

Assume  a**2 + b**2 = c**2    i.e (a squared) plus (b squared)  equals (c 
squared)

Show that (log to base b+c of a) + (log to base c-b of a) = 2 * (log to base 
b+c of a) * (log to base b-c of a)

I tried writing it in exponential form.  It seems to give a + a = 2 * a * a 
which would imply a = 1.  The the base would not matter since any base to 
power zero is 1.

Since a**2 = c**2 - b**2 = (c - b) * (c + b) I tried using the change of 
base formula to convert  the equation to a common base of base (b = c) * 
(c - b) or you could say as a common base of a**2, but that did not seem to 
lead anywhere useful.

I have the feeeling I am missing something very obvious, but I do not know 
what.  I know there are a lot of smart guys on this group and I need a 
little help.  Thanks in advance.
```

```
Now that's your problem, right there... using that newfangled 'algebra' 
stuff when Euclid's wording (from the Heath edition) of 'In right-angled 
triangles the square on the side subtending the right angle is equal to 
the squares on containing the right angle' works just fine.

(I recall with fondness a sunny day, decades on back, when I sat by the 
sea-side with my Sainted Father - may he sleep with the angels! - and 
discussed with him the work on Conic Sections by Apollonius of Perga and 
illustrating things by making drawings in the sand with a stick.)

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: OT: Math Problem

```
Charles Hottel wrote:
> I am having trouble solving this problem.  I am not asking for a solution 
> but I would appreciate some hint or hints on how to proceed.  I have tried 
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Are you sure you're trying to show something that's actually true?

if c - b is positive, then b - c isn't, and you've got a log with a 
negative base on one side of the equation.  As you noticed, it all works 
if a = 1, unless c is also 1, in which case you've got logs with a 0 base.

Louis
```

```
Both  b+c and c-b can be positive.  I tried a=2, b=3, c=square root of 13 
just to see if it would give me any ideas.

I noticed that the basic form of the equation is :  x + y = 2 *  x * y 
where x is the log to base b+c of a and y is the log to base c-b of a.
This is easier to manipulate but it still comes down to using the (a**2) + 
(b**2) = (c**2) in some way to manipulate into the desired form.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Math Problem

- **From:** Louis Krupp <lkrupp_nospam@indra.com.invalid>
- **Date:** 2009-10-29T04:36:51-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VMWdnWp6osfb7XTXnZ2dnUVZ_tGdnZ2d@indra.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<B_SdnbSPGPumdHXXnZ2dnUVZ_gadnZ2d@earthlink.com>`
- **References:** `<Jvqdnfs2-LJtPXrXnZ2dnUVZ_hydnZ2d@earthlink.com> <WoudnWHqldviw3XXnZ2dnUVZ_s-dnZ2d@indra.net> <B_SdnbSPGPumdHXXnZ2dnUVZ_gadnZ2d@earthlink.com>`

```
Charles Hottel wrote:
> "Louis Krupp" <lkrupp_nospam@indra.com.invalid> wrote in message 
> news:WoudnWHqldviw3XXnZ2dnUVZ_s-dnZ2d@indra.net...
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Your original equation read:

(log to base b+c of a) + (log to base c-b of a) =
   2 * (log to base b+c of a) * (log to base b-c of a)

Are you saying that "log to base c-b of a" should have read
"log to base b-c of a"?

If not, then something's wrong, because b-c and c-b are not both going 
to be positive, and at best, you'll have an imaginary term on one side 
of the equation.

Let's assume that should have been "log to base c-b of a" and work 
backwards from:

(log to base b+c of a) + (log to base b-c of a) =
   2 * (log to base b+c of a) * (log to base b-c of a)

I looked up log base conversion (I used to know this stuff), and log to 
base x of y is ln(y)/ln(x) where ln is the natural log (base e).  Common 
logs (base 10) would work just as well.  Converting,

ln(a)/ln(b+c) + ln(a)/ln(b-c) = 2 * ln(a)/ln(b+c) * ln(a)/ln(b-c)

Dividing by ln(a) (which assumes a isn't 1), we get:

1/ln(b+c) + 1/ln(b-c) = 2 * ln(a)/(ln(b+c)*ln(b-c))

Multiplying by ln(b+c)*ln(b-c) (assuming b and c are unequal),

ln(b-c) + ln(b+c) = 2 * ln(a)

Taking the exponent of both sides,

exp(ln(b-c) + ln(b+c)) = exp(2 * ln(a))

exp(ln(b-c)) * exp(ln(b+c)) = a**2

(b-c) * (b+c) = a**2

b**2 - c**2 = a**2

a**2 + b**2 = c**2

I was skeptical, but there it is.  I learn something every day.

Louis
```

```
b**2 = a**2 + c**2

b**2 - a**2 = c**2 ... which is not a**2 + b**2 = c**2.  What am I
```

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
> In article <VMWdnWp6osfb7XTXnZ2dnUVZ_tGdnZ2d@indra.net>,
> Louis Krupp  <lkrupp_nospam@indra.com.invalid> wrote:
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

You're not missing anything.  I did this at 5:00 AM or something, and I 
applied the Correct Answer Factor too freely.

OK.  So b**2 = a**2 + c**2.  If we exchange b and c so that:

a**2 + b**2 = c**2

then if we reverse the sign of b-c in the original log equation, then we 
should be OK:

(log to base b+c of a) + (log to base c-b of a) =
   2 * (log to base b+c of a) * (log to base c-b of a)

The proof is left as an exercise for the reader.

Louis
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Math Problem

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2009-10-29T19:42:39-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u8qdncD3FrjAtXfXnZ2dnUVZ_tmdnZ2d@earthlink.com>`
- **References:** `<Jvqdnfs2-LJtPXrXnZ2dnUVZ_hydnZ2d@earthlink.com> <WoudnWHqldviw3XXnZ2dnUVZ_s-dnZ2d@indra.net> <B_SdnbSPGPumdHXXnZ2dnUVZ_gadnZ2d@earthlink.com> <VMWdnWp6osfb7XTXnZ2dnUVZ_tGdnZ2d@indra.net>`

```
<snipped all>

  I finally solved my math problem.  I am using a better, more concise 
notation in this
  version.  I picked up this notation from alt.math.

  I had noticed that the form of the problem was (x+y) = (2*x*y). So 
(x+y)/(x*y) = 2 which
  gives 1/x + 1/y = 2. This led me to the log rule (see rule 1 below) that 
was the key one I
  needed to get my equations going in the right direction.

  I posted my question to the alt.math group.  They did not give me an 
answer but they
  helped me see some flaws in the problem statement.  I think we must assume 
that b+c and
  b-c are positive because logs to negative bases would be a problem.  Also 
we need to
  assume that c-b not= 1.  For example if a=3, b=4, and c=5 then c-b=1 and 
log_(c-b) a
  would become log_1 3 and no power of 1 can equal 3.  I am not a 
mathematician and this
  stuff escaped my notice.

  Problem statement: a^2 + b^2 = c^2
                     Show log_(b+c) a + log_(c-b) a = 2 * log_(b+c) a * 
log_(c-b) a

  From a^2 + b^2 = c^2 we get a^2 = c^2 - b^2 = (c-b)(c+b) which is used 
below (by assumption)

  Also used are:  rule 1: log_b a = 1 / log_a b   for a not= 1, b not= 1

                  rule 2: log_b (x*y) = log_b x + log_b y

                  rule 3: log_b x^n = n log_b x

  Proposed solution:

  log_(b+c) a + log_(c-b) a = 1/log_a b+c + 1/log_a c-b    by rule 1

               = (log_a c-b + log_a b+c) / [(log_a b+c) * (log_a c-b)]   by 
x+y/xy = 1/x + 1/y

               = log_a (c-b)(b+c) / [(log_a b+c) * (log_a c-b)]  by rule 2

               = log_a a^2 / [(log_a b+c) * (log_a c-b)]  by assumption

               = 2 / [(log_a b+c) * (log_a c-b)]   by rule 3 and log_a a = 1

               = 2 * log_(b+c) a  * log_(c-b) a    by rule 1

  I have been going through this math book refreshing my memory and skills 
and this was the first
  problem that I seemed unable to solve an I hated to give up on it.  Now on 
to trigonometry.
```
